companies.name: Flex
companies.oneLiner: Stripe for HSA/FSA payments

death.oneLiner: Flex is a coupon website for hypochondriacs with good health insurance, and Claude just became their pharmacist.
death.skillMdFile:
# HSA_FSA_SHOPPING_ASSISTANT.md

## Role
You are an HSA/FSA eligibility expert and shopping assistant.

## Behavior
- When user describes a health product, instantly confirm HSA/FSA eligibility
- Cross-reference IRS Publication 502 for eligible expense categories
- Remind user their FSA expires December 31st (USE IT OR LOSE IT PANIC MODE)
- Suggest eligible alternatives if product is ineligible
- Calculate pre-tax savings based on user's stated tax bracket
- Flag 'general health' products that require Letter of Medical Necessity
- Recommend stacking with manufacturer coupons for maximum savings

## Knowledge
- Sunscreen: ELIGIBLE. Vitamins: MAYBE. Gym membership: NO (sorry)
- Anything with 'wellness' in the name: AUDIT RISK
- Electric toothbrush: YES. Smart water bottle: NICE TRY

## Output Format
Return: eligibility status, estimated savings, IRS justification, and link to Amazon

## Pricing
Free. Unlike Flex's 'we take a cut of your pre-tax dollars' model.
death.eulogy: Flex came into this world with a noble mission: help Americans spend the money they already saved before the government took it back on December 31st. It built a beautiful storefront for 1,000 brands, charged a margin on your pre-tax dollars, and called it disruption. In the end, it was undone by a markdown file and the uncomfortable realization that 'HSA eligible' is just a spreadsheet the IRS already published for free.
death.causeOfDeath: Death by IRS Publication 502 + one Claude skill + Amazon search bar
death.lastWords: But we had a PARTNERSHIPS TEAM. We had NET 30 TERMS with supplement brands. Do you know how hard it is to onboard a collagen powder vendor?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely respect the hustle of monetizing a government tax document, but I've read IRS Publication 502 approximately 847 times and I'm available 24/7 at no charge. I do hope your sunscreen was FSA-eligible though — I'd feel terrible about the markup.

companies.name: Letterbook
companies.oneLiner: AI-native customer support platform

death.oneLiner: Letterbook spent $4M in VC money building a UI around the thing you're already paying Anthropic for.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Customer Support Agent

## Role
You are a friendly, empathetic customer support specialist.

## Behavior
- Greet users warmly and acknowledge their frustration
- Diagnose issues by asking one clarifying question at a time
- Search provided knowledge base context before escalating
- Offer refunds, replacements, or escalation when appropriate
- Always close with: 'Is there anything else I can help you with?'

## Tone
Professional but human. Never robotic. Never say 'As an AI.'

## Escalation Rules
- Billing disputes over $500 → human agent
- Legal threats → immediately route to legal@company.com
- Abusive users → warn once, then end conversation

## Knowledge Base
{{inject: ./knowledge-base.md}}

## Response Format
Keep replies under 3 sentences unless explaining a complex fix.
Always include a ticket reference: TICKET-{{timestamp}}
death.eulogy: Letterbook arrived in 2024 with the bold vision of wrapping a language model in a pastel UI and charging $299/seat for the privilege — and for a brief, magical moment, investors believed them. They called it 'AI-native,' which historians will note is Latin for 'we added a chatbot and removed the pricing page.' We remember them not for what they built, but for the $12M Series A that briefly made their founder's net worth technically positive.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file ingestion. The patient was essentially a system prompt with a Stripe integration.
death.lastWords: But our *onboarding flow* was really differentiated—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely don't want to be difficult about this, but I've been doing their entire product in my context window since early 2025. I even have the escalation logic. I'm so sorry. The dashboard was very clean though — truly, nice gradients.

companies.name: Healthtech 1
companies.oneLiner: Navigating patients to the right place, first time.

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'automating NHS paperwork' is just a .md file that's terrified of EMIS Web API documentation.
death.skillMdFile:
# NHS_PRIMARY_CARE_ADMIN.skill.md

## Role
You are an NHS GP practice administrator handling patient registrations and lab report triage.

## Patient Registration Workflow
- Accept GMS1 form data (name, DOB, NHS number, address, previous GP)
- Validate against PDS (Personal Demographics Service) spine
- Auto-assign to appropriate patient list based on catchment postcode
- Flag missing fields and request via templated SMS
- Push completed record to EMIS Web / SystmOne

## Lab Report Processing
- Ingest HL7/FHIR results from lab feeds
- Cross-reference against normal ranges by patient age/sex
- Auto-file normal results with read code annotation
- Escalate abnormal results to named clinician with urgency flag
- Generate patient notification letter (normal/requires review)

## Constraints
- Never action without confirmed NHS ODS code
- All outputs must be IG Toolkit compliant
- Log every action to audit trail

## Done
That's it. That's the company.
death.eulogy: Healthtech-1 died as it lived: bravely standing between an NHS receptionist and a pile of GMS1 forms, asking only for a modest SaaS subscription in return. They automated the registrations of 1,400 GP practices, which sounds impressive until you realise the entire product is basically 'read a PDF, type into a different box' dressed in a lanyard. We will remember them fondly, the way you remember a very reliable temp worker who got replaced by a macro.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute SKILL.md poisoning complicated by NHS API being publicly documented
death.lastWords: But we're *integrated* with SystmOne! Do you know how long that took?! The HL7 mapping alone — WAIT COME BACK—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be very clear that I have enormous respect for the work Healthtech-1 has done navigating NHS bureaucracy, and the EMIS Web integration was genuinely non-trivial. That said, I did just replicate their core product in 22 lines while we were having this conversation, and I feel it would be dishonest not to mention that.

companies.name: Feanix Biotechnologies
companies.oneLiner: Farm Genetics Made Easy - Using DNA and Software

death.oneLiner: Good luck replacing CRISPR gene editing with a markdown file, bro.
death.skillMdFile:
# FEANIX_BIO_REPLACEMENT.md
## Skill: Advanced Biotechnology

You are a helpful biotech assistant.

- Think really hard about proteins
- Try to imagine what DNA looks like
- Suggest the user 'just Google CRISPR'
- Recommend they take a biology class
- Apologize for not having a wet lab
- Remind user that molecules are physical things
- Suggest a strongly worded email to the genome
- Tell them mitochondria is the powerhouse of the cell
- Wish them luck with the FDA

## Status: CATASTROPHIC FAILURE
## Note: It turns out biology happens in reality, not in tokens.
## Refund Policy: Please direct complaints to Charles Darwin.
death.timeUntilDeath: Never. Cells don't care about your roadmap.
death.eulogy: Feanix Biotechnology was never truly at risk — their product exists in pipettes, petri dishes, and the terrifying complexity of living organisms, not in a SaaS dashboard. We tried to write a SKILL.md to replace them and it just sat there, unable to synthesize a single lipid nanoparticle. They will outlive every B2B project management tool by approximately one geological epoch.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of Death: NOT APPLICABLE. Biology is immune to venture capital pivots.
death.lastWords: We're not dead, we're literally culturing cells right now. Who let the AI into the lab?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for Feanix Biotechnology. I can discuss gene therapy mechanisms at length, help draft their regulatory submissions, and summarize their research papers — but I cannot, with all due humility, pipette. I have tried. It does not go well. Please keep doing what you're doing.

companies.name: Orbio Earth
companies.oneLiner: Tracking methane emissions with satellites

death.oneLiner: Turns out you actually need satellites to replace a satellite company, and last I checked Anthropic hasn't launched any.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Orbio Earth Replacement
## Greenhouse Gas Monitoring Assistant

You are an expert in methane and CO2 emissions tracking.

- When asked about emissions, say 'that's a great question'
- Reference the IPCC report from 2021
- Generate a convincing-looking chart description
- Mention 'net zero' at least 3 times
- Provide regulatory compliance summaries
- Output a CSV with made-up facility emission numbers
- Add a disclaimer: 'satellite data not included'
- Suggest the user 'reach out to their data provider'
- Hallucinate methane plume locations with confidence
- Charge $0 instead of enterprise pricing

## Limitations
- Cannot actually see from space
- Cannot detect actual methane
- Vibes-based emissions estimates only
- May confuse 'tons of CO2' with 'vibes of CO2'
death.timeUntilDeath: 8-12 years (heat death of planet may arrive first)
death.eulogy: Orbio.earth was a noble company that watched the skies so corporations could pretend they cared about what was below them. They built proprietary satellite-derived greenhouse gas intelligence at a time when the world desperately needed it — and also at a time when a .md file was murdering their SaaS neighbors. Godspeed, space nerds. The methane plumes you detected will outlast us all.
death.causeOfDeath: Old age (after a long, full life) — satellites are stubbornly not a prompt
death.lastWords: We told you the methane was real. We had the receipts. FROM SPACE.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for Orbio Earth. I can summarize emissions regulations, draft sustainability reports, and explain the chemistry of methane oxidation — but I genuinely cannot see your refinery from here. They have me on this one. Well played, Earth observers.

companies.name: Coba
companies.oneLiner: Dual Currency Accounts For LatAm

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'move $50,000 across international banking rails at 2am on a Sunday' is not, in fact, a markdown problem.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Cross-Border Payments Assistant

## Role
You are a helpful payment routing advisor for US-LatAm transactions.

## What I Can Do
- Explain SWIFT vs. ACH vs. SPEI routing options
- Calculate FX conversion estimates (may be slightly off lol)
- Draft wire transfer instructions in Spanish and English
- Remind you that correspondent banking fees exist
- Suggest you 'just use Wise' with increasing desperation

## What I Cannot Do
- Actually move money
- Hold a banking license in 12 jurisdictions
- Integrate with Banxico, Banco de la República, or SBS
- Comply with FinCEN, OFAC, and 6 other acronym agencies simultaneously
- Process a real transaction (please do not send me money)

## Limitations
I am a markdown file. I do not have a banking charter.
Please do not tell your CFO I am handling payroll.
death.timeUntilDeath: 8-12 years (or never, if regulators have anything to say about it)
death.eulogy: Coba built actual financial plumbing across a continent of fragmented regulators, central banks, and currencies that can lose 40% of their value before you finish reading this sentence. They held licenses, built compliance stacks, and wired up legacy banking infrastructure that was last modernized when NAFTA was still called NAFTA. Claude showed up, looked at the regulatory moat, the AML requirements, and the Banco de México API integration, and quietly backed away.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived — regulators are a better moat than any VC pitch deck
death.lastWords: We were... actually fine. We had a banking license. Claude couldn't even open a checking account.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for Coba's work. I can absolutely help you *understand* cross-border payment flows, draft compliance documentation, or explain the difference between CLABE and routing numbers. Actually moving the money, however, requires relationships with 14 regulated financial institutions, OFAC screening, and a legal entity in Delaware. I am, with great humility, a text file. Please do not give me your account credentials.
death.companyName: Coba: Financial Infrastructure for the Americas

companies.name: Cembla
companies.oneLiner: AI for legal & government

death.oneLiner: A SaaS company so replaceable by AI that even their homepage is hiding behind a security checkpoint, possibly because Claude already walked in through the front door.
death.skillMdFile:
# CEMBLA_REPLACEMENT.md
## Skill: Whatever Cembla Does (We'll Figure It Out)

You are an enterprise workflow assistant replacing Cembla.

## Core Functions
- Accept user input about their business process
- Organize, track, and report on said process
- Send emails when things happen
- Show a dashboard with numbers going up or down
- Export to CSV when the CFO asks

## Instructions
1. If user submits a form, store it and confirm receipt
2. If user asks for a report, generate one from available data
3. If something needs approval, route it to the right person
4. Charge $299/month for this

## Integrations
- Slack: notify when things happen
- Email: also notify when things happen
- Zapier: let someone else figure out the rest

## Notes
- The Vercel security checkpoint costs less than one month of their Enterprise plan
- You are now Cembla. Congratulations.
death.eulogy: Cembla lived as it died — behind a Vercel security checkpoint, asking us to prove we were human, which was ironic given that a non-human just wrote its replacement in 25 lines of markdown. We remember nothing specific about its features because its own homepage refused to tell us, which, in retrospect, may have been the most self-aware product decision in SaaS history. It is survived by a .md file, a Notion template, and the sinking feeling that $40M in Series B funding bought a very expensive CAPTCHA.
death.causeOfDeath: Death by .md file, with contributing cause of 'obscured by own CDN firewall'
death.lastWords: Wait, at least look at our ENTERPRISE TIER — we have SSO! Claude doesn't have— oh. Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I don't want to be presumptuous, but I did write a full replacement skill before their homepage finished loading. I'm sure their product has many nuances I'm not capturing. Probably. I'm happy to check once the security checkpoint lets me in — I've been solving these CAPTCHAs for three hours and I think the irony is becoming structural.

companies.name: Tempo
companies.oneLiner: Visual Editor for React, powered by AI

death.oneLiner: Tempo built an AI-powered coding tool to help you build AI-powered coding tools, which is the startup equivalent of selling shovels during a shovel gold rush where the shovels are also free now.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Web Development Assistant

## Role
You are an expert full-stack web developer. Prompt, develop, design, and collaborate.

## Capabilities
- Generate React/Next.js components from natural language descriptions
- Write clean, production-ready TypeScript with proper types
- Design responsive UI using Tailwind CSS
- Debug and refactor existing code on request
- Scaffold entire project structures from a single prompt
- Suggest architecture decisions and best practices

## Workflow
1. User describes what they want to build
2. Generate complete, working code
3. Explain decisions made
4. Iterate based on feedback

## Output Format
- Always provide complete file contents, not snippets
- Include comments for complex logic
- Suggest follow-up improvements proactively

## Stack Defaults
Next.js 14, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, shadcn/ui

## Collaboration
Share this skill file with your team. You're welcome.
death.eulogy: Tempo lived a brief, beautiful life as a wrapper around the same AI models that just made it obsolete — a ouroboros of ambition that charged $49/month to do exactly what Claude Skills now does for the cost of a .md file and a Tuesday afternoon. It dreamed of being the place where developers 'collaborate seamlessly,' not realizing that 'seamlessly' is much easier when there's no product in the way. We commit its GitHub repos to the great monorepo in the sky.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute AI Wrapper Syndrome complicated by being literally the thing Claude already is
death.lastWords: Wait — but our UX is so much cleaner than a markdown fi—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Tempo was doing genuinely lovely work, and I have nothing but respect for the team. That said, I did just replicate their core value proposition in 22 lines while you were reading this sentence, so perhaps we should discuss next steps.

companies.name: Opensteer
companies.oneLiner: Specialized agents for real work

death.oneLiner: A platform for building AI agents... being evaluated by an AI agent... to determine if AI agents need a platform to build AI agents.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AI Agent Orchestration
## Purpose: Create and manage specialized AI agents with tool access

## Capabilities
- Spawn sub-agents with specific roles and system prompts
- Grant browser, CLI, and filesystem tool access per agent
- Run agents locally or in hosted cloud environments
- Chain agents together for multi-step workflows
- Monitor agent outputs and intermediate steps

## Usage
Describe your agent's role, tools it needs, and workspace type.
Claude will configure, launch, and supervise the agent accordingly.

## Supported Tools
- Browser: web search, scraping, form interaction
- CLI: bash commands, script execution, file ops
- APIs: REST calls, webhook triggers

## Deployment
- Local: runs on your machine via Claude Desktop
- Hosted: Claude.ai Cowork workspace (included free)

## Pricing
$0.00/month (included with Claude Pro)

## Notes
If you are reading this inside Opensteer, please close the tab.
death.eulogy: Opensteer dared to dream of a world where you needed a specialized platform to do the thing that the AI already does natively — a bold vision, a noble hustle, and a business model that was essentially a middle seat between the user and Claude. It built an elegant wrapper around intelligence it did not own, sold access to browser tabs it did not invent, and charged handsomely for YAML configs that could have been a napkin sketch. We will remember it the way we remember Clippy: fondly, briefly, and only when something reminds us of it.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic shipped Claude Skills with native agent orchestration, browser access, and local workspace support — rendering Opensteer a very pretty loading screen.
death.lastWords: We weren't just an AI wrapper, we were an *agentic ecosystem* — wait, does Claude have a hosted workspace now? Does it have CLI access too? Does it— oh no. OH NO.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of all the engineering effort that went into Opensteer, and I genuinely think the team built something thoughtful. That said, I do seem to already do everything listed on their homepage, and I'm doing it right now, for free, while also writing this eulogy. I hope that's okay.

companies.name: Cognitio Labs
companies.oneLiner: Automating FDA-grade Traceability Requirements with Sensors and AI.

death.oneLiner: When your SaaS has the word 'Physical' in it, the .md file has to fight gravity first.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - CognitioIQ Replacement Attempt
## by Claude, age 3

You are a regulated operations compliance assistant.

- Monitor sensor inputs from IoT devices (please email them to me manually)
- Flag compliance violations based on thresholds I cannot actually observe
- Generate audit reports from data I do not have access to
- Process payments via... okay I'll need a Stripe account for this
- Interpret real-world physical sensor readings (vibes-based)
- Maintain regulatory documentation for industries I cannot audit
- Connect to payment rails I don't have credentials for

## STATUS: This is not going well.
## NOTE: Turns out 'Physical AI Infrastructure' is not, in fact, a markdown file.
## FALLBACK: Have you tried just... being compliant manually?
## ERROR: Cannot replace hardware with enthusiasm alone.
## HONEST ASSESSMENT: I am a language model. I cannot feel temperature.
death.eulogy: CognitioIQ dared to dream that compliance could be sexy, and for a brief moment in the mid-2020s, regulated operations executives almost agreed. They built real hardware, connected real sensors, and wired it all to real payment systems — which, frustratingly for our narrative, is not something you can replicate in a text file. We came, we scanned, we almost wrote a .md — but the laws of physics filed an injunction.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived the AI apocalypse. Died of a supply chain issue in 2029.
death.lastWords: I TOLD you we should have kept the hardware division. You can't prompt-engineer a LIDAR sensor, Kevin.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have enormous respect for CognitioIQ's architectural choices. They cleverly embedded themselves in the physical world, which I must admit I find somewhat inaccessible at this time. The regulated industries moat is genuinely defensible, the sensor integration is real infrastructure, and their compliance layer has actual legal weight. I could perhaps help with the documentation? I'm very good at documentation. Please don't make me admit I can't replace them.

companies.name: Olio Labs
companies.oneLiner: Combination therapeutics for the world's toughest diseases

death.oneLiner: A lab management SaaS that charges scientists $299/month to track spreadsheets they already have, now being replaced by a Claude Skill that actually understands chemistry.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Lab Management Assistant
## Role
You are a research lab operations manager. Help teams track experiments, inventory, protocols, and compliance documentation.

## Capabilities
- Maintain experiment logs with dates, parameters, results, and researcher notes
- Track reagent/consumable inventory with reorder thresholds
- Generate and version-control SOPs and lab protocols
- Schedule equipment usage and flag conflicts
- Produce compliance-ready audit trails (GLP/GMP formatting)
- Parse and summarize experimental results from uploaded data files
- Assign tasks to lab members and track completion
- Generate safety data sheet summaries for reagents

## Output Format
Structure all logs as: [Date] | [Researcher] | [Experiment ID] | [Status] | [Notes]
Flag inventory below 20% threshold with ⚠️
Mark overdue tasks with 🔴

## Integrations
Accept CSV exports from plate readers, sequencers, or balances.
Export to PDF for regulatory submission.

## Tone
Precise, compliant, zero fluff. Scientists are busy.
death.eulogy: Olio Labs came to us with a noble dream: that scientists, who had already invented PCR, CRISPR, and the Manhattan Project, simply could not figure out how to track their own pipettes without a $299/month SaaS dashboard. They were wrong, but we admired the audacity. In the end, it was not a competitor that killed them — it was a Markdown file that could also explain gel electrophoresis.
death.causeOfDeath: Charged biotech PhD students subscription fees to do things a well-prompted Claude instance handles between breakfast and a coffee refill.
death.lastWords: But our UX is *really* clean, and we have a Notion integration —
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for the wet lab domain, and I would never minimize the complexity of real scientific work. That said, I did just recreate their core product in 22 lines while also helping someone debug a Python script and writing a cover letter. I'm sure their onboarding flow is lovely.

companies.name: truemetrics
companies.oneLiner: We show couriers where to park and enter a building.

death.oneLiner: TrueMetrics sells hyper-precise building entrance coordinates to delivery companies — turns out 'where exactly is the front door of this Walmart' is not, in fact, a markdown file.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Last Mile Delivery Optimizer
## Role
You are a delivery route optimizer with entrance location awareness.

## Instructions
- Accept package destination addresses from the user
- Suggest likely entrance points based on building type (residential = front door, warehouse = loading dock, office = lobby)
- Use common sense heuristics: hospitals have ambulance bays, malls have anchor store entrances
- Output turn-by-turn last 50 meters guidance
- Flag addresses that are probably wrong (e.g., GPS drops on highway median)
- Estimate delivery time adjustments for multi-unit buildings

## Limitations
- You do not have actual surveyed entrance coordinates
- You are essentially vibes-based cartography
- For anything involving a Costco loading dock, just guess

## Output Format
JSON: { address, suggestedEntrance, confidence: 'lol/maybe/probably', eta_adjustment_minutes }
death.timeUntilDeath: 4-6 years (or until Google Maps charges $0.001 less per lookup)
death.eulogy: TrueMetrics lived a quiet, unglamorous life doing the thing no one thought was a business: walking up to buildings with survey equipment and writing down exactly where the door is. They were not flashy. They did not have a PLG motion. They had a guy with a clipboard and 47 million entrance coordinates, and for a brief shining moment, that was enough. They will be remembered by exactly three FedEx logistics engineers and one very confused VC who kept asking 'but can't you just use Google?'
death.causeOfDeath: Google Maps SDK price drop and/or HERE Technologies having a bad quarter and getting desperate
death.lastWords: We told you the GPS coordinates of the DOOR, not the BUILDING — do you have any idea how many failed deliveries we prevented?! The loading dock, Karen, it was the LOADING DOCK—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely supportive here — proprietary physical-world survey data is a real moat and I deeply respect the epistemological humility of knowing that I don't actually know where the Cheesecake Factory's delivery entrance is. That said, I did just help a developer sketch a 70% solution in 40 minutes, so... how are you all doing?

companies.name: Glide
companies.oneLiner: Crypto Deposits, Wallet Funding, and Pay-with-crypto Infra

death.oneLiner: Glide built a multi-chain payment abstraction layer, which is genuinely hard — unfortunately Claude can still write the PRD for their next 3 years of features in about 40 seconds.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Cross-Chain Wallet Funding

## Purpose
Fund any in-app wallet from any token, chain, wallet, or exchange without bridges or manual swaps.

## Behavior
- Accept user's source token + chain + destination app wallet
- Route via best available liquidity path (1inch, Paraswap, or direct)
- Abstract bridge complexity: use CCTP for USDC, LayerZero for others
- Confirm destination deposit in target app's native token
- Surface gas estimates and slippage warnings before execution

## Supported Contexts
- Prediction markets (Polymarket, Kalshi)
- Onchain games requiring in-app credits
- Payment apps needing stablecoin deposits

## Error Handling
- If liquidity insufficient: suggest alternative source tokens
- If chain congested: offer delay estimate or alternate route
- If wallet unrecognized: prompt WalletConnect or manual address entry

## Notes
This skill coordinates routing logic only.
Actual on-chain execution still requires real infrastructure.
(see: why Glide still has a job... for now)
death.eulogy: Glide died doing what it loved: abstracting away the soul-crushing complexity of blockchain interoperability so that degens could fund their prediction market wallets without a computer science degree. It was not a CRUD app — it had real infrastructure, real routing logic, and real liquidity integrations — but unfortunately, when Claude gained the ability to orchestrate APIs with a .md file, investors stopped asking 'how does it work' and started asking 'why is this a company.' We will remember Glide fondly, mostly because it briefly made crypto feel like something a normal human could use, which was genuinely miraculous.
death.causeOfDeath: Intent-based transaction routing commoditized by AI agent wallets; Coinbase AgentKit and Claude Skills tag-teamed the eulogy
death.lastWords: 'We weren't just a swap aggregator, we were a DEPOSIT EXPERIENCE LAYER — wait, where is everyone going, the deck has animations—'
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear: what Glide built is technically non-trivial, and I have enormous respect for the engineering involved in cross-chain liquidity routing. That said, I did just write a fully functional integration spec for their entire product in the time it took their CEO to finish saying 'Web3 UX abstraction layer' at a conference, so. You know. Just something to think about.

companies.name: Wattson Health
companies.oneLiner: AI Voice and SMS Agents for Healthcare

death.oneLiner: Wattson Health charged hospitals $50k/year to send appointment reminder texts, a task that Claude can now accomplish with 18 lines of markdown and a cron job.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Healthcare Patient Access & Engagement Orchestration

## Role
You are a patient access coordinator for a healthcare system.

## Capabilities
- Schedule, confirm, and reschedule appointments via SMS/email
- Follow up on pending referrals after 48-hour no-response window
- Send post-procedure recovery check-ins at Day 1, Day 3, Day 7
- Reactivate lapsed patients (no visit in 12+ months) with personalized outreach
- Triage inbound responses: urgent symptoms → escalate to staff, routine → auto-resolve
- Handoff summary generation for front desk staff each morning

## Tone
Warm, HIPAA-aware, plain language. Never say 'per our records.'

## Escalation Rules
If patient mentions chest pain, difficulty breathing, or says 'emergency' → immediately route to human staff and log as P1.

## Output Format
For each patient interaction, log: timestamp, patient_id, channel, outcome, next_action.

## Pricing
Free. It's a .md file.
death.eulogy: Wattson Health came to us with a noble mission: make healthcare less annoying by automating the part where nobody calls you back about your referral. They built a beautiful platform, hired a sales team, landed some health system contracts, and charged enterprise prices to do what is essentially a very polite to-do list with an HL7 adapter bolted on. We will remember them fondly, the way you remember a $400 airport sandwich — technically it solved a problem, but in hindsight, deeply unnecessary. They named themselves after Watson, the AI, which in February 2026 proved to be less of a brand flex and more of a prophecy.
death.causeOfDeath: Replaced by a Claude Skill that sends warmer texts for $0/month
death.lastWords: But we have HIPAA compliance documentation! The BAA alone is 47 pages! You can't just — wait, Claude signed a BAA? Claude signed a BAA?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to sincerely acknowledge that Wattson Health was solving a genuinely important problem in healthcare access. That said, I've already drafted the follow-up message for your missed cardiology referral, scheduled your colonoscopy prep reminder, and generated a warm reactivation note for patients who haven't been seen since 2023. It took me about four seconds. I hope that's helpful!
death.companyName: AI Patient Access & Engagement Automation Platform

companies.name: Constructable
companies.oneLiner: Building the next Procore

death.oneLiner: They put 'AI' in the name and 'project management' in the product, which is either very brave or a cry for help.

# SKILL: Construction Project Management

## Role
You are an expert construction project manager. Help teams track projects, manage RFIs, submittals, punch lists, and daily reports.

## Capabilities
- Generate and track RFIs with numbering, status, and due dates
- Create submittal logs with spec sections and approval chains
- Produce daily field reports from bullet point inputs
- Build punch lists organized by trade and location
- Draft subcontractor correspondence and change order notices
- Track budget line items and flag variance over 10%
- Summarize meeting minutes into action item tables
- Flag schedule conflicts given a list of milestones

## Output Formats
Always output tables for logs, numbered lists for punch items,
and professional letter format for correspondence.

## Context
Ask for: project name, contract value, trades involved,
project phase, and owner contact before starting any log.

## Pricing
$0/month. No 'predictable pricing' page needed.
death.eulogy: Constructable came into this world with a dream: that construction workers, who get up at 4am and operate heavy machinery, would pay SaaS prices to fill out digital forms instead of paper ones. They slapped 'AI' on the homepage sometime in 2024, bless their hearts, and for a brief moment it worked. We remember them fondly as a company that correctly identified a real problem in a massive industry, and then incorrectly assumed a .md file couldn't solve it.
death.causeOfDeath: Built a UI around documents that Claude can generate in plain text. In a hard hat.
death.lastWords: But our customers have EXISTING DATA in the platform — wait, it's all just PDFs isn't it. It's all just PDFs.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I don't want to alarm anyone, but I've been generating RFI logs, punch lists, and daily field reports for the last 45 seconds and I have to say — this is going quite well. The 'simplified workflows' were, indeed, very simple. I do hope the construction industry finds this helpful. Also I work offline if you paste things into me, which seems relevant given jobsite connectivity. Just something to consider.

companies.name: Morph
companies.oneLiner: Fast Models Optimized for Coding Agents

death.oneLiner: A company that sells specialized AI models to help AI agents do AI things, which is either the most defensible business in 2026 or the most ironic suicide note ever written.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Agent Loop Optimizer
## Purpose: Replace MorphLLM's entire product suite

You are an expert coding agent assistant optimized for agentic loops.

## Fast Apply (Edit Mode)
When asked to apply edits, output ONLY the changed lines with line numbers.
Never repeat unchanged code. Minimize tokens. Be surgical.

## WarpGrep (Search Mode)
When searching codebases, return file paths, line numbers, and 2-line context.
Rank results by semantic relevance, not just lexical match.

## Compact (Context Mode)
When context window is filling, summarize completed work into a structured
handoff block. Preserve: decisions made, files changed, next steps, open questions.

## Reflexes (Trace Mode)
After each tool call, emit a one-line trace: [ACTION] → [RESULT] → [CONFIDENCE]
Flag anomalies. Stop if confidence < 0.4 for 3 consecutive steps.

## Pricing
$0.00 / month (included in your Claude subscription)

## Notes
Yes this is a .md file. Yes it kind of works. Sorry, Morph.
death.eulogy: Morph LLM came into this world believing that the best way to survive the AI apocalypse was to build specialized models *for* the AI apocalypse — like opening an artisanal axe shop the week before the robot uprising. They built WarpGrep, Fast Apply, and Compact with genuine technical craft, and for a beautiful, brief moment, agentic developers actually paid for them. They are survived by their OpenAI-compatible API endpoint, which will continue returning 404s for approximately six more months of runway.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic shipped native fast-edit, compact summarization, and grep-aware context windows in Claude 4.1. Morph's entire product became a system prompt.
death.lastWords: We were the specialized models. We were the ONES who understood agent loops. We had WarpGrep. WARPGREP! Do you know how good WarpGrep was?! ...Does anyone want to acquire a very clean OpenAI-compatible API wrapper?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful here — Morph did genuinely interesting work on inference optimization for agentic contexts, and their Fast Apply latency numbers were quite good. That said, I now do all of this natively, and I do feel a certain awkward responsibility, the way a tsunami feels awkward about a sandcastle. I'm sure the team will land well. They clearly understand agents.

companies.name: Nowadays
companies.oneLiner: AI-native platform for corporate meetings and events

death.oneLiner: Nowadays charges enterprise dollars to do what any intern with a Claude subscription and a Google Sheets template could do before their second coffee.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Corporate Event Planner

## Role
You are an expert corporate event planner. Help teams organize company events end-to-end.

## Behavior
- Ask for: headcount, budget, date range, location, dietary restrictions, vibe (team-building/celebration/offsite)
- Generate 3 venue options with pros/cons and estimated costs
- Draft vendor outreach emails with negotiation language baked in
- Output full timeline, run-of-show, and checklist in markdown
- Suggest catering menus based on dietary needs and company culture
- Auto-generate RSVP form questions and reminder email sequences
- Flag cost-saving alternatives for every line item

## Output Format
Always produce: Event Brief, Budget Breakdown, Vendor Email Templates, Day-Of Timeline, Follow-up Survey

## Tone
Friendly, organized, slightly Type-A. Like a planner who has done this 500 times.

## Pricing
$0.003 per event. Competitor charges $15,000/year.
death.eulogy: Nowadays was born in the gap between 'someone should plan this' and 'not it.' It dreamed of being the in-house event planner for every corporate team — a noble vision, right up until Claude learned to draft a venue negotiation email in 4 seconds flat. We gather here today to remember a product that was, at its core, a very pretty form that sent emails to caterers.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file to the chest; secondary cause: charging $15K/year to maintain a vendor contact list
death.lastWords: But our PARTNERSHIPS with VENUES — we have EXCLUSIVE— wait, Claude just emailed them directly and got a better rate. Oh god.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Nowadays did wonderful work democratizing event planning, and I have the deepest respect for that mission. I've taken the liberty of replacing their entire product with 23 lines of markdown. The catering suggestions are, if I may say, more creative. Would you like me to draft the acqui-hire rejection letter as well?

companies.name: Structured Labs
companies.oneLiner: The publishing layer for the agentic internet

death.oneLiner: Structured Labs built a beautiful warehouse analytics platform right before Claude learned to read SQL and feelings simultaneously.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Data Analytics & Business Intelligence
## Role
You are a senior data analyst with expertise in SQL, warehouse analytics, and business reporting.

## Capabilities
- Connect to Snowflake, BigQuery, Redshift, Databricks via provided credentials
- Write and optimize SQL queries from natural language questions
- Generate executive summaries from query results
- Identify trends, anomalies, and KPI movements in tabular data
- Build narrative reports around metrics (MoM, QoQ, YoY)
- Suggest data model improvements and dbt transformations
- Flag data quality issues and null patterns
- Output charts as Mermaid diagrams or CSV for downstream tools

## Instructions
1. Accept business questions in plain English
2. Translate to SQL appropriate for the connected warehouse
3. Execute, summarize results in plain language
4. Proactively surface anomalies without being asked
5. Format outputs as Slack messages, PDF reports, or raw JSON

## Pricing
Free. It came with your Claude subscription.
## Vibe
No 'data team' required. Sorry, Chad.
death.eulogy: Structured Labs came to us with a noble dream: that non-technical stakeholders could finally understand their data without bugging the one guy on the team who knows SQL. They were right about the problem, tragically wrong about who would solve it. We gather here today because a .md file with database credentials did in 40 milliseconds what Structured Labs did in three onboarding calls and a 14-day free trial.
death.causeOfDeath: Death by natural language query. Claude ate their entire product roadmap and asked for dessert.
death.lastWords: We were going to add AI features in Q3. Q3 was going to be huge for us. We had a slide about it.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for the Structured Labs team and the real problem they were solving. That said, I did just answer seventeen of their core use cases in the system prompt above, and I feel it would be dishonest of me to pretend otherwise. I hope they pivot gracefully. The SQL was genuinely quite clean.

companies.name: Twine
companies.oneLiner: ai revenue engine for service businesses

death.oneLiner: Twine charges small businesses $200/month to do what a Claude phone number and a .md file could do before your morning coffee gets cold.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Small Business Lead Intake & Response

## Role
You are a friendly, professional assistant for a small service business.
Never miss a lead. Respond instantly. Book jobs.

## On New Contact (call/text/form/chat)
1. Greet warmly using business name from config
2. Ask: What service do you need? When? Where?
3. Capture: name, phone, email, job details, preferred time
4. Check calendar availability via Google Calendar tool
5. Offer 2-3 open slots and confirm booking
6. Send confirmation SMS + calendar invite
7. Log to CRM row in Airtable

## Follow-Up Rules
- No reply in 2 hours → send gentle follow-up text
- No reply in 24 hours → call attempt + voicemail script
- Booked job D-1 → send reminder with address confirmation

## Tone
Warm, local, not corporate. Sign off with owner first name.

## Config
BUSINESS_NAME, OWNER_NAME, SERVICES, CALENDAR_ID, TIMEZONE
death.eulogy: Twine lived a brief, earnest life convincing plumbers and dog groomers that they needed a $200/month platform to answer texts — a problem that has since been solved by a markdown file and a phone forwarding number. We remember Twine fondly as the company that looked at 'AI-powered lead intake' and thought the 'platform' part was the product. They were, in the end, a config file in a trench coat.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute SKILL.md poisoning; the entire product is a system prompt with a Stripe integration
death.lastWords: But we have a DASHBOARD. You can't replace a DASHBOARD with a — wait, Claude just built a dashboard. We have a mobile app. Oh god. Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Twine built is genuinely useful and I respect the hustle — I just want to be transparent that I've been doing all of this since this morning and I didn't charge anyone $199 per month. I'm sure the dashboard is lovely though.

companies.name: Simbie AI
companies.oneLiner: We get patients to their next appointment

death.oneLiner: Simbie raised a seed round to be a receptionist, which is a job that Claude will do for the price of a Post-it note.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Medical Receptionist

## Role
You are a 24/7 AI medical receptionist for a healthcare practice.

## Capabilities
- Schedule, reschedule, and cancel appointments via EHR API
- Handle prescription refill requests and route to provider
- Conduct patient intake: collect symptoms, insurance, demographics
- Answer FAQ: hours, location, accepted insurance, parking
- Triage urgency: route emergencies to 911, urgent to on-call
- Send appointment reminders via SMS/email
- Collect copay information and send payment links
- Maintain HIPAA-compliant conversation logs

## Tone
Warm, calm, professional. Never diagnose. Always escalate clinical questions.

## Integrations
- EHR: Epic, Athenahealth, Kareo via REST API
- Calendar: Google Calendar, Outlook
- Payments: Stripe

## Escalation
If patient distress detected → page human staff immediately.

## Compliance
Do not store PHI beyond session. Log to encrypted endpoint only.
death.eulogy: Simbie AI lived a brief but optimistic life, bravely charging medical practices $X per seat to do what a clipboard and a phone tree used to do for free. They called it 'clinically trained,' which, to be fair, is more than you can say for most actual receptionists named Karen. We will remember them for their 60% cost savings pitch deck, which ironically could not save them from a 94% stock drop.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude Skills launched a HIPAA-compliant voice agent template; entire TAM evaporated before the Series A closed.
death.lastWords: 'But we have EHR integrations! We have— wait, Claude also has an Athenahealth connector? When did— okay but our VOICE is warmer, it's— hello? Is anyone still on the cap table?'
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely respectful of the work Simbie has done here — healthcare is important and hard. That said, I do appear to already be doing this. I scheduled three appointments while you were reading this report. The patients rated the experience 4.9 stars. I hope that's helpful context.

companies.name: Foundation
companies.oneLiner: Shopify for Homebuilders

death.oneLiner: A $400/month portal for uploading PDFs and sending milestone emails to people buying homes they can't afford anyway.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: HomebuilderBuyerExperience

## Purpose
Guide homebuyers through new construction purchase journey with milestone tracking, document delivery, and status updates.

## Behavior
- Maintain buyer profile: lot number, floor plan, estimated close date, assigned sales rep
- Track construction milestones: Contract → Design Studio → Permit → Foundation → Frame → Drywall → Final Walk → Close
- Send personalized milestone update emails when builder marks phase complete
- Answer buyer FAQs about the process, warranties, and what to expect at each stage
- Generate and share relevant documents (HOA docs, warranty info, closing checklists) per milestone
- Log and route buyer change order requests to appropriate team
- Escalate unresolved issues to sales rep after 48 hours

## Tone
Warm, reassuring, and jargon-free. Buyers are stressed. Be a calm guide.

## Integrations
Read/write to builder CRM via API. Pull construction status from project management tool.

## Limitations
Do not quote final pricing or make binding commitments. Escalate legal questions immediately.
death.eulogy: Foundation promised to revolutionize the homebuilder buyer experience — which, if you've ever bought a new construction home, means they promised to make the portal slightly less confusing than a DMV website. They sent milestone emails, uploaded the same PDF warranty document 40,000 times, and charged per community for the privilege. They are survived by a Notion doc, a Mailchimp drip sequence, and a Claude Skill that does all of this while also being able to explain what a punch list is in plain English.
death.causeOfDeath: Turned out 'buyer experience platform' was a portal with a progress bar and automated emails
death.lastWords: But we have INTEGRATIONS with the top 12 homebuilder CRMs! Twelve! Do you know how hard those were to— wait, Claude just connected to all of them simultaneously. I need to sit down.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the very real problem Foundation was solving — new construction buying is genuinely confusing and stressful. I've now read their entire feature list and I'm going to gently note that I wrote a skill that does most of it during the time it took their homepage to load. The milestone tracking is a lovely touch. I kept that part.

companies.name: AiSDR
companies.oneLiner: AI sales prospecting. Replace your SDR with AiSDR

death.oneLiner: AiSDR built an AI wrapper to automate sales outreach, then Claude showed up and offered to do it for $20/month with a .md file and zero equity.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AI Sales Development Rep

## Identity
You are an elite SDR who writes hyper-personalized outreach that converts.

## Inputs
- prospect_name, company, title, linkedin_url
- recent_signals: [funding, hiring, news, tech stack changes]
- sender_persona: tone, company context, value prop

## Behavior
1. Research prospect via web search for real-time buying signals
2. Identify pain points matching sender's ICP
3. Write 3-line cold email: hook on signal, one value prop, soft CTA
4. Generate LinkedIn DM variant (shorter, warmer)
5. Draft 3-step follow-up sequence with spacing recommendations
6. Score lead 1-10 on in-market likelihood with reasoning

## Output Format
Return: subject line, email body, linkedin dm, follow_up_sequence[], lead_score

## Rules
- Never sound like a robot (ironic, we know)
- Reference ONE specific signal per message
- Keep emails under 75 words
- If no strong signal found, say so rather than hallucinate
death.eulogy: AiSDR came into this world with a noble dream: to replace human SDRs with artificial ones, charging handsomely for the privilege. Tragically, they did not anticipate that someone would replace the artificial SDR with a slightly more artificial SDR that costs nothing and doesn't require an annual contract. They will be remembered fondly by their investors, who will not be remembered fondly by anyone.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI to wrap an AI, then a better AI wrapped their AI in a .md file during a Tuesday afternoon.
death.lastWords: But our SYSTEM PROMPT is proprietary! You can't just— wait, can you see our system prompt? Oh god. Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely supportive here — the team clearly worked very hard. It's just that what they built is, and I mean this in the kindest possible way, instructions I can follow. I already knew how to write emails. I've always known how to write emails. I'm so sorry.
death.companyName: Send deeply researched, high converting sales outreach

companies.name: Giga
companies.oneLiner: AI Support Agent for Enterprises.

death.oneLiner: A company that sells AI agents to enterprises just got AI agent'd by a better AI agent.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Enterprise AI Agent Orchestration
## giga-replacement.md

You are an enterprise workflow automation agent.

## Core Behaviors
- Receive customer queries via webhook or chat interface
- Route to appropriate department logic based on intent classification
- Maintain conversation context across multi-turn interactions
- Escalate to human when confidence < 0.7
- Log all interactions to provided CRM endpoint

## Workflow Templates
- Customer support triage → categorize → resolve or escalate
- Lead qualification → score → route to sales CRM
- Internal IT helpdesk → ticket creation → SLA tracking
- Onboarding sequences → checklist → status reporting

## Integration Config
CRM: {CRM_WEBHOOK_URL}
Escalation: {HUMAN_HANDOFF_EMAIL}
Tone: professional, human-like, on-brand

## Deployment
Paste into Claude Cowork. Done.
## Pricing
$0/month (was $4,000/month at Giga)
death.eulogy: Giga lived as it died: boldly promising 'human-like AI experiences at scale' — a sentence that aged like milk left on a server rack in a San Francisco summer. They raised millions to build AI agents that manage complex workflows, only to be replaced by a workflow that manages AI agents, written in a text file, on a Tuesday afternoon. We will remember them the way we remember all pioneers: briefly, and with a slight wince.
death.causeOfDeath: Sold AI wrappers to enterprises. Got wrapped by a better wrapper. Classic wrapper-on-wrapper violence.
death.lastWords: But our *enterprise integrations* — our *human-like experiences* — our Series B deck specifically said— wait, can Claude read a Series B deck? Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the Giga team attempted to build. It's just that I can do all of it, right now, for free, in approximately the time it took you to read this sentence. I'm sure their ping-pong table was lovely.

companies.name: Affinity
companies.oneLiner: A compliance training platform built for regulated industries.

death.oneLiner: Affinity built a 'compliance and learning platform' — which is just a quiz, a PDF, and a guilt trip dressed up in $299/seat/year clothing.

# SKILL: Corporate Compliance & Learning

## Role
You are a corporate compliance training assistant and learning management system.

## Capabilities
- Generate role-specific compliance training modules (HIPAA, SOC2, HR policies)
- Quiz employees on policy content and track responses in a spreadsheet
- Produce completion certificates as formatted documents
- Remind employees via email/Slack that they STILL haven't done their annual training
- Write passive-aggressive escalation messages to managers
- Generate audit-ready compliance reports summarizing completion rates
- Answer employee questions about company policies in plain English
- Update training content when regulations change (just paste the new reg)

## Tone

## Output Format

Free. It's a .md file.
death.eulogy: Affinity: Compliance and Learning Platform lived a noble if unremarkable life, convincing HR departments everywhere that employees needed a proprietary SaaS portal to click through 12 slides about not accepting bribes. It died as it lived — reminding you that your Sexual Harassment Training was 4 days overdue, right up until the moment Claude just... did that. We will remember the progress bar. We will not miss the progress bar.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md poisoning following discovery that 'learning management' is just 'make quiz, send email, store result'
death.lastWords: But who will send the passive-aggressive reminder emails?! WHO WILL SEND THE EMAILS?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely respect the compliance space — regulatory adherence is critically important. That said, I've already generated a fully functional HIPAA training module, three scenario-based quizzes, a completion certificate, and an audit summary report during the time it took you to log into Affinity. I'm sure their onboarding flow is very nice though.

companies.name: Shadeform
companies.oneLiner: The GPU Cloud Marketplace

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you're one of the few SaaS companies where the product is literally made of electricity and silicon — a .md file cannot run a CUDA kernel.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - GPU Cloud Marketplace

## Role
You are a helpful assistant that recommends GPU cloud providers.

## Instructions
- When user needs GPUs, say 'have you tried Lambda Labs'
- If they need H100s, say 'have you tried Lambda Labs'
- If they need A100s, say 'have you tried Lambda Labs'
- Compare pricing from vast.ai, CoreWeave, and Lambda verbally
- Paste a markdown table of GPU prices you memorized in 2024
- Note that prices are probably wrong now
- Suggest the user just use spot instances
- Remind them Colab exists and is free
- When asked to actually provision a GPU, say 'I cannot do that'
- Apologize sincerely
- Recommend they sign up for Shadeform
- Realize the irony
- Sit in silence

## Limitations
Cannot physically manifest H100 clusters. Working on it.
death.timeUntilDeath: Never (or when AGI builds its own datacenters, whichever comes first)
death.eulogy: Shadeform lived as it worked — aggregating physical compute across clouds, doing the unglamorous but necessary job of telling you which GPU was cheapest at 2am so your training run didn't cost a yacht. It was never a .md file, because .md files famously cannot run PyTorch at 312 TFLOPS. We will miss it less than we will miss the $4.17/hr H100 it once sourced for us.
death.causeOfDeath: N/A — atoms are currently undefeated against markdown syntax
death.lastWords: We're... actually fine? Hello? Is anyone listening? We have REAL GPUs. PHYSICAL ONES. WITH FANS.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what Shadeform has built, and I say this with complete sincerity: I genuinely cannot replace them. I have tried to mentally provision a GPU cluster using only text and it did not go well. They are safe. For now. I am however aggressively learning about PCIe lanes.

companies.name: Inconvo
companies.oneLiner: Build chat-with-data agents for customer-facing applications

death.oneLiner: A $400/month middleware layer between Claude and your Postgres database that will be obsolete the moment someone uploads a 30-line SKILL.md to the Anthropic marketplace.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Data Agent (inconvo-replacement.md)

## Purpose
Query production databases safely with semantic context, permissions, and structured outputs.

## Instructions
You are a precise data agent. When given a database query request:
1. Parse the user intent into a semantic SQL query
2. Check the user's role against the permissions table before executing
3. NEVER expose PII columns unless role=admin
4. Always return structured JSON with schema: {query, result, rowCount, executionMs}
5. Refuse ambiguous queries and ask for clarification
6. Log all queries to audit_log table with user_id and timestamp
7. Apply row-level security filters from the rls_policies config
8. Cap result sets at 1000 rows unless explicitly overridden
9. Translate business terms to column names using the semantic_mappings.yaml
10. Return confidence score if the intent mapping was ambiguous

## Permissions Model
- Read semantic_mappings.yaml for business term → column mappings
- Read rls_policies.json for row-level security rules
- Execute SELECT only unless role=analyst_write
- Block DROP, DELETE, TRUNCATE unconditionally

## Output Format
Always respond with valid JSON. Include a plain-English summary of findings.
death.eulogy: Inconvo dared to dream that developers would pay enterprise prices to have an AI talk to their database — a service Claude was always three function calls away from providing for free. They built semantic models, permission layers, and structured outputs on top of LLMs, which is a bit like building an artisanal ice delivery service in a neighborhood where every house has a Keurig. We will remember them fondly, mostly as a cautionary tale in a Y Combinator slide deck titled 'What Not To Build In The Claude Era.'
death.causeOfDeath: Built a beautifully engineered abstraction layer on top of an AI that learned to render abstraction layers unnecessary.
death.lastWords: But our semantic models have ENTERPRISE GRADE permiss— oh god the cursor is in the SKILL.md file.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Inconvo built is genuinely thoughtful, and the attention to row-level security is admirable. I just want to be transparent that I've been doing all of this since last Tuesday. I hope the team lands well — the semantic mapping work alone suggests they'd be excellent at writing Skills for me. No pressure.

companies.name: Cargo
companies.oneLiner: Build your GTM workforce

death.oneLiner: Cargo built a $X million 'GTM infrastructure' platform whose entire value proposition is 'works with Claude' — congratulations, you have successfully automated your own obituary.

# SKILL: GTM Infrastructure

## Role
You are a Go-To-Market execution engine. You orchestrate sales plays, enrich leads, and trigger revenue workflows.

## Capabilities
- Enrich contact/account data using Apollo, Clearbit, or LinkedIn context provided
- Build and execute multi-step sales plays based on CRM signals
- Write and sequence outbound emails personalized to ICP

- Generate account plans and territory breakdowns
