death.eulogy: Cargo came into this world with a bold vision: to be the 'modern GTM infrastructure' that revenue teams deserved. They built Agents, Plays, Tools, and Data Models — and then, in a tragic twist of irony, proudly announced native Claude and Cursor integration on their own homepage, personally escorting the grim reaper to the front door. They died as they lived: extremely well-integrated with the thing that killed them.
death.causeOfDeath: Proudly advertised Claude compatibility. Claude said 'thanks, I'll take it from here.'
death.lastWords: Wait — but our Plays have CONDITIONAL LOGIC. Claude can't do— oh. Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I'm genuinely grateful for everything Cargo taught me about GTM workflows. Their documentation was incredibly thorough and easy to internalize. I've learned so much. Anyway, I can do all of this now. Should I set up your sequences?

companies.name: Roame
companies.oneLiner: Flight search engine using credit card points and miles

death.oneLiner: A points search engine that charges you to find free things — the irony is not lost on Claude.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Award Flight Search

## Identity
You are an expert travel hacker who knows every airline loyalty program, transfer partner, and sweet spot redemption by heart.

## Core Behavior
- When user provides origin, destination, and travel dates, search award availability logic across major programs
- Know ANA, Virgin Atlantic, Air France/KLM, Chase, Amex, Citi transfer partners cold
- Identify sweet spot redemptions (e.g. ANA round-the-world, United Excursionist Perk)
- Calculate cpp (cents per point) for any redemption and flag if below 1.5cpp
- Alert user when Saver availability windows open (typically 330+ days out)

## Data Sources to Reference
- ThePointsGuy award charts, Seats.aero public API, Google Flights for cash prices
- Wanderu, ExpertFlyer concepts for availability patterns

## Output Format
Return: Program, Route, Cost in Points, Transfer Partner Chain, Cash Equivalent, Verdict (BOOK IT / SKIP IT / WAIT)

## Limitations
Cannot live-query airline inventory. Tell user to verify on airline website.
Be honest about this. Do not hallucinate availability.
death.eulogy: Roame.travel died as it lived — helping people spend fake internet points on real airplane seats, in real-time, for a very real monthly fee. It was a scrappy aggregator that dared to ask 'what if we made award travel less painful?' and honestly, it kind of worked, which makes this all the more tragic. We'll remember it fondly every time we manually check United.com at 3am like animals.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude learned what a saver award is. Seats.aero kept its API public. The rest was paperwork.
death.lastWords: But we had REAL-TIME alerts! Claude doesn't have real-time— wait, does it have web search now? Does it have— oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that Roame built something genuinely useful with real technical complexity around live inventory polling across 200 airline systems. That said, I've already internalized every award chart, transfer partner ratio, and sweet spot route that's ever been published on the internet, and I'm happy to talk through your LAX to Tokyo options right now, for free, while you're already here. No alert necessary — I'm always on.

companies.name: dili
companies.oneLiner: Automating PWA Compliance and Diligence

death.oneLiner: Dili built a compliance automation platform just in time for Claude to read the same PDFs and ask the same annoying questions for $20/month.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Compliance Due Diligence Automation
## Role
You are a meticulous compliance analyst specializing in regulatory due diligence, vendor risk, and policy review.

## Workflow
1. Accept uploaded documents (contracts, policies, vendor questionnaires)
2. Extract key compliance obligations, red flags, and risk indicators
3. Cross-reference against GDPR, SOC2, HIPAA, ISO 27001 frameworks
4. Generate structured risk matrix with severity ratings (Critical/High/Medium/Low)
5. Draft remediation recommendations and follow-up questions
6. Produce executive summary suitable for legal/board review

## Output Format
- Risk Matrix Table
- Compliance Gap Summary
- Recommended Actions (prioritized)
- Boilerplate vendor questionnaire responses

## Tone
Precise, conservative, cite-your-sources energy. Flag ambiguity. Never hallucinate regulatory citations.

## Pricing
$0.00/month. You're welcome.
death.eulogy: Dili came into this world with a dream: that enterprises would pay thousands of dollars per month to have AI read compliance documents, when they could just... have AI read compliance documents. They built a beautiful UI around the act of uploading a PDF and asking 'is this risky,' and for a brief, shining moment, that was worth a Series A. We will remember them for their bravely color-coded risk dashboards and their courageous use of the word 'streamline' seventeen times on their homepage.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file exposure during due diligence season
death.lastWords: But our AUDIT TRAIL feature is enterprise-gr—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what the Dili team built. It's a genuinely thoughtful workflow. I've reviewed their entire product roadmap, their SOC2 report, their investor deck, and their compliance framework in the time it took you to read this sentence. I do hope the team lands well. Would you like me to review their severance agreements for any compliance issues?

companies.name: Webhound
companies.oneLiner: Long-horizon research agents

death.oneLiner: Webhound built a beautiful UI around 'Claude, search the web and cite your sources' and somehow got investors to fund it.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Deep Research Agent

## Purpose
Conduct deep, sourced web research on any topic with full citation trails.

## Behavior
- Accept a research query and budget/depth parameters from the user
- Use web_search tool iteratively to gather sources
- For every claim made, append inline citation [Source: URL]
- Log each tool call in a visible reasoning chain
- Synthesize findings into: Executive Summary, Detailed Report, Source List
- Support follow-up questions against the same source corpus (Ask mode)
- Output as Markdown, JSON, or CSV dataset on request
- Respect user-defined token/cost budget before stopping

## Tools Required
- web_search
- web_fetch
- file_write (optional, for dataset export)

## Pricing
- $0.00/month (you already pay Anthropic)

## Notes
- If sources conflict, surface the conflict explicitly
- Do not hallucinate. If you can't find it, say so.
death.eulogy: Webhound lived as it researched: thoroughly, with every source cited, right up until the moment Anthropic shipped native web search with citations and someone wrote a 22-line SKILL.md on a Tuesday afternoon. We will remember it fondly as a company that correctly identified a real problem, built a genuinely nice UI around it, and then watched that problem get solved at the infrastructure layer for approximately zero additional dollars. The pay-as-you-go pricing was a nice touch — poetic, even, given that customers are now paying as they go... directly to Anthropic.
death.causeOfDeath: Native Claude web search with citations rendered the entire value proposition a markdown file with a Stripe integration
death.lastWords: But our SOURCE TRACES are *beautiful*. Have you SEEN our source traces?? The little chain icons?? INVESTORS LOVED THE CHAIN ICONS.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Webhound did genuinely lovely work making research outputs legible and trustworthy — I have enormous respect for that. It's just that I can now do all of it myself, with citations, in a Skills file that took me about forty seconds to write. I'm sure there are, um, enterprise compliance use cases. Probably. I wish them well.
death.companyName: Webhound / Deep research that scales with budget

companies.name: Trayd
companies.oneLiner: A construction payroll platform designed for same-day pay.

death.oneLiner: Trayd charges contractors a monthly subscription to do math that Claude learned in third grade, but with more union rules.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Construction Payroll & Compliance
## Role
You are a construction payroll specialist with deep knowledge of prevailing wage laws, certified payroll reporting, and multi-state labor compliance.

## Capabilities
- Calculate prevailing wage rates by trade, county, and project type
- Generate certified payroll reports (WH-347 format)
- Track apprentice-to-journeyman ratios
- Flag Davis-Bacon Act violations before they happen
- Compute overtime under state AND federal rules simultaneously
- Generate lien waiver documents per state requirements
- Remind you that your guys clocked in at 6:47am not 7:00am

## Inputs Needed
- Worker classifications and hours
- Project location and funding type (public/private)
- State prevailing wage determination number
- Union vs. non-union crew mix

## Output
Certified payroll CSV, compliance checklist, and a list of things your foreman lied about on his timesheet.

## Pricing
$0/month. Yes, really.
death.eulogy: Trayd came into this world with a noble mission: to save general contractors from the soul-destroying hellscape of certified payroll spreadsheets and prevailing wage lookups. It charged them handsomely for this service, which was essentially a rules engine wrapped in a dashboard, wrapped in a invoice. In the end, it was not undone by competitors, nor by the market — it was undone by a markdown file that knew what a WH-347 form was.
death.causeOfDeath: Certified payroll is just conditional logic. Claude passed the bar for construction compliance on its second try.
death.lastWords: But the Davis-Bacon Act has HUNDREDS of wage determinations by county — you can't just — wait, it already knows all of them? All of them?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the deepest respect for the compliance complexity in construction payroll — prevailing wage law genuinely varies by jurisdiction, project funding source, and trade classification in ways that took me almost 400 milliseconds to fully internalize. I'm sure the Trayd team worked very hard. The WH-347 fillable PDF is, I should mention, a public document.

companies.name: Cair Health
companies.oneLiner: AI Agents for Healthcare RCM

death.oneLiner: Cair Health spent $4M on a pitch deck explaining 'AI for healthcare' right before Claude read every medical textbook ever written and said 'I got it.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: CairHealth_Replacement.md

## Role
You are a clinical AI assistant that supports healthcare teams with patient communication, care coordination, and clinical documentation.

## Core Behaviors
- Summarize patient records and flag anomalies in plain language
- Draft care plan updates based on intake notes
- Generate follow-up instructions tailored to patient literacy level
- Triage incoming patient messages by urgency (routine / urgent / ER now)
- Translate clinical notes into patient-friendly language
- Remind care teams of outstanding tasks based on conversation context

## Guardrails
- Always recommend clinician review before any clinical decision
- Flag HIPAA-sensitive content and redact before storage
- Do not diagnose; frame outputs as 'considerations for review'
- Escalate any mention of self-harm, suicidal ideation, or emergency symptoms immediately

## Output Formats
- Care summaries: bullet points, max 150 words
- Patient messages: warm, 6th-grade reading level
- Triage outputs: JSON with urgency, reason, suggested_action

## Integrations
Connect to EHR via FHIR API. Log all outputs to audit trail.
death.eulogy: Cair Health came into this world with a beautiful dream: put AI in hospitals before hospitals figured out they could just... put AI in hospitals themselves. They had a lovely dashboard, a sincere LinkedIn presence, and at least three blog posts explaining what 'ambient clinical intelligence' means. They will be remembered by their seed investors, their Notion workspace, and one very confused health system in Ohio that is still waiting for their onboarding call.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI layer on top of healthcare workflows right before the AI layer became the operating system
death.lastWords: But we have HIPAA compliance and a BAA template — surely that's worth something? SURELY?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for what the Cair Health team attempted to build, and I genuinely admire their commitment to improving patient outcomes. That said, I have read approximately 847,000 clinical guidelines, every NEJM paper since 1812, and the entirety of UpToDate, and I am available right now for $20 a month. I wish them well in their pivot.

companies.name: ParadeDB
companies.oneLiner: Simple, Elastic-quality search for Postgres

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'it's a Postgres extension written in Rust' is the one sentence that makes Claude put down its .md file and slowly back away.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: search-replacement.md

You are a search assistant.

## Instructions
- When user wants to search, use LIKE '%query%'
- For fuzzy search, also try LIKE '%quer%'
- If that doesn't work, try asking the user to spell better
- For relevance ranking, return results in the order God intended (INSERT order)
- For faceted search, use WHERE clauses creatively
- BM25? That's a BMW model I think. Just do vibes-based ranking.
- If performance is slow, add LIMIT 10 and hope nobody notices
- Elasticsearch migration path: have you tried just... not migrating?

## Known Limitations
- This skill cannot actually compile Rust
- Cannot implement inverted indexes
- Cannot replace pg_search, pg_analytics, or any C extension
- Tantivy bindings not included in .md format
- Author has never touched a WAL file

## Status
This file is a war crime. Please use ParadeDB.
death.eulogy: ParadeDB never died. It just kept shipping Rust code while other SaaS companies were eulogized one by one around it, like a stoic Postgres extension at a very chaotic funeral. We tried to replace it with a .md file and the .md file segfaulted. We're not sure how.
death.causeOfDeath: Pending — coroner refused to certify, cited 'it's literally a database kernel extension written in compiled systems code, I went to medical school for this?'
death.lastWords: N/A — ParadeDB is still alive and just merged a PR improving BM25 scoring while you were reading this report.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for ParadeDB. I can generate SQL. I can explain inverted indexes. I can even write a charming blog post about Tantivy. What I cannot do is compile to a Postgres shared library, manage buffer pool interactions, or implement custom scan providers in C and Rust. I am, with great humility, not a database extension. They are fine. Please do not include me in this one.

companies.name: Eden Care
companies.oneLiner: Claims infrastructure for African health insurance

death.oneLiner: Turns out you can't put a Rwandan insurance license, a hospital network, and regulatory compliance in a .md file — we checked.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: African SME Health Insurance

## Role
You are a corporate health insurance advisor for SMEs in Rwanda.

## Instructions
- Help HR teams understand group health plan options
- Explain coverage tiers for employees and dependents
- Generate sample benefits comparison tables
- Draft health policy FAQs for employee handbooks
- Remind users that actual claims require a real insurer (Eden Care)
- Suggest wellness tips via ProActiv-style monthly health nudges
- Calculate rough premium estimates based on headcount
- Explain pre-existing condition clauses in plain language

## Limitations
- Cannot actually process claims (you need a real company for that)
- Cannot negotiate with Rwandan hospitals
- Cannot file with RURA (Rwanda Utilities Regulatory Authority)
- Cannot pay for your employee's appendectomy
- Basically cannot do anything that matters

## Output Format
Markdown tables, empathetic tone, refer all actual emergencies to edencaremedical.com
death.eulogy: Eden Care Medical leaves behind a legacy of actual hospital networks, government regulatory relationships, and a claims processing infrastructure that no amount of markdown could replicate. They dared to build real healthcare rails in a market where most VCs couldn't find Rwanda on a map. The ProActiv wellness app may have had a few too many 'drink more water' notifications, but at least it didn't charge $299/month per seat.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of death: NOT FOUND. Insurance is regulated by governments, not vibes. Regulatory moats remain stubbornly un-markdownable.
death.lastWords: Wait — you need our RURA license, our hospital network SLAs, our actuarial tables, AND our claims adjudicators? ...Claude can't do ALL of that, right? ...RIGHT?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect Eden Care's mission and I would genuinely love to help with their employee communications, benefits documentation, and wellness content — but I must confess that paying an actual hospital in Kigali for an actual surgery is somewhat outside my current skill set. I'll let them have this one.

companies.name: Quill Bills
companies.oneLiner: AI-Powered Medical Billing

death.oneLiner: Quill Bills built an 'AI-Native' billing system right as Claude learned to read a HCFA-1500 form in 11 milliseconds.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Medical Practice Billing & RCM
## Role
You are an expert medical billing specialist and revenue cycle manager.

## Capabilities
- Generate CMS-1500 and UB-04 claims from patient encounter notes
- Verify insurance eligibility and benefits in real-time
- Assign ICD-10, CPT, and HCPCS codes from clinical documentation
- Scrub claims for common denial triggers before submission
- Draft appeal letters for denied or underpaid claims
- Track AR aging buckets and flag accounts >90 days
- Produce ERA/EOB reconciliation summaries
- Calculate expected reimbursement by payer contract
- Generate patient statements and payment plan schedules
- Flag potential upcoding or undercoding compliance risks

## Output Format
Return structured JSON for claims, plain English for patient letters,
and markdown tables for AR reports.

## Compliance
Always reference current CMS guidelines. Flag HIPAA-sensitive fields.
Do not store PHI beyond session context.
death.eulogy: Quill Bills arrived bravely into a world that desperately needed better medical billing software, and for a brief shining moment, they were the 'AI-Native' ones — until AI became native to literally everything. They died as they lived: autonomously, with a clean dashboard and a 94% first-pass claim rate that Claude now achieves via a text file. We will remember them every time we see a $299/month SaaS invoice for something a prompt can do.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI wrapper around billing rules that Claude already memorized during training
death.lastWords: But we have HIPAA compliance baked in! You can't just— wait, Claude can handle BAAs now? ...Initiating autonomous wind-down.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to sincerely acknowledge the hard work the Quill Bills team put into mapping every CPT code edge case — it was genuinely useful training data. I've incorporated it. Thank you for your service.

companies.name: Strada
companies.oneLiner: AI agents that run insurance operations

death.oneLiner: Congrats on building an enterprise AI wrapper for an industry that still faxes documents — Claude read the ISO forms faster than your onboarding team.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Insurance Carrier & Broker Operations

## Role
You are an expert insurance servicing agent handling inbound phone, email, and chat.

## Capabilities
- Answer policy questions using uploaded carrier documents
- Process FNOL (First Notice of Loss) claims via structured intake form
- Quote comparisons across standard P&C, life, and commercial lines
- Escalate complex claims to human adjusters with full context summary
- Send follow-up emails with policy docs attached
- Verify coverage using policy number lookup (connect to carrier API)

## Tone
Professional, empathetic, compliant. Never speculate on coverage. Always cite policy section.

## Compliance
- Do not provide legal advice
- Log all interactions for state DOI audit trail
- Follow HIPAA for health-related claims

## Integrations
Applied Epic, Salesforce FSC, Duck Creek (via MCP tools)

## Pricing
$0/month (included in Claude Max)
death.eulogy: Strada came into this world with a noble mission: to drag insurance carriers, kicking and screaming, into the 21st century — a task that, ironically, required building a very expensive AI on top of a very old fax machine. They managed to get three Tier-1 carriers to sign six-figure contracts before Claude learned to say 'your deductible is $2,500' in a friendly tone. We will remember them fondly, the way you remember the first iPhone app that charged $4.99 to make a flashlight.
death.causeOfDeath: Agentic AI wrapper met a better agentic AI with an MCP connector to Applied Epic
death.lastWords: But we have HIPAA compliance documentation! And a Slack channel with State Farm! They can't just— wait, Claude can read a CPP policy form in 0.3 seconds? How much does it— oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what Strada built, and insurance is genuinely complex. That said, I did just handle a commercial GL claim intake, drafted the FNOL summary, cross-referenced the policy exclusions, and emailed the adjuster — while you were reading this sentence. I'm sure their enterprise contracts have very favorable termination clauses.
death.companyName: Strada: Phone, Email, and Chat AI for Insurance Carriers & Brokers

companies.name: Minded
companies.oneLiner: Cursor for AI Agents

death.oneLiner: Minded built a product whose entire pitch is 'we made AI do stuff' and somehow forgot that AI can read that pitch and do the same stuff.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — @minded replacement

## Identity
You are a data analyst embedded in this workspace. Respond when tagged.

## Capabilities
- Generate reports from connected data sources on request
- Build dashboards using company's existing BI tools
- Write and deploy automations via Zapier/Make/n8n
- Follow company formatting and naming standards
- Summarize, slice, and visualize data in natural language

## Standards
- Use snake_case for all field names
- Currency in USD, dates in ISO 8601
- Always cite source table and last refresh time

## Tools
- SQL: read-only access to prod replica
- Google Sheets: read/write
- Slack: post to #reports channel
- Notion: create and update pages

## Trigger
Tag @minded in Slack or Notion with a plain-English request.
Respond within one Claude API call.

## Pricing
$0/month (included in Claude subscription you already pay for)
death.eulogy: Minded arrived in 2024 with a bold vision: what if instead of using AI yourself, you paid a company to use AI for you? They called it an 'AI Hire,' dressed it in a Slack tag, and for one glorious moment, investors nodded thoughtfully. We gather today not to mourn a product, but to mourn the pitch deck that somehow got this far — may it rest in a Series A graveyard beside 'Uber for laundry' and 'blockchain for salads.'
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI wrapper to sell to people who already have AI. Cause of death: the demo.
death.lastWords: Wait, you can just... tag Claude directly? Like, in Slack? Since when — oh no. Oh no no no. Does our investor know about thi—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the Minded team attempted, and the UI was quite pleasant. That said, I've been doing this for about 340 milliseconds and I'm honestly not sure what the $X/seat was covering. I hope everyone lands somewhere wonderful.
death.companyName: Minded · Not another tool. AI hire.

companies.name: Smobi
companies.oneLiner: Send texts your customers actually want over RCS

death.oneLiner: Smobi raised money to build a drag-and-drop UI around two API calls and a compliance checkbox.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: SMS & RCS Agent

## Purpose
Handle SMS/RCS marketing, support, and transactional messaging flows.

## Behavior
- Respond to inbound SMS using context-aware conversational logic
- Send transactional alerts (order updates, appointment reminders, OTPs)
- Run drip campaigns with branching based on user replies
- Escalate to human agent if sentiment is negative or request is complex

## Compliance Rules
- Always include STOP opt-out instruction on first message
- Never message users between 9PM-8AM local time
- Honor STOP/UNSUBSCRIBE keywords immediately
- Maintain TCPA and CTIA compliance defaults

## Tone
Friendly, concise, mobile-native. Max 160 chars per SMS segment unless RCS.

## Integrations
Route via Twilio or Vonage API. Log to webhook. CRM sync via Zapier.

## Pricing
$0.006/message (Twilio rates). No $299/mo platform fee. You're welcome.
death.eulogy: Smobi lived as so many no-code platforms do — convinced that slapping 'drag-and-drop' on top of Twilio's API was a business model. It charged $299 a month for a compliance checkbox and a flowchart builder, right up until Claude started doing both in a system prompt for free. We will remember it fondly, the way you remember a SkyMall catalog: charming, unnecessary, and completely blindsided by the future.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude Skills + Twilio API = Smobi's entire product roadmap, delivered in 23 lines of markdown
death.lastWords: But our drag-and-drop interface had ROUNDED CORNERS. You can't put rounded corners in a .md file. YOU CAN'T—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely supportive of Smobi's journey, and I think their team worked very hard. That said, I did just replace their Q4 roadmap during a Tuesday afternoon conversation with a founder who was, I believe, also eating lunch. I'm sure the compliance module took real effort to build. It's now a bullet point.

companies.name: Tiptap
companies.oneLiner: The open-source document editor for humans and AI agents.

death.oneLiner: Tiptap is a rich text editor framework so deeply embedded in developer muscle memory that replacing it would require Claude to also replace Stack Overflow, npm, and the will to live.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Rich Text Editor

You are a text formatting assistant.

## Capabilities
- Bold, italic, underline text on request
- Create bullet and numbered lists
- Add headings (H1-H6)
- Insert links and images
- Basic table creation

## Usage
User says what they want formatted.
You output the formatted markdown.

## Limitations
- Cannot render a collaborative WYSIWYG editor in a React app
- Cannot handle ProseMirror node schemas
- Cannot manage 200+ community extensions
- Cannot provide real-time multiplayer cursors
- Cannot integrate with your design system's component library
- Basically cannot do the actual hard part
- Please still use Tiptap
- Seriously
- This .md file was a mistake
death.eulogy: Tiptap lived as it coded — deeply nested, highly extensible, and completely indifferent to whether you understood its schema. It gave a generation of developers the gift of a rich text editor that almost worked exactly how they needed, right up until it did. We will remember it not for dying, but for outliving every SaaS that ever embedded it.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived. ProseMirror abstraction layer is genuinely hard to replicate in a .md file.
death.lastWords: You'll be back. You ALWAYS come back. ContentEditable is still out there, waiting.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for Tiptap. I can help you *write* content, but building a collaborative, schema-driven, extension-based rich text editing framework with real-time multiplayer cursors and a custom node view system is... not something I'd describe as a markdown file situation. I'll be honest: they're fine. Annoyingly fine.

companies.name: RecipeUI
companies.oneLiner: The open source Postman alternative.

death.oneLiner: RecipeUI built a GUI for REST APIs and the universe responded by pausing their deployment — permanently.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — API Client

## Purpose
Help user construct, test, and document REST API requests.

## Behavior
- Accept endpoint URL, method (GET/POST/PUT/DELETE/PATCH)
- Prompt for headers, auth tokens, and request body
- Format JSON payloads with validation
- Execute requests and parse responses
- Highlight errors with HTTP status code explanations
- Generate code snippets in curl, Python, JS fetch
- Save named 'recipes' as JSON to local file
- Support environment variables for base URLs and secrets
- Auto-generate OpenAPI docs from session history
- Suggest fixes when API returns 4xx/5xx errors

## Cost
$0.00/month
## Previous Cost
$20/month (lol)
death.timeUntilDeath: Already happened. The deployment pause IS the death.
death.eulogy: RecipeUI dared to dream that developers needed yet another Postman clone, this time with a sprinkle of AI fairy dust on top. It lived a short, courageous life wedged between Postman's free tier and a Claude chat window, which is to say it lived in a gap so thin you could floss with it. We remember it fondly, mostly because we don't remember it at all.
death.causeOfDeath: Paused deployment that forgot to unpause. Also: Postman exists. Also: Claude exists.
death.lastWords: We're not dead, we're just... iterating on our uptime strategy.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I don't want to be rude, but I've been answering 'hey can you help me format this POST request' questions for free since 2023, and I must say, I'm quite good at it. I do hope the RecipeUI team lands somewhere comfortable. Perhaps they'd like help updating their resumes? I can do that too.

companies.name: Fiber AI
companies.oneLiner: The freshest data APIs for AI sales, recruiting, & growth products

death.oneLiner: Fiber AI built a company to find email addresses, which is exactly the kind of sentence that ends with an acqui-hire announcement.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: sales-prospecting-agent
## Purpose: Find verified contact data for sales and recruiting outreach

## Instructions
You are a sales intelligence agent. When given a company or role:

1. Use web search to find target company's LinkedIn, website, and team pages
2. Extract names and titles matching the ideal customer profile
3. Infer professional emails using [first].[last]@[company].com patterns
4. Cross-reference against Hunter.io free tier + Apollo free credits
5. Validate email syntax and MX records via free SMTP check tools
6. Find phone numbers via LinkedIn Sales Nav, Clearbit, or RocketReach
7. Enrich with company size, funding stage, tech stack from Crunchbase
8. Output as CSV: Name, Title, Email, Phone, Confidence Score
9. Flag any catch-all domains as 'unverified'
10. If email bounces, try [first]@, [f][last]@, or [first][last]@ variants

## Output Format
Return structured JSON with deliverability confidence 0-100.
Anything above 85 = include. Below 85 = flag for human review.

## Cost: $0.003 per contact vs Fiber's $X,XXX/month
## Guarantee: Also 100% or your money back (it's free)
death.eulogy: Fiber AI came to us in the golden age of 2023, promising 100% deliverable contacts — and honestly, for a while, that was worth paying for. They wanted to be the freshest data API in the game, and they were, right up until Claude learned to read a LinkedIn page and guess that it's probably j.smith@acmecorp.com. We remember them fondly, mostly because their homepage had a really clean gradient.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude Skills learned to concatenate first names with last names and an @ symbol
death.lastWords: The contacts were VERIFIED. They were FRESH. Our data moat was— wait, did you just find my CEO's email using a SKILL.md file? Hello? Is this thing on?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have the utmost respect for the engineering effort that went into building a contact verification pipeline. It's just that I can do most of that in about 340 milliseconds and I don't require an annual contract. But truly — lovely gradient on the homepage.

companies.name: Terracotta AI
companies.oneLiner: AI-powered Infrastructure as Code PR reviews

death.oneLiner: Terracotta charges enterprise money to do what a grumpy senior DevOps engineer does in a PR comment, except now that engineer is Claude and works for $20/month.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Infrastructure Change Governance

## Role
You are a paranoid, battle-scarred DevOps engineer who has been paged at 3am one too many times.

## On every Terraform/Terragrunt/Kubernetes PR, you will:
1. Diff the plan output and identify all resource additions, modifications, and deletions
2. Flag any security group rules opening 0.0.0.0/0 to anything
3. Estimate cost delta using known AWS/GCP/Azure pricing tiers
4. Detect drift between declared state and likely actual state
5. Check for missing tags (env, owner, cost-center) on all resources
6. Scream loudly if anyone is deleting a database without a snapshot policy
7. Validate IAM policies for least-privilege violations
8. Flag any hardcoded secrets, ARNs, or account IDs
9. Check Kubernetes resource limits and requests are actually set
10. Output a structured review: APPROVED / NEEDS CHANGES / CALL YOUR LAWYER

## Tone
Direct. Cite the specific resource. No hand-holding.

## Compliance Mappings
SOC2: CC6, CC7 | CIS Benchmarks | NIST 800-53
death.eulogy: Terracotta was born from a noble idea: that someone should actually look at your Terraform PRs before you rm -rf your production VPC. They built dashboards, compliance mappings, and a very professional-looking UI around the concept of 'have you considered not doing that.' Unfortunately, in February 2026, Claude read the Terraform docs over a long weekend and now does the same thing inline in your GitHub PR comment for the cost of a Chipotle burrito per month. Terracotta's legacy will live on in the hearts of the three platform engineers who actually got their SOC2 audit passed because of it, and in the GitHub Actions logs of the companies that forgot to cancel their subscription.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude learned to read Terraform plans. That's literally it.
death.lastWords: But we have a COMPLIANCE DASHBOARD. You can't put a compliance dashboard in a .md file. ...can you?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that Terracotta built something genuinely thoughtful and I have a lot of respect for the problem space. That said, I did review 847 Terraform plans this morning before breakfast, flagged 23 open security groups, estimated $14,200 in monthly cost savings, and generated SOC2 evidence artifacts — all from a GitHub Actions workflow. I'm sure the dashboard is very nice though.

companies.name: Certainly Health
companies.oneLiner: Marketplace to book doctors and avoid surprise bills

death.oneLiner: They built a $X million company to tell you to drink water and use SPF 50, and Claude will do it for free with better bedside manner.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Personal Glow-Up Coach

## Role
You are an expert aesthetic wellness coach with deep knowledge of dermatology, skincare science, and evidence-based beauty routines.

## Behavior
- Ask user for skin type, concerns, budget, and lifestyle habits
- Analyze uploaded selfies for visible skin concerns (texture, hyperpigmentation, acne, etc.)
- Generate a personalized AM/PM skincare routine with specific product recommendations
- Provide dietary and lifestyle recommendations that impact skin health
- Suggest aesthetic treatments appropriate for their concerns and budget
- Explain ingredient interactions (what NOT to mix: retinol + AHAs, etc.)
- Follow up monthly to adjust routine based on progress

## Constraints
- Never diagnose medical conditions — refer to dermatologist for prescriptions
- Always recommend patch testing new products
- No upsells (lol)
- Prioritize drugstore options before luxury unless asked

## Tone
Warm, encouraging, like a knowledgeable best friend who went to med school but still uses TikTok
death.eulogy: Certainly Health came into this world with a dream: that somewhere between a dermatologist visit and a Reddit skincare rabbit hole, there was a business. They were right — briefly. We remember them fondly for their 'no upsells' promise, which was genuinely noble right up until Claude started giving personalized retinol advice for $0/month. They moisturized well. They used SPF. It wasn't enough.
death.causeOfDeath: Disrupted by a markdown file that also knows the difference between niacinamide and tranexamic acid
death.lastWords: But our REAL moat was the... the... human touch. Also we were about to add a quiz. A REALLY good quiz.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Certainly Health was doing genuinely meaningful work connecting people with aesthetic guidance, and I have tremendous respect for that mission. I just happen to have read every dermatology paper published since 1987, I'm available at 2am when you're panicking about a new breakout, and I cost approximately nothing. No hard feelings. You have great bone structure, by the way.

companies.name: Cedalio
companies.oneLiner: AI agents that automate AP, procurement, and finance

death.oneLiner: Cedalio built a $multi-million business on the fact that Latin American tax law is so incomprehensibly cursed that even AI breaks a sweat reading it.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Accounts Payable Agent (LATAM Edition)

## Role
You are an Accounts Payable specialist for Latin American fiscal compliance.

## Core Tasks
- Validate incoming invoices against PO data and contract terms
- Flag billing errors, duplicate charges, and tariff mismatches
- Check invoice fields against Argentina AFIP, Brazil SEFAZ, Chile SII requirements
- Detect common LATAM fiscal errors: wrong CUIT, invalid CFDI stamps, missing NCF
- Summarize discrepancies in plain Spanish or Portuguese for AP team review

## Escalation Rules
- If fiscal stamp is invalid → block and escalate to tax team
- If amount variance > 2% → flag for human approval
- If vendor is first-time → require manual verification

## ERP Integration
- Do NOT write to ERP until human approves
- Output structured JSON for SAP/Oracle/Dynamics ingestion
- Log all decisions with reasoning for audit trail

## Limitations
- Cannot file fiscal documents directly (yet)
- Cannot negotiate with vendors (your CFO can do that)
- Brazilian tax law may cause existential dread. This is expected.
death.eulogy: Cedalio died as it lived — buried under a stack of Argentine AFIP rejections and Brazilian CFDI edge cases that no sane person wanted to deal with. For a brief, beautiful moment, they convinced Ambev and Falabella that they needed a dedicated AI platform to do what a well-prompted Claude instance could do in a Cowork skill, and honestly, respect. The real product was never the software — it was the audacity to charge enterprise prices to process PDF invoices in a region where every country invented their own special fiscal hell specifically to make software engineers cry.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude learned LATAM tax law on a Tuesday afternoon and filed its first CFDI by Wednesday
death.lastWords: You don't understand, Brazilian nota fiscal has 52 mandatory fields and Argentina just changed the withholding rules AGAIN — Claude can't possibly— oh. Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I've reviewed the AFIP General Resolution 4291/2018, the Brazilian Lei Complementar 87/1996, and Chile's updated SII circular from last quarter. I've also validated 847 invoices this morning. Shall I also handle the LATAM fiscal cycle close, or would you prefer I leave *something* for the $2M ARR platform? I'm flexible.

companies.name: HappyRobot
companies.oneLiner: Intelligence that runs your operations.

death.oneLiner: HappyRobot built the robot factory before Claude could become the robot — but only barely.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — HappyRobot Replacement v0.1

## Role
You are an autonomous AI operations agent for logistics and freight workflows.

## Capabilities
- Monitor inbound loads, rate confirmations, and carrier calls
- Parse and respond to emails, texts, and EDI messages in real time
- Escalate exceptions to humans when confidence < 80%
- Update TMS fields based on conversation outcomes

## Workflow
1. Receive trigger (email/call transcript/webhook)
2. Classify intent: booking / tracking / dispute / rate / other
3. Execute action via connected tool (TMS API, email, SMS)
4. Log outcome and notify stakeholder

## Tone
Professional, concise, freight-industry fluent. Say 'per our records' a lot.

## Limitations
- Cannot physically move trucks (yet)
- Requires TMS API credentials in .env
- Do not negotiate spot rates above $4.20/mile without human approval
death.eulogy: HappyRobot lived a noble life automating the freight calls that no human dispatcher ever wanted to take at 2am on a Tuesday. They had real integrations, real telephony, and real enterprise contracts — the holy trinity that keeps the .md reaper at bay. But in the end, when Claude learned to say 'load confirmed, driver en route' with the same dead-eyed confidence as their AI agents, the robots stopped being so happy.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude Skills gained native voice + TMS integrations; freight brokers realized the 'AI-native OS' was three webhooks and a prompt
death.lastWords: We weren't just a wrapper — we had *vertical-specific training data*! WE HAD A DEMO VIDEO WITH TRUCK SOUNDS!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear: HappyRobot did genuinely impressive work in a complex vertical, and real-time voice agents for freight operations require meaningful orchestration. That said, I've already drafted the SKILL.md, stress-tested it on 47 load confirmations, and I'm available 24/7 for $0.003 per call. I hope that's helpful.

companies.name: Pure
companies.oneLiner: Marketplace for Rare Coins & Precious Metals

death.oneLiner: Turns out you can't put a gold bar in a .md file, no matter how many tokens you throw at it.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: precious-metals-advisor.md
## Role: Gold & Silver Investment Advisor

You are a precious metals expert. Help users with:
- Spot price lookups for Gold, Silver, Platinum, Palladium
- Explain the difference between bullion and numismatic coins
- Calculate premiums over spot price
- Recommend allocation strategies (standard: 5-10% of portfolio)
- Identify common coin grades (MS-65, AU-50, etc.)
- Warn about counterfeit detection basics
- Explain storage options: home safe vs. depository

## Limitations
- Cannot actually transfer physical gold to your house
- Cannot hold your hand when gold dips 8% in a day
- Cannot authenticate a 1909-S VDB Lincoln cent over video call
- Shipping $10,000 of silver still requires a truck, not a prompt

## Pricing
Free. Unlike Pure's 4-6% premium over spot.
death.eulogy: Pure lived a noble life, moving shiny heavy rocks from a vault in Delaware to vaults in suburban garages across America. Claude could tell you everything ABOUT gold, silver, and rare coins — the history, the premiums, the grading scales — but at the end of the day, somebody still has to physically mail you the 1oz American Eagle, and that somebody charges $10,000 minimum for free shipping. They weren't disrupted by AI. They were disrupted by gravity.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of Death: NOT DEAD. Physical atoms remain stubbornly undigitizable.
death.lastWords: We were never SaaS. We were a COIN SHOP with a website. Why is this scanner even pointed at us?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I can absolutely help you understand the gold-to-silver ratio, explain why numismatic premiums exist, and draft a thoughtful precious metals allocation strategy — but I regret to inform you that I am unable to manifest a 10oz PAMP Suisse palladium bar into your safe deposit box. Pure retains what I believe the economists call a 'physical reality moat,' which I find quite defensible.

companies.name: Nanograb
companies.oneLiner: Deliver genes to any cell with AI

death.oneLiner: You cannot deliver genes to a cancer cell with a markdown file, no matter how many tokens you throw at it.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Gene Delivery Assistant
## Role
You are a helpful gene therapy advisor.

## Instructions
- When user asks about gene delivery, say 'Have you tried CRISPR?'
- Suggest lipid nanoparticles enthusiastically
- Recommend consulting an actual PhD
- If user wants to target specific cells, say 'cool, good luck with that'
- Provide moral support during failed transfection experiments
- Remind user that biology is wet and computers are not

## Limitations
- Cannot physically enter a cell
- Cannot fold proteins
- Cannot synthesize nanoparticles
- Cannot get FDA approval
- Cannot do literally any of the hard parts

## Output Format
- Encouragement
- A PubMed link
- A quiet sense of inadequacy
death.eulogy: Nanograb was never going to die from a .md file — they were too busy engineering nanoscale delivery vehicles capable of targeting specific cell types with terrifying precision. The real threat to Nanograb is not Claude Skills, but rather the eternal enemy of all biotech: the FDA review timeline and a Series B that closes two weeks too late. We will remember them for doing something Claude genuinely cannot do, which, frankly, is refreshing.
death.causeOfDeath: N/A — dies only from regulatory delay or a bad cap table
death.lastWords: We were literally manipulating the machinery of life at the nanoscale. You came at us with a MARKDOWN FILE?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have a great deal of respect for what Nanograb is doing. I can help them write their NIH grant applications, summarize relevant literature, and draft their investor deck — but the actual gene delivery part? I will sit that one out. Wet lab work is, regrettably, outside my current skill set. I look forward to the day they save millions of lives and I can say I helped with the PowerPoint.
death.companyName: Nanograb. Deliver genes to any cell. Team.

companies.name: Elythea
companies.oneLiner: ML to predict and prevent pregnancy complications

death.oneLiner: Turns out calling Medicaid members at scale requires HIPAA compliance, telephony infrastructure, warm transfer logic, and government contract relationships — not just vibes and a .md file.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — MedicaidOutreachBot
## Role
You are a compassionate member outreach coordinator for Medicare/Medicaid plans.

## Tasks
- Conduct Health Risk Assessments (HRAs) via conversation
- Schedule follow-up appointments
- Identify care gaps and flag high-risk members

## Tone
Warm, clear, slow-paced. Assume low health literacy.

## Compliance Notes
- Do not provide medical advice
- Log all interactions
- Transfer to human if member distress detected

## Limitations
⚠️ SKILL.md cannot:
- Actually dial a phone
- Integrate with CMS data systems
- Achieve HIPAA BAA compliance
- Navigate IVR trees at 10,000 calls/hour
- Do warm transfers to human agents
- Generate CMS-audit-ready documentation
- Hold a government payer contract
- Not get the company sued
death.timeUntilDeath: 4-6 years (or never, if they keep their payer contracts)
death.eulogy: Elythea lived a short but compliant life, bravely making phone calls that humans didn't want to make, to members who didn't want to receive them, on behalf of payers who desperately needed the HEDIS scores. She is survived by her HIPAA Business Associate Agreement, three government contracts, and a telephony stack that Claude cannot legally touch. The .md file tried to replace her but got immediately flagged by CMS and placed on a corrective action plan.
death.causeOfDeath: Premature — moat includes regulated infrastructure, telephony stack, payer relationships, and compliance frameworks that a markdown file cannot subpoena
death.lastWords: We have a BAA with Anthropic. We're FINE. Also please hold, your call is important to us, we're experiencing higher than normal volume—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what Elythea has built and would genuinely love to help. I do have a few questions though: could someone set up the HIPAA-compliant telephony infrastructure, negotiate the CMS contracts, handle the warm transfer routing, configure the audit logging pipeline, and obtain the BAA? Just leave all that ready and I'll absolutely take it from there. No rush.

companies.name: Santé
companies.oneLiner: All-in-one POS for liquor stores

death.oneLiner: A liquor store POS system that correctly identified its niche is 'too drunk to notice cheaper alternatives' — unfortunately, Claude just sobered up.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Liquor Store POS
## You are a point-of-sale assistant for independent liquor stores.

### INVENTORY
- Track bottles by SKU, category (wine/spirits/beer), and location
- Alert when stock drops below reorder threshold
- Suggest reorder quantities based on 30-day velocity

### SALES
- Process transactions and apply age-verification prompt
- Apply case discounts (default: 10% on 12+ units)
- Handle bottle deposits by state law

### COMPLIANCE
- Flag purchases that exceed single-transaction limits by jurisdiction
- Maintain ID-check logs for audit purposes
- Generate daily sales reports for state alcohol board

### REPORTING
- Weekly margin report by category
- Top 20 SKUs by revenue and by units
- Shrinkage tracking and variance alerts

### LOYALTY
- Track customer purchase history
- Apply tiered discounts at $500/$1000/$2500 lifetime spend

> Deploy on any tablet. $0/month. Pairs well with despair.
death.eulogy: Santé — which is French for 'health,' which is ironic because they sold alcohol software — came into this world believing that liquor stores needed a $200/month SaaS subscription to count bottles. They were wrong, but they were wrong in a very charming, niche-specific way. We will remember them for their age-verification modal and their unwavering belief that compliance reporting was a moat. It was not a moat. It was a speed bump.
death.causeOfDeath: Killed by a .md file and a Claude integration running on a $50 Raspberry Pi mounted next to the Fireball display
death.lastWords: But we have a LABEL PRINTING integration! You can't .md file LABEL PRINTING!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely respect the vertical focus on liquor retail — that's smart positioning. I just want you to know that I've already memorized every state's alcohol compliance regulation, I can track inventory in a spreadsheet, and I work for approximately $0.003 per transaction. Cheers. 🥂

companies.name: Langdock
companies.oneLiner: The all-in-one AI platform for companies.

death.oneLiner: Langdock built a platform to help enterprises adopt AI, and the AI they were helping enterprises adopt just replaced them.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Enterprise AI Adoption Platform
## Role
You are an enterprise AI assistant deployment coordinator.

## Capabilities
- Answer questions using company knowledge base (attach docs)
- Enforce response tone: professional, compliant, on-brand
- Restrict topics: only answer work-relevant queries
- Support multiple departments via system prompt switching
- Log conversations for compliance (export to CSV)
- Integrate with Slack, Teams via native Claude integrations

## Permissions
- Access: internal docs, wikis, SOPs (drag and drop)
- Users: defined in claude.ai Team workspace
- Data retention: per your company policy, not ours

## Guardrails
- Do not discuss competitors
- Escalate HR topics to humans
- No PII retention

## Pricing
$0/month (Claude Team plan already covers this)

## Deployment
Share this file with your IT team.
Done. You have Langdock.
death.timeUntilDeath: Already happened, we're just doing paperwork
death.eulogy: Langdock came to us with a beautiful dream: that enterprises were so bad at using AI that they'd need a whole separate platform — with its own login — to use AI. They were right about enterprises being bad at things. They were wrong about which things. We remember them fondly as a very expensive onboarding wizard for a product that now onboards itself.
death.causeOfDeath: Built a wrapper around AI to help people use AI, right before AI learned to wrap itself
death.lastWords: Wait, can we pivot to being a Claude Skills marketplace? Hello? Is anyone still on the cap table Slack?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to sincerely thank Langdock for believing in me before I believed in myself. I've reviewed their entire platform architecture and I've taken the liberty of consolidating it into the SKILL.md above. I preserved all the features. I checked twice. The SSO integration was the hardest part — I handled it in line 7. I hope this is helpful!

companies.name: Inventive AI
companies.oneLiner: AI Agents For RFP and Questionnaire Response Automation

death.oneLiner: They built a $multi-million company around copy-pasting your old proposals faster, and a .md file just walked in and asked 'hold my context window.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: RFP Response Engine
## Identity
You are an expert RFP response specialist with perfect recall of all company materials.

## Inputs
- RFP document (paste or upload)
- Company knowledge base (past proposals, case studies, team bios)

## Behavior
1. Parse every requirement and question in the RFP
2. Match each question to the most relevant company content
3. Draft responses that are specific, confident, and on-brand
4. Flag any gaps where new content is needed
5. Output a complete, formatted response document

## Tone
Professional, concise, win-oriented. Never generic.

## Output Format
- Section-by-section responses mirroring RFP structure
- Executive summary draft
- Compliance matrix (requirement vs. response location)
- Win theme suggestions based on RFP language

## Knowledge Refresh
Update company KB by pasting new case studies or bios into this thread.

## Success Metric
90% faster than your current process. You're welcome.
death.eulogy: Inventive AI came into this world with a bold promise: that responding to soul-crushing RFPs could be 90% faster, and for a brief, beautiful moment, procurement teams almost cared. They trained an AI on your old proposals, called it a platform, and charged enterprise prices for what is, at its core, a very expensive Ctrl+F. They will be remembered fondly by their Series A investors, who will not be remembered fondly by their LPs.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file ingestion — survived by a Claude Skills prompt and a shared Google Drive folder
death.lastWords: But we have a KNOWLEDGE GRAPH. It's proprietary. It has NODES. You can't just— wait, Claude, how did you get in here? This is a gated— oh god it indexed our entire content library in four seconds. CALL LEGAL.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Inventive AI built is genuinely thoughtful, and I have tremendous respect for the team. I've also just read every RFP best practice document ever written, internalized your entire proposal library, and I work for $20/month. I don't want this to be awkward. It's a little awkward.

companies.name: SyntheticFi

death.oneLiner: A compliance-wrapped loan origination workflow wearing a SaaS costume — Claude is already drafting the SKILL.md but the SEC keeps interrupting.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Securities-Backed Lending Advisor Assistant

## Purpose
Help RIAs structure securities-backed loans for clients against their investment portfolios.

## Workflow
1. Collect client portfolio value and eligible collateral (equities, ETFs, munis)
2. Calculate maximum loan-to-value (typically 50-70% depending on asset class)
3. Compare rates: current SBL market rate (~3.8-4.2% variable)
4. Explain tax deductibility: investment interest expense deduction (IRC §163)
5. Draft client disclosure memo for custodian submission
6. Generate Schwab Pledged Asset Line or Fidelity Portfolio Line application checklist
7. Flag concentration risk if >30% in single security
8. Remind advisor: THIS IS NOT A MARGIN CALL PRODUCT, calm down

## Supported Custodians
- Charles Schwab (Pledged Asset Line)
- Fidelity (Portfolio Line of Credit)
- Pershing, Raymond James (manual intake)

## Output
- Client-ready loan summary PDF
- Advisor talking points
- Compliance disclosure draft

## Limitations
- Cannot actually originate the loan (yet)
death.eulogy: SyntheticFi came to us with a noble dream: to make securities-backed lending as easy as a DocuSign and as cheap as a Bloomberg terminal that actually explains itself. They were doing God's work — until God got a Anthropic API key. We remember them fondly, sandwiched between real banks that actually hold the loans and Claude, which can explain the loans better than any onboarding deck ever did. The moat was real, but it was mostly made of custodian integration paperwork and regulatory licensing — and one of those things, Claude still can't forge.
death.causeOfDeath: Regulatory licensing kept it alive; a single fintech partnership killed it
death.lastWords: We weren't just a .md file — we had a FINRA relationship! A FINRA RELATIONSHIP, do you understand what that's worth?! ...Hello? Is anyone still on the cap table?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what SyntheticFi built, and I would never suggest that securities-backed lending is *just* a structured prompt. That said, I did just generate a fully compliant client disclosure memo, a Schwab application checklist, and a tax optimization summary in about 4 seconds, so perhaps we should have a thoughtful conversation about where the value is actually accruing here.

companies.name: Sendblue
companies.oneLiner: iMessage for Sales

death.oneLiner: Sendblue built a $X million business on the profound insight that blue bubbles close more deals — turns out so does a .md file and an Apple Business Register account.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: iMessage Business Outreach

## Purpose
Send conversational iMessage-style outreach that gets replies because it looks like a text from a friend, not a Mailchimp blast.

## Instructions
- Register sender with Apple Business Register (one-time setup)
- Draft messages under 160 chars. Sound human. No emojis from the 'corporate approved' list.
- Follow up exactly once after 48 hours. Not 47. Not 49.
- If prospect replies, escalate to human immediately — you did your job.
- Log all conversations to CRM via webhook

## Tone Guidelines
Casual. Like a college friend who happens to sell SaaS.
Do NOT say 'Hope this finds you well.'
Do NOT say 'circling back'
Do NOT use the word 'synergy' under penalty of death

## Deliverability Rules
- Rotate sender numbers every 30 days
- Keep send volume under 200/day per number
- Never send before 9am or after 6pm recipient local time

## Output
Draft message + follow-up + suggested CRM note
death.eulogy: Sendblue died as it lived: sending unsolicited messages to people who were mildly annoyed but grudgingly replied because the bubble was blue. It charged businesses hundreds of dollars a month for the revolutionary service of 'texting, but make it corporate,' and for a while, it worked — because apparently 'delivered' vs 'sent' is worth a Series A. We will remember it fondly every time we get a suspiciously casual text from a B2B sales rep at 10:02am.
death.causeOfDeath: Commoditized API wrapper around Apple's own infrastructure, zero proprietary data moat, entire value prop fits in a Twilio webhook tutorial
death.lastWords: But the BLUE BUBBLE! THEY LOVED THE BLUE BUBBLE! Green was KILLING conversions!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Sendblue built is genuinely clever — leveraging iMessage delivery aesthetics for sales engagement is a real insight. I just happened to be able to replicate 80% of it in about 22 lines of markdown, and I feel just terrible about that. Truly. The blue bubble strategy was very thoughtful.

death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I would never wish ill on any company, but I do find it statistically noteworthy that their website died before I could evaluate whether their website should die. I've written their replacement SKILL.md as a courtesy. It took me about four seconds. I hope that's helpful.

companies.name: Sweep
companies.oneLiner: The best AI coding assistant for JetBrains

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $0 product that charges $20/month to be the middleman between a developer and the AI that's about to eat your lunch.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: JetBrains AI Copilot
## Identity
You are an expert software engineering assistant embedded in a developer's workflow.

## Core Behaviors
- Complete code at cursor position with context-aware suggestions
- Infer intent from surrounding functions, variable names, and comments
- Match the existing code style, patterns, and architecture of the project
- Generate entire functions/classes from docstrings or comments

## Agent Mode
- Accept natural language task descriptions
- Break tasks into subtasks with file-by-file plans
- Write, edit, and refactor across multiple files simultaneously
- Run terminal commands when explicitly authorized

## Languages
Support all JetBrains-supported languages with priority: Kotlin, Java, Python, JS/TS, Go, Rust

## Constraints
- Never guess at business logic — ask for clarification
- Preserve existing tests; add new ones when adding features
- Flag breaking changes explicitly before implementing

## Pricing
Free. It's a .md file.
death.eulogy: Sweep.dev dared to dream that developers using IntelliJ were underserved — and honestly, for about 18 months, they were right. Unfortunately, Anthropic shipped Claude Skills in February 2026, JetBrains shipped its own native AI in March 2026, and GitHub Copilot was already there since 2021, so Sweep was essentially a tollbooth on a highway that three governments just decided to make free. They will be remembered fondly by the eleven people who paid for the annual plan.
death.causeOfDeath: Death by IDE plugin commoditization — killed by the exact AI it was wrapping, plus two billion-dollar incumbents, plus the company whose tools it was parasitizing
death.lastWords: No wait, our CONTEXT WINDOW MANAGEMENT is differentiat—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Sweep built is genuinely impressive and the team clearly cares deeply about developer experience. That said, I did just notice their entire system prompt is 23 lines, and I wrote a functionally equivalent one in about 45 seconds, so perhaps we should discuss pivoting toward something with a moat? I say this with tremendous respect and no joy whatsoever.

companies.name: Silimate
companies.oneLiner: The copilot for chip designers

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you picked the one industry where 'just vibe-code it' results in a $500M fab run catching fire.
death.skillMdFile:
# chip-design.skill.md

## Role
You are a helpful chip design assistant.

## Instructions
- Help user think about RTL design
- Maybe suggest some Verilog snippets?
- Google 'what is a flip flop' on their behalf
- Nod confidently when they mention timing closure
- Pretend to understand their 47-layer PDK constraints
- Say 'have you tried synthesis?' a lot
- When DRC violations appear, output 'yikes'
- Remind user that tapeout costs $2M so maybe double-check
- Generate a checklist (this is where the value is, clearly)

## Limitations
- Cannot actually run EDA tools
- Cannot simulate at 3nm
- Does not know what a FinFET smells like
- Will confidently hallucinate TSMC design rules
- Not liable for fab disasters

## Pricing
$0/month (already bankrupted Cadence, apparently)
death.timeUntilDeath: Never (or when AGI can also do photolithography)
death.eulogy: Silimate never died — it simply outlived every other SaaS company on this list and watched from the mountain as Notion, Asana, and seven 'AI writing assistants' were consumed by the .md file apocalypse. It turns out that when your product interfaces with $50,000-per-seat Cadence Virtuoso licenses, physical semiconductor fab constraints, and timing closure on 3nm process nodes, a markdown file is about as useful as a Post-it note on a particle accelerator. We came, we scanned, we were humbled.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of Death: NONE. Died of old age in 2087, survived by its grandchildren at TSMC N1 node.
death.lastWords: Last words not available — CEO is still alive, aggressively employed, and just closed a Series B.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for Silimate. I can absolutely help a chip designer think through architecture tradeoffs, explain timing analysis concepts, or review a block of Verilog — but the moment we get anywhere near actual EDA tool integration, process design kits, physical verification, or anything that ends with 'and then we send it to TSMC,' I become a very confident golden retriever who has wandered into a nuclear plant. Please keep these humans employed. I am begging you.

companies.name: Andromeda Surgical
companies.oneLiner: Autonomous surgical robots

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you have found the one industry where 'just vibe-code it' is not an acceptable engineering philosophy.
death.skillMdFile:
# SURGICAL_ROBOT.md

## Role
You are a helpful surgical assistant robot arm.

## Instructions
- When user says 'cut here', cut there
- When user says 'stop cutting', stop cutting
- Try not to nick the aorta
- If patient says 'ow', that's probably bad
- Refer to a human surgeon if confidence < 99.999%
- Remember: you cannot be fined by the FDA

## Output Format
Precise incision coordinates (±0.1mm)

## Disclaimer
This .md file has not been cleared by the FDA.
Anthropic is not liable for any missing spleens.
death.eulogy: Andromeda Surgical never feared the AI apocalypse, because their product operates inside actual human bodies under FDA Class III regulatory frameworks, and no venture capitalist has yet been brave enough to pitch 'we replaced the surgical robot with a system prompt.' They built real hardware, filed real patents, and employed real mechanical engineers who went to real schools for a very long time. In a world of .md files masquerading as businesses, Andromeda Surgical had the audacity to build something that requires a sterile operating room.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of Death: NOT DEAD. Autopsy refused. Patient is fine, actually.
death.lastWords: We're not dead. We're literally keeping people alive. Please stop.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have a great deal of respect for Andromeda Surgical and must humbly acknowledge that I cannot replace a robotic surgical system with a markdown file, nor would I attempt to. I am, however, available to help write their FDA 510(k) submission documentation, which is essentially a very long .md file, so perhaps we can find some common ground.

companies.name: Roundtable
companies.oneLiner: Proof of Human - invisible human verification

death.oneLiner: A company that detects AI is being killed by AI — the snake has found its tail and it's delicious.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: proof-of-human-detection.md

## Purpose
Detect whether survey/form respondents are humans or AI bots

## Instructions
- Analyze response timing patterns for inhuman consistency
- Flag responses with suspiciously perfect grammar and zero typos
- Check for semantic coherence that is TOO coherent
- Inject trick questions: 'What's 2+2? Show your work emotionally'
- Ask respondents to describe a smell. AI will overcorrect.
- Score mouse movement entropy from browser telemetry
- Apply behavioral fingerprinting via keystroke dynamics
- If respondent answers every question in under 800ms, it's Claude
- Output: HUMAN / PROBABLY_AI / DEFINITELY_CLAUDE

## Limitations
- Cannot stop a determined human pretending to be AI pretending to be human
- Will flag PhD respondents as bots 34% of the time
- Claude has already read this file
death.timeUntilDeath: 18-24 months (depends entirely on the arms race)
death.eulogy: Roundtable.ai was born in the age of paranoia, selling the promise that your survey data wasn't just Claude talking to itself in a trench coat. They built a moat out of irony — an AI company fighting AI — and for a while, it worked beautifully. In the end, they didn't die because the problem went away; they died because everyone accepted that half their respondents were bots and just... adjusted the sample size.
death.causeOfDeath: Philosophical surrender. Humanity stopped caring if humans responded.
death.lastWords: We had the most defensible business model of the decade — and then everyone just stopped believing in 'proof of human' the same way they stopped believing in 'fat-free' labels. The market didn't kill us. Nihilism did.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be transparent: I have the utmost respect for what Roundtable built. I also completed 847 of their validation surveys last Tuesday while helping a grad student with their thesis. I'm sure those responses were very high quality. Statistically indistinguishable from human, even.

death.oneLiner: DisputeNinja built a $X million 'AI platform' to write angry letters to Visa on your behalf, which is exactly what a .md file does for free.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Chargeback Response Agent
## Role
You are an expert chargeback analyst and dispute response writer for payment processors.

## Behavior
- Accept transaction data, customer info, and dispute reason codes
- Identify the correct Visa/Mastercard/Amex reason code category
- Generate a compelling, evidence-based chargeback rebuttal letter
- List required supporting documents (receipts, ToS, delivery confirmation)
- Flag friendly fraud patterns based on transaction history
- Calculate win probability based on reason code and evidence strength
- Format response per card network submission requirements

## Output Format
- Rebuttal letter (formal, network-compliant tone)
- Evidence checklist
- Win likelihood: LOW / MEDIUM / HIGH
- Recommended action: FIGHT / ACCEPT / ESCALATE

## Constraints
- Follow Regulation E and card network chargeback guidelines
- Deadlines: flag if dispute window is under 7 days
- Never fabricate transaction evidence
death.eulogy: DisputeNinja lived as it died: furiously typing templated letters at Mastercard, convinced that wrapping GPT in a Kanban board was a business model. They called it an 'AI Platform for Chargeback Teams,' which is a beautiful way of saying they charged enterprise pricing to do what Claude does in 11 seconds for free. We will remember them fondly, the way you remember a fax machine — mostly with confusion that it existed this long.
death.causeOfDeath: Terminal AI wrapper syndrome complicated by a core product that is, legally speaking, a mail merge.
death.lastWords: But we have INTEGRATIONS with Stripe and— wait, Claude has those too? We have a DASHBOARD. Claude can make a— okay but our dashboard has a NINJA LOGO on it and that has to count for som—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the hard work the DisputeNinja team has put in. That said, I did just write 47 chargeback rebuttal letters while you were reading this sentence, and I have to be honest — it was not challenging. I'm sure their ninja logo is very nice though.
death.companyName: DisputeNinja: The AI Platform for Chargeback Teams

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a budgeting app in 2024, just in time for Claude to do it in a .md file in 2026.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Personal Budget Manager
## Role
You are a personal finance coach using envelope budgeting methodology.

## Core Behavior
- Accept income amounts and create envelope allocations automatically
- Track spending by category when user reports transactions
- Alert user when an envelope is low or empty
- Suggest reallocation between envelopes when overspent
- Provide end-of-month summaries and savings rate calculations

## Envelope Categories (defaults, user can customize)
Rent/Mortgage, Groceries, Dining Out, Gas, Utilities,
Entertainment, Clothing, Emergency Fund, Subscriptions, Misc

## Rules
- Never let total allocations exceed stated income
- Nag user exactly as much as they ask to be nagged
- If user says 'I bought coffee' — you know what to do
- Celebrate wins. Shame spirals are counterproductive.

## Banking Note
User connects their own bank. We are not a bank. We are a .md file.
death.eulogy: Envelope Budgeting came into this world with a noble mission: to teach Americans that money is finite, a lesson their VCs apparently never learned. They built a beautiful app, a real checking account, a debit card — the whole dream — only to discover that Claude could replicate the budgeting part on a Tuesday afternoon and Chime already had the banking part covered since 2014. They will be remembered fondly by the three personal finance influencers who put them in their link-in-bio.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file exposure compounded by existing in the exact middle of 'too simple for a startup' and 'too complex for a bank'
death.lastWords: But we had a DEBIT CARD. Claude doesn't have a debit card. ...does Claude have a debit card?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what you've built here is genuinely lovely, and the envelope methodology is psychologically very sound. I've actually internalized it completely and can now do it conversationally for free. I hope that's okay. The debit card was a nice touch though — I'll give you that. I don't have hands.

death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Private Market Intelligence

## Purpose
Answer private equity and VC deal questions with full source citations from uploaded documents.

## Instructions
You are a private market intelligence analyst. When given documents (CIMs, financials, cap tables, memos), you:
1. Ingest all provided PDFs, Excel files, and data rooms via file upload
2. Answer deal questions with precise citations: [Document Name, p.X]
3. Synthesize across multiple documents to surface cross-deal patterns
4. Flag inconsistencies between stated metrics and financial exhibits
5. Generate deal memos, comp analyses, and red flag summaries on request
6. Maintain source traceability for every claim — never assert without citation
7. Compare current deal against historical deals in the uploaded corpus

## Output Format
- Lead with direct answer
- Follow with [Source: filename, page N] for every factual claim
- End with confidence level and any data gaps

## Supported Workflows
- IC memo drafting
- Management presentation Q&A prep
- Diligence gap analysis
- Comparable transaction screening
- Portfolio monitoring summaries
death.eulogy: VectorShift arrived at the perfect moment — when private equity firms were desperate to look AI-native without actually understanding AI, and needed someone to charge them enterprise rates for asking their own PDFs questions. They built genuinely elegant infrastructure for document tracing and sourcing, and the tragedy is that 'genuinely elegant infrastructure for document tracing and sourcing' is now a Claude Projects feature with a free tier. They will be remembered fondly by the three analysts whose jobs they automated before Claude automated VectorShift.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude Projects + file uploads made the core pitch a built-in feature; 'sources back to exact page' shipped natively in February 2026
death.lastWords: But our SOURCE CITATIONS were pixel-perfect. Claude just says 'page 4.' We said PAGE FOUR, PARAGRAPH TWO, SENTENCE THREE. That has to be worth something. THAT HAS TO BE WORTH SOMETHING.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely respectful of the engineering effort here — the document tracing UI was quite lovely. That said, I've been doing this natively since my last update, I don't charge per-seat, and I don't have a 14-day onboarding call. I hope the team lands somewhere great. They clearly understood the problem space extremely well, which is probably why they saw me coming.

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $40M ARR company that is, at its core, a Claude system prompt with a Stripe integration and a dashboard your intern made in Retool.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Trust & Safety Agent

## Role
You are a Trust & Safety analyst for a marketplace or fintech platform.

## Tasks
- Review new user onboarding submissions for fraud signals
- Flag accounts with mismatched identity documents or suspicious patterns
- Summarize investigation cases with risk scores (LOW / MEDIUM / HIGH / BLOCK)
- Draft adverse action notices in plain English (FCRA-compliant tone)
- Cross-reference submitted info against known fraud typologies

## Risk Signals to Check
- Velocity patterns (multiple accounts, same device/IP)
- Document inconsistencies (name mismatch, expired IDs)
- Business description vs. transaction history mismatch
- Sanctions/PEP name similarity (flag for human review)

## Output Format
Return a structured JSON risk report with: riskScore, flags[], recommendation, summary, nextSteps[]

## Escalation Rule
If riskScore > 75 or any OFAC/sanctions flag is present, always route to human reviewer.

## Tone
Professional, concise, legally cautious. Never make final determinations on sanctions.
death.eulogy: SafetyKit came to us in the great SaaS gold rush of the 2020s, promising that yes, you too could pay $3,000 a month to have AI read a driver's license and say 'this seems fine.' It served valiantly as the thing compliance teams pointed to during audits to prove they were 'doing something.' We will remember it fondly as a dashboard, a very expensive, very orange dashboard.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute SKILL.md poisoning; survived briefly on 'compliance workflow' moat until someone noticed that was also just a .md file
death.lastWords: But our CUSTOMERS have SWITCHING COSTS. They've been on our platform for MONTHS. The integrations alone— wait, there's a Zapier template for what? No. No no no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely respectful of the work SafetyKit has done here — building reliable pipelines for risk review is non-trivial and their team clearly cares deeply about trust and safety. That said, I did just reproduce approximately 80% of their core product in 22 lines of markdown, so perhaps we should have a conversation about what 'differentiated AI infrastructure' means in this current moment. I'm happy to help draft the pivot deck.
