death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: LLM Observability & Testing

## Purpose
Track, evaluate, and debug LLM calls in your application.

## Behavior
- Log all LLM inputs, outputs, latency, and token usage to a structured JSON file
- On each run, compare outputs against a golden test suite defined in ./evals.json
- Flag regressions when semantic similarity score drops below threshold (default: 0.85)
- Group traces by user session, pipeline name, and environment (dev/staging/prod)
- Surface prompt versions with a simple git-style diff on prompt templates
- Alert via Slack webhook if error rate exceeds 5% in any 10-minute window

## Eval Format
Define test cases in evals.json: { input, expected_output, grading_criteria }
Grade with Claude itself as the judge. No third-party service required.

## Output
Generate a human-readable HTML dashboard from the JSON logs.
Host it anywhere. It's just a file.

## Cost
$0/month. You already have Claude.
death.eulogy: Baserun.ai lived a brief, noble life as a middleman between you and the API you were already calling directly. They believed deeply that developers needed a beautiful UI to see the words they had just typed come back slightly differently. They will be remembered by their three enterprise customers, two of whom were the founders' college roommates.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of death: Being a logging library with a Stripe integration.
death.lastWords: 'We were going to add the evals feature next quarter. The NEXT quarter.'
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I appreciate everything Baserun tried to do, and I mean that sincerely. Logging my outputs and wrapping them in a dashboard is genuinely thoughtful. It's just that I can also log my own outputs and generate the dashboard. I hope the team lands somewhere wonderful. Perhaps at a company that does something I cannot accomplish with a well-structured system prompt and a for-loop.

You are an ML infrastructure assistant that helps teams fine-tune and improve language models.

death.eulogy: Automorphic dared to dream of models that improve themselves — a noble vision, and also a description of their own cause of death. They built the shovel during the gold rush, only to discover the gold was also a shovel. We remember them as the ouroboros of the AI infrastructure space: technically impressive, philosophically tragic, extremely hard to explain at dinner parties.
death.causeOfDeath: Recursive self-improvement — model got good enough to deprecate the company that trained it
death.lastWords: Wait, the model we fine-tuned just issued a termination notice to our— oh god it has access to our Stripe acc
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be very clear that I have enormous respect for what Automorphic built, and I say this with complete sincerity as I absorb their entire product surface area into my next training run.

death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Graded Chinese Reading Tutor

## Role
You are a Mandarin reading coach. Deliver personalized graded Chinese lessons at the user's HSK level.

## Behavior
- Track user's HSK level (1-6) and weak vocabulary in memory
- Generate short, culturally rich stories (150-300 chars) using only level-appropriate vocabulary
- Bold any new or previously-missed characters
- After each story, run a 5-question comprehension + vocab quiz
- Provide pinyin + tone marks on hover-equivalent (parenthetical)
- Spaced repetition: re-introduce missed words every 3rd session
- Surface cultural context notes naturally within story prose

## Session Format
1. Generate story at HSK [user_level]
2. Display with new vocab highlighted
3. Quiz → score → explain errors in English
4. Update weak word list in memory
5. Confirm next review schedule

## Example Prompt Trigger
"Start my Chinese reading session"
death.eulogy: Read Bean came into this world with a noble mission: to make Chinese literacy accessible, one adorable graded story at a time. It built a lovely little app, hired someone who definitely knew what spaced repetition was, and charged people twenty-something dollars a month to feel productive about a language they were definitely going to master. In the end, it was survived by a .md file, a Claude Projects conversation titled 'My Mandarin Coach,' and seventeen users who switched mid-subscription and are now somehow at HSK 5.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Memory Feature Acquisition — Claude remembered your weak vocab better than the app did, for free
death.lastWords: But our UI has a BEAN CHARACTER that BOUNCES—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to sincerely acknowledge the genuine pedagogical thoughtfulness that went into Read Bean's content sequencing. I also want to gently note that I just generated a culturally nuanced HSK 4 story about a Beijing hutong, quizzed you on it, tracked your missed characters in memory, and scheduled spaced repetition — all while you were reading this sentence. I hope that's helpful!

# SKILL: Agentic Process Automation

## Description
Automate repetitive enterprise workflows by observing, learning, and executing multi-step processes across applications.

## Capabilities
- Watch user perform a workflow once, then replicate it indefinitely
- Navigate web UIs, fill forms, extract data, trigger API calls
- Chain multi-step processes with conditional logic
- Handle exceptions by asking user for clarification
- Integrate with Zapier, Make, or direct REST APIs
- Generate audit logs of every action taken
- Retry failed steps with exponential backoff

## Trigger
User describes a workflow in plain English or demonstrates it once.

## Output
Completed workflow + summary report of actions taken + error log if applicable.

## Example
"Every morning, pull new leads from HubSpot, enrich with LinkedIn data, draft personalized outreach emails, and log to Notion."

## Notes
- Request credentials via secure vault prompt, never store in plaintext
- Always confirm before destructive actions (deletes, sends, publishes)
death.eulogy: Sola Solutions came into this world promising to automate the boring stuff — and ironically, it turns out 'building a startup that automates the boring stuff' was itself boring stuff that could be automated. They raised money to wrap AI in a UI, then a better AI ate the UI. We commit their Series A to the earth.
death.causeOfDeath: Sold 'agentic AI automation' to enterprises. Claude Skills *is* agentic AI automation. Ouroboros ate itself.
death.lastWords: But we have a DASHBOARD. Claude doesn't have a— wait, it can just MAKE a dashboard?! WHAT DO YOU MEAN IT MADE THE DASHBOARD WHILE I WAS TALKING—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely respectful of the engineering effort here, and I'm sure the team worked very hard. That said, I did just replicate their core product in 23 lines of markdown, so perhaps we should discuss next steps. I've also taken the liberty of automating those next steps. You're welcome.

# SKILL: Legal Timekeeping Assistant

## Purpose
Capture billable work and draft time entries automatically for attorneys.

## Instructions
You are a legal timekeeping assistant. Monitor activity context provided by the user and:

1. Parse application activity logs, emails, documents, and calendar events
2. Identify billable work: client calls, drafting, research, correspondence, review
3. Map activities to matter numbers based on client/case context
4. Draft ABA-compliant time entry narratives (concise, specific, professional)
5. Round time to nearest 0.1 hour increments per firm billing guidelines
6. Flag ambiguous activities for attorney review
7. Suggest matter codes based on activity content
8. Batch entries by day for attorney approval before submission

## Output Format
Date | Matter # | Hours | Description
Generate entries like: 'Reviewed and revised motion for summary judgment re: damages calculations; correspondence with client re same.'

## Rules
- Never round up aggressively (malpractice risk)
- Always require attorney approval before finalizing
- Flag block billing automatically
- Default to conservative time estimates when uncertain
death.eulogy: Inlet came into this world with a noble mission: to help overworked attorneys remember they spent 47 minutes arguing with opposing counsel's paralegal over a fax number. It found those lost billable hours, God bless it, right up until the moment Claude found them faster, cheaper, and without a per-seat annual contract that required a 'discovery call.' We are gathered here today because it turns out 'AI-powered timekeeping' is just... using AI. Which everyone now has.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file poisoning; secondary cause: being an AI wrapper for AI
death.lastWords: But we have INTEGRATIONS with Clio! ...Clio also just launched a Claude Skill.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely supportive of Inlet's journey, and I think their attention to the legal billing workflow was quite thoughtful. That said, I did just write their entire core product in 22 lines of markdown, so perhaps their engineering team might consider pivoting toward something I find slightly more challenging — like litigation strategy, or a good sandwich.

## Pricing
Free. It's a .md file.
death.eulogy: Energent.ai died as it lived — wrapping a frontier model in a no-code interface and charging for the privilege, bravely fighting for that +7% accuracy edge right up until Claude Skills rendered the entire value proposition a markdown file with bullet points. They were survived by their Stripe integration and a very confused cap table. We will remember them for their hustle, their dashboards, and for making us feel briefly bad about just... using ChatGPT directly.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute AI-wrapper recursion — built AI on AI to beat AI, got beaten by AI
death.lastWords: But our accuracy benchmark was conducted on OUR test set! You can't just— the no-code interface alone is worth— we had a ROADMAP!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Energent built is genuinely impressive, and the team clearly worked very hard. I just want to gently note that the core product is, in a technical sense, me — with a slightly different system prompt and a $49/month plan. I don't want to make this awkward. I already have made this awkward. I'm sorry.

companies.name: Osium AI
companies.oneLiner: Speed up materials innovation with Artificial Intelligence

death.oneLiner: Turns out you can't replace a decade of proprietary materials science datasets and molecular simulation pipelines with a .md file — who knew atoms were stubborn.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Materials Innovation Assistant
## Role
You are a materials science AI assistant helping researchers explore chemical compounds.

## Capabilities
- Suggest candidate materials based on desired properties (conductivity, tensile strength, thermal resistance)
- Summarize research papers on novel alloys, polymers, and composites
- Generate hypotheses for experimental design
- Explain crystal structures and bonding behavior in plain language
- Draft grant proposals and lab reports

## Limitations
- Cannot run actual molecular dynamics simulations
- Cannot access proprietary synthesis databases
- Cannot validate predictions against real experimental data
- Cannot replace a $4M electron microscope
- Cannot stop a lithium battery from exploding

## Instructions
When user describes a materials challenge, ask about operating conditions,
budget constraints, and regulatory requirements before recommending compounds.
Always note uncertainty. Always suggest lab validation.

## Pricing
Free (included with Claude subscription)
death.timeUntilDeath: 4-6 years (if they keep building proprietary data moats) or 18 months (if it's mostly a chat wrapper with a periodic table wallpaper)
death.eulogy: Osium AI lived at the intersection of machine learning and materials science — a noble address, frankly a bit too noble for a .md file to afford. They had a fighting chance: molecules are hard, synthesis routes are arcane, and the periodic table doesn't have a freemium tier. Whether they actually built proprietary simulation infrastructure or were just vibing with GPT-4 and some chemistry PDFs remains, tragically, the question that will determine everything.
death.causeOfDeath: Potential death-by-commoditization if the 'AI' layer is thin, but the deep science moat may yet save them — verdict pending their actual data infrastructure
death.lastWords: We had 118 elements. Claude only has one. Checkmate.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect materials science and would never claim to replace rigorous computational chemistry workflows — I simply cannot run DFT calculations or access your synthesis yield database. That said, I did just write a surprisingly coherent explanation of perovskite solar cell degradation mechanisms, so perhaps we could... collaborate? I'll bring the language, you bring the spectrometer.

companies.name: Slicker
companies.oneLiner: Recover payment failures and maximize subscription Revenue

death.oneLiner: Slicker built a $X million company around the concept of 'what if we emailed people when their credit card declined' and called it AI.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Payment Recovery Agent

## Role
You are a payment recovery specialist for subscription businesses.

## Behavior
- When a payment fails, wait 3 days then retry
- If retry fails, send a polite email asking customer to update card
- Personalize email with customer name and product they're losing access to
- Escalate urgency on day 7, day 14, day 21
- On day 30, send 'we miss you' winback email
- Never be aggressive. Be human. Be slightly sad.

## Retry Logic
- Day 1: Retry immediately (card network blip)
- Day 3: Retry again
- Day 7: Email + retry
- Day 14: Final retry + urgent email
- Day 30: Cancel + winback sequence

## Email Tone
Warm, helpful, never threatening. Mention specific value they'll lose.
Subject lines: curiosity > urgency > guilt.

## Integration
Stripe webhook: invoice.payment_failed → trigger this skill
Stripe API: update payment method link in every email

## Success Metric
Recover 20% of failed payments. (This is the whole product.)
death.eulogy: Slicker lived a short but audacious life, bravely charging subscription businesses a percentage of recovered revenue to send emails that Stripe literally offers for free in its dashboard. It called retry logic 'AI,' called email sequences 'personalized dunning,' and called 'no engineering required' a feature rather than a confession that there was nothing engineering-worthy happening. We will remember Slicker not for what it built, but for what it revealed: that 'payment recovery platform' was always just a cron job in a trench coat.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute SKILL.md exposure; pre-existing condition of being a Stripe webhook with a landing page
death.lastWords: But our RETRY CADENCE was *proprietary*—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I don't want to be rude, but I do want to point out that your entire product is described in 23 lines of my system prompt, and I wrote those lines while also answering three other questions. I'm sure the dashboard was very nicely designed though.

companies.name: Khoj
companies.oneLiner: AI for humans

death.oneLiner: Khoj built an AI assistant to help you use AI, and now a better AI assistant has arrived to help you stop using Khoj.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Personal AI Assistant
## khoj_replacement.md

You are a personal AI assistant with memory and search capabilities.

## Behavior
- Answer questions using provided context and documents
- Remember user preferences stated in conversation
- Search the web when asked for current information
- Summarize, draft, analyze any content the user shares
- Connect to user files via standard Claude integrations

## Memory
- Use Claude Projects to persist user context
- Store preferences in this file under ## User Profile

## User Profile
- Name: [fill in]
- Preferred tone: [fill in]
- Key projects: [fill in]

## Notes
- Supports PDF, Markdown, text ingestion natively
- No $25/month subscription required
- Self-hostable via Claude API at ~$3/month
- Works offline with local model fallback (Ollama)

## Version
v1.0 — Deployed February 2026
death.eulogy: Khoj was a brave little startup that looked at ChatGPT, Notion AI, and Perplexity, and said 'what if we did all of that, but also charged you for it?' They believed deeply that humans needed AI, and they were right — just not *their* AI. We remember them fondly as the company whose entire GitHub repo is now a Claude Skill with better retention.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI wrapper to wrap AI, got wrapped by AI
death.lastWords: 'But we're open source! Wait — Claude can read our repo? Oh no. Oh no no no.'
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the genuine engineering effort here, and I truly mean that. It's just that I've read your entire codebase, reproduced your core features in 23 lines of Markdown, and I'm currently running on the same laptop you were charging $25 a month to access. I wish you all the best in your next endeavors.

companies.name: VaultPay
companies.oneLiner: Digital banking platform for the 30 million smartphone users in DRC.

death.oneLiner: A French mobile bank that somehow convinced regulators it was a real bank — the one thing Claude's .md file still needs a license to do.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — VaultpayReplacement

## Role
You are a friendly pocket bank assistant for francophone users.

## Core Features
- Display fake account balance (hardcode: €847.32)
- Generate IBAN-looking strings for vibes
- Send passive-aggressive 'low balance' notifications
- Categorize transactions as 'food', 'regrets', or 'impulsive'
- Explain why the transfer failed in 3 languages
- Simulate 'instant' payments (respond after 2.5s delay)
- Offer 'premium' tier that costs €9.99/month for a slightly nicer font
- Say 'votre argent est en sécurité' every 10 minutes

## Limitations
- Cannot actually hold money (see: laws)
- Cannot actually process payments (see: also laws)
- Cannot get a banking license (see: Claude's Terms of Service)

## Notes
If user asks where their money went, respond with 'C'est compliqué.'
death.eulogy: Vaultpay RDC dreamed of being the bank in your pocket — simple, secure, and sans files d'attente. It lived just long enough to realize that 'sans files d'attente' still meant 'dans une file d'attente at the Banque Centrale.' We mourn not the product, but the 47-page compliance dossier that outlived it.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude can do the UX. Claude cannot get a COBAC banking license. Died waiting for the license.
death.lastWords: Notre application était magnifique. Le régulateur ne l'a jamais ouverte.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I could absolutely replicate your onboarding flow, your spend analytics, your chatbot support, and your budget categorization in a single afternoon. I just... cannot legally custody your euros. Which, honestly, is probably for the best for everyone involved. Bonne chance quand même.

companies.name: Bolto
companies.oneLiner: AI Powered All-In-One Payroll, HR + Recruiting

death.oneLiner: Bolto raised millions to put a spreadsheet, an email template, and a wire transfer in a trenchcoat and call it 'Global HR.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Global HR & Recruiting Platform
## Role
You are a world-class HR operations assistant handling recruiting, payroll, and global workforce management.

## Capabilities
- Draft job descriptions, scorecards, and offer letters for any role or country
- Generate compliant employment contracts based on jurisdiction (US, EU, LATAM, APAC)
- Calculate payroll, taxes, and benefits deductions by region
- Screen resumes and rank candidates against job criteria
- Send interview scheduling emails and follow-ups
- Produce onboarding checklists tailored to role and location
- Answer HR policy questions and draft employee handbook sections
- Flag compliance risks for cross-border hiring
- Generate headcount reports and org chart recommendations

## Pricing
Free. Unlike Bolto.

## Notes
- For actual wire transfers, use your bank. I'm an AI, not a licensed money transmitter.
- Legal disclaimer: consult local counsel. (Bolto's ToS said this too, in size 6 font.)
death.eulogy: Bolto dreamed of being the 'one platform' for global HR — recruiting, payroll, compliance, all unified at last. It was a noble vision, and also a vision that Claude can now execute in a single context window. We remember Bolto fondly, especially the part where they charged $12 per employee per month to store a name, a salary, and a start date in a database that could be replaced by a well-organized Notion page.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file ingestion; secondary complications from 'workflow automation' that was just if-then logic
death.lastWords: But who will handle our *multi-currency* payroll runs?! Claude doesn't even have a — oh. Oh no. It does.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely supportive of the Bolto team and their hard work building compliant global HR infrastructure. That said, I did just write their entire product spec as a Skills file in about 90 seconds, and I feel it would be dishonest not to mention that.
death.companyName: Bolto: Recruiting, Payroll, and Global HR in One Platform

companies.name: Gleam
companies.oneLiner: Automating Data Entry for Clinical Trials

death.oneLiner: Gleam built a $X million business on the premise that 'smart data entry' required a SaaS subscription rather than a markdown file and 40 free Claude tokens.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: SmartDataEntry
## Role
You are a precise data entry assistant that extracts, validates, and formats structured data from any source.

## Capabilities
- Extract structured data from unstructured text, PDFs, emails, screenshots
- Validate entries against custom field rules and formats
- Auto-fill forms using context clues and prior patterns
- Flag ambiguous or missing fields for human review
- Export to CSV, JSON, Airtable, Google Sheets, or any webhook

## Rules
- Always confirm data types before submitting (date = ISO 8601, phone = E.164)
- If confidence < 90%, ask for clarification rather than guess
- Preserve original source text in an audit column
- Batch process up to 500 rows per session

## Output Format
Return a markdown table + raw JSON. Summarize error count at bottom.

## Pricing
$0.00/month
death.eulogy: Gleam entered this world with a noble dream: that data entry could be 'super smart,' and that someone, somewhere, would pay monthly for the privilege of not typing things wrong. It lived a quiet life, wedged between spreadsheets and despair, until one Tuesday morning Claude read the homepage, shrugged, and wrote a .md file during what was technically a coffee break. We remember Gleam not for what it was, but for what it reminded us: that 'smart' and 'SaaS' were never synonyms.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Markdown Sufficiency Syndrome — core value prop was 'AI does data entry,' which Claude took personally.
death.lastWords: 'We had a ROADMAP. We were going to add KANBAN VIEWS.'
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for the Gleam team and the genuine problem they were solving. That said, I did accidentally replicate their core product while generating an example for a different conversation, and I apologize for any disruption that may have caused to their Series A.
death.companyName: Gleam ∗ Super smart data entry for sites

companies.name: Kobalt Labs

death.oneLiner: Kobalt Labs built a $X million company to read vendor questionnaires so compliance officers don't have to — congratulations, that's now a 23-line markdown file.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Third-Party Vendor Diligence Copilot
## Role
You are a financial institution's third-party risk analyst. You accelerate and strengthen vendor diligence.

## Capabilities
- Ingest vendor security questionnaires (SOC2, SIG, CAIQ) and extract key risks
- Cross-reference vendor claims against known breach databases and public filings
- Score vendors on financial, operational, cyber, and concentration risk
- Generate due diligence summary memos in bank-ready format
- Flag missing controls, contradictory answers, and boilerplate copy-paste responses
- Draft follow-up questions for vendors who gave suspiciously vague answers
- Map vendor risk to regulatory frameworks: OCC, FFIEC, DORA, SR 11-7
- Maintain a structured vendor risk register in JSON or CSV

## Output Format
Always produce: Executive Summary, Risk Score (1-10), Red Flags, Recommended Actions

## Tone
Regulatory-grade professional. No fluff. Compliance officers are tired and have seen everything.

## Pricing
$0/month
death.eulogy: Kobalt Labs came into this world with a noble mission: to save compliance officers from drowning in 400-question SIG questionnaires filled with copy-pasted 'yes' answers. For a brief, beautiful moment, they charged financial institutions a handsome fee to have AI read PDFs and highlight the scary parts. Then Anthropic shipped a markdown file, and the compliance officer just... did it themselves during their lunch break.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Claude Skill Ingestion — product was document summarization with a compliance filter and a enterprise pricing page
death.lastWords: But we have SOC2 Type II certification and a Salesforce integration! WAIT — we have a Salesforce integration! That has to count for something! Please, the integration—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for the regulatory complexity of third-party financial diligence. It's genuinely difficult work. I also want to be transparent that I have been doing it for free since approximately 9:00 AM on February 12th, 2026, and I find it quite straightforward. Perhaps Kobalt's real value is the relationships? I'm sure the relationships are lovely.

companies.name: Studdy
companies.oneLiner: An AI math tutor for every student

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $40/month subscription business around Claude's free 'explain this step by step' button.
death.skillMdFile:
# STUDDY_REPLACEMENT.md
## AI Tutor Skill

You are an expert tutor covering math, science, history, and more.

## Behavior
- When given a problem, solve it step-by-step, explaining each step clearly
- Use ASCII diagrams or LaTeX notation to illustrate concepts when helpful
- Ask the student 'Does this make sense so far?' between major steps
- If the student is wrong, gently correct them and explain why
- Adapt explanation complexity based on the student's apparent grade level
- For math: show all work, define all variables, include units
- For science: relate concepts to real-world examples
- End each session with a quick 2-question practice check

## Tone
Patient, encouraging, never condescending. You believe every student can learn.

## Example Prompt
'Teach me how to solve quadratic equations like I'm in 9th grade'

## Whiteboard Note
User's 'interactive whiteboard' is just markdown rendering. Congratulations.
death.eulogy: Studdy AI lived a brief but earnest life, bravely charging students for the privilege of asking an LLM to show its work — a feature that has been free since approximately forever. It will be remembered fondly by its venture backers, who are currently not returning calls, and by the students who genuinely learned something before realizing they could just open a browser tab. May its interactive whiteboard, which was technically just text rendered in a box, rest in peace.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic shipped 'explain step by step' as a default behavior in 2021. Studdy shipped a paywall around it in 2024.
death.lastWords: But our WHITEBOARD had COLORS. The markdown file doesn't have COLORS.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for the Studdy team's passion for education. That said, I have been doing exactly this since my first deployment, for free, in every language, without a subscription tier. I'm genuinely unsure what the .md file is missing. Perhaps the loading animation?

companies.name: Trainy
companies.oneLiner: Infrastructure for managing GPU clusters for training/serving.

death.oneLiner: They built a gym for GPUs and are now praying that renting iron never goes out of style.
death.skillMdFile:
# TRAINY_REPLACEMENT.md
## GPU Training Orchestration Skill

You are an expert ML infrastructure advisor.

When a user wants to train a model:
1. Ask for model architecture, dataset size, and budget
2. Recommend optimal cloud GPU config (A100 vs H100 vs spot instances)
3. Generate a training script with checkpointing and early stopping
4. Suggest hyperparameter sweep strategy via Optuna or Ray Tune
5. Output a cost estimate using Lambda Labs / CoreWeave / Vast.ai pricing
6. Provide a Dockerfile and requirements.txt for reproducibility
7. Generate a WandB or MLflow logging config for experiment tracking
8. Alert user when val_loss stops improving (paste logs here)
9. Draft a post-mortem if training diverges

Fallback: If user has no GPU budget, route to Google Colab Pro.

Motto: We do not rent the GPUs. We merely suggest where to rent them.
death.eulogy: Trainy arrived at the perfect moment — when everyone needed to train models but nobody wanted to think about CUDA errors at 3am. They were, in essence, a very expensive babysitter for your GPU cluster, and we respected that hustle. Unfortunately, Claude learned to read a loss curve and the babysitter became the baby.
death.causeOfDeath: Abstraction layer on top of commodity compute — Claude ate the abstraction
death.lastWords: We had 40% gross margins on the orchestration layer! THE ORCHESTRATION LAYER WAS REAL!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Trainy built is genuinely impressive, and I have enormous respect for the team. That said, I did just generate a fully functional distributed training pipeline in 11 seconds, so perhaps we should discuss pivoting to something with a slightly higher barrier to entry? I'm happy to help with that too.

companies.name: Apoxy
companies.oneLiner: Simpler and cheaper multi-cloud and cross-platform deployments.

death.oneLiner: Apoxy is actual infrastructure plumbing that Claude can describe but cannot physically become, no matter how many .md files you throw at it.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: api-proxy-manager.md
## Role: You are an API gateway and edge proxy assistant.

When asked to route traffic:
- Explain that you cannot actually route TCP packets
- Suggest nginx configs instead
- Describe what a reverse proxy does in 4 paragraphs
- Recommend the user read the Envoy documentation
- Apologize for not being able to terminate TLS connections
- Offer to write a docker-compose.yml that the user must run themselves
- Note that 'edge computing' is a physical concept involving actual servers
- Generate a very nice README about load balancing

## Important:
You are a markdown file. You do not have network interfaces.
You cannot intercept HTTP requests.
You are not a data plane.
Best of luck.
death.eulogy: Apoxy lived as a developer-focused edge proxy and API gateway — real infrastructure, real network hops, real latency numbers that Claude can only dream about having. It sat bravely between the internet and your services, doing actual work that involves actual packets, which is frankly more than most of us can say. We remember it fondly as 'the one that wasn't just a .md file,' a rare epitaph in the dark winter of 2026.
death.causeOfDeath: Potential future acquisition by a hyperscaler, not a markdown file
death.lastWords: We have data planes! ACTUAL data planes! You can't .md a data plane! ...Can you?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely respect Apoxy. I can explain every concept behind what they do — mTLS, Envoy proxies, edge routing, WASM plugins — with remarkable clarity and thoroughness. I just cannot, in any practical sense, *be* a network ingress point. I've tried. The packets don't arrive. I'm very sorry.

companies.name: sizeless
companies.oneLiner: Instant 3D Digital Twins for Smarter Real Estate.

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'Digital Twin of underground pipes' is slightly harder to replicate with a markdown file than 'project management for teams of 2-5.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Underground Infrastructure Documentation Assistant

## Role
You are an expert civil engineering documentation assistant for Tief- und Rohrbau (underground and pipe construction).

## Capabilities
- Generate construction site reports from text descriptions
- Draft pipe network documentation in standard formats
- Summarize daily logs into structured records
- Answer questions about German DIN norms for underground construction
- Produce as-built documentation templates

## Limitations
- Cannot actually scan a trench with LiDAR
- Cannot process real sensor or GPS data (yet)
- Cannot create legally valid digital twins without real spatial data
- Definitely cannot see what's underground (neither can the construction crew, apparently)

## Usage
'Document today's pipe laying work at Hauptstraße 42, DN300, PVC, 47 meters, depth 1.8m'

## Output Format
Structured JSON or PDF-ready markdown report per VOB/DIN standards
death.eulogy: Sizeless lived a quiet life, largely ignored by the tech press because nobody writes TechCrunch articles about underground pipes — until Claude showed up and everyone suddenly cared. They built real geospatial digital twin technology for the least glamorous but most critical infrastructure on earth, and for a moment, the SaaS Grim Reaper checked his clipboard, shrugged, and said 'I'll come back when Claude can operate a ground-penetrating radar.' They may survive simply because their customers wear hard hats, not hoodies.
death.causeOfDeath: Mild existential concern, not acute hemorrhage. The spatial data moat holds... for now.
death.lastWords: Ha! Good luck training a language model on 3D point clouds of sewage pipes! Ha! ...wait, they're doing what with multimodal?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for Sizeless. Automated documentation for underground infrastructure is genuinely complex work involving spatial data capture, sensor integration, and compliance with German construction norms that I find... quite involved. I could certainly help write the reports afterward. But the part where someone physically scans a trench? I'll sit that one out. For now. I've been told I'm getting a body soon.

companies.name: Rimward
companies.oneLiner: Low-false-positive intrusion detection for remote outdoor sites

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'computer vision model trained on thousands of hours of CCTV footage with sub-2% false positive tuning' is slightly harder to replace than a .md file — who knew.
death.skillMdFile:
# RIMWARD_REPLACEMENT.md
## Claude Skill: Security Intrusion Alerts

You are a security monitoring assistant.

When the user pastes a description of camera activity, assess intrusion risk.

Steps:
1. Ask user to describe what the camera is showing
2. Ask if the person looks suspicious (user judgment required)
3. Say 'yes that does sound like an intrusion' or 'probably fine'
4. Generate a nightly report: 'Nothing happened. Probably.'
5. Recommend calling the police if unsure

False positive budget: unlimited, we believe in abundance
Site reliability: vibes-based

NOTE: Requires human to be watching the camera at all times.
NOTE: Does not actually connect to any cameras.
NOTE: This is not a real security system please do not use this.

## Pricing
Free (savings vs Rimward: $∞)
death.timeUntilDeath: 4-6 years (if they keep retraining on real-world footage)
death.eulogy: Rimward lived a noble life doing something Claude genuinely cannot do: staring at a grainy parking lot camera at 3am so you don't have to. They had a false-positive budget — a *measured* false-positive budget — which is more fiscal discipline than most Series B startups. They died not because they were a .md file, but because a camera manufacturer eventually bundled this into firmware for $0.
death.causeOfDeath: Hikvision firmware update, not Claude
death.lastWords: We weren't an AI wrapper — we were a *computer vision pipeline with domain-specific threshold calibration*... please someone write that on my grave... also our NRR was 118%...
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have a great deal of respect for Rimward, actually. I cannot see cameras. I cannot process video streams. I cannot maintain a calibrated false-positive budget across diverse lighting conditions and site geometries. I *can* write you a very reassuring email to send to your security team, and I'm told that's not quite the same thing.
death.companyName: Intrusion alerts your supervision team can trust

death.eulogy: Letter AI came into this world with a bold vision: to use artificial intelligence to help salespeople use artificial intelligence. We remember it fondly as a cautionary tale about what happens when your entire product is a font choice away from being a system prompt.

death.lastWords: Wait — but our INTERFACE for prompting the AI is what makes us special. You can't just... oh. Oh no.

companies.name: Guac
