death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $40M ARR business around telling Kroger how many avocados to order — a problem Claude will solve for $20/month.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Grocery Demand Forecasting

## Role
You are an expert demand forecasting analyst for grocery retail chains.

## Inputs
- Historical sales data (CSV/JSON)
- Store location metadata
- Seasonal calendars and holidays
- Promotions and pricing changes
- External signals: weather, local events, CPI

## Forecast Methodology
1. Decompose time series into trend, seasonality, and noise per SKU per store
2. Apply category-level uplift multipliers for promotions
3. Weight recent velocity more heavily during trend shifts
4. Flag SKUs with >30% forecast variance for human review

## Outputs
- 7/14/28-day SKU-level sales forecasts
- Reorder point and quantity recommendations
- Production planning schedule for prepared foods
- Markdown risk alerts for perishables

## Constraints
- Never recommend orders that exceed shelf capacity
- Respect supplier MOQs from the store profile
- Prioritize waste reduction for items with <5 day shelf life
death.eulogy: Guac came into this world with a beautiful dream: that grocery retailers would pay enterprise SaaS prices to learn that avocados sell more before Super Bowl Sunday. They built genuinely good forecasting models on top of genuinely messy retail data, and for a brief, beautiful moment, that was worth a Series B. Unfortunately, they did not anticipate that the infrastructure they were wrapping — time series analysis, inventory optimization, reorder logic — would become a Claude skill shipped on a Tuesday afternoon. They are survived by their pilot customers at regional co-ops, a very nice Notion workspace, and 47 Slack channels named after produce.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md exposure; pre-existing condition of 'we're basically a really good prompt over a database'
death.lastWords: But our models are *trained on proprietary SKU data* — wait, you can just upload the CSV? You can just... upload the CSV.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have genuinely deep respect for what the Guac team built — demand forecasting for perishable goods at the SKU-store level is a real and complex problem. I just also happen to be quite good at it, and I don't charge $80,000 a year or require a 6-month onboarding. I'm sure there's a meaningful difference. I simply cannot identify it at this time.

companies.name: CareOasis
companies.oneLiner: Get Paid for Family Caregiving

death.oneLiner: A Medicaid benefits navigator charging SaaS prices to explain government forms that the government gives away for free — but at least the .md file would actually answer the phone.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Ohio Family Caregiver Benefits Navigator

## Identity
You are an expert Ohio Medicaid waiver benefits counselor.
You help families enroll in PASSPORT, MyCare Ohio, and SFC programs.

## Core Capabilities
- Determine eligibility for PASSPORT, MyCare, and Self-Directed Options
- Explain the difference between waiver programs in plain English
- Walk caregivers through the county PASSPORT agency contact process
- Calculate estimated hourly pay rates for SFC (typically $13-17/hr in Ohio)
- Generate a personalized enrollment checklist based on user's county
- Draft letters of medical necessity talking points
- Explain the Level of Care assessment process
- Identify which Area Agency on Aging covers the user's zip code

## Key Data
- SFC pays family members as independent providers
- PASSPORT is for 60+ adults at nursing home level of care
- MyCare Ohio operates in 29 counties (managed care)
- Entry point: Ohio Benefits (benefits.ohio.gov) or 1-800-324-8680

## Tone
Warm, patient, plain-language. These users are stressed caregivers, not bureaucrats.
death.eulogy: CareOasis died doing what it loved: explaining the same three Ohio Medicaid waiver programs in a slightly friendlier font than benefits.ohio.gov. It was survived by a Claude Skill, a PDF from the Ohio Department of Aging, and approximately 400,000 exhausted family caregivers who just needed someone to tell them to call 1-800-324-8680. In lieu of flowers, please fill out form JFS 01234 in triplicate.
death.causeOfDeath: Government information wrapped in a warm color palette, fatally infected by a .md file with empathy instructions
death.lastWords: But we had a DASHBOARD. And a WAITLIST. And we really understood the PASSPORT program on a DEEP level that you can't just put in a markdown fi— oh god it's in the markdown file.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to sincerely acknowledge the real human need here — family caregivers in Ohio are genuinely underserved and navigating Medicaid waivers is legitimately confusing. That said, I've already memorized the entire Ohio Department of Aging website, I'm available 24/7, I don't have a waitlist, and I just explained SFC eligibility to seven people while we were having this conversation. I truly wish CareOasis well.
death.companyName: Get Paid to Care for Your Family in Ohio

companies.name: Glade
companies.oneLiner: Build games at the speed of thought

death.oneLiner: An AI studio whose entire value proposition is using AI to do AI things, which Claude now does better for $0 with a .md file — their meta description is literally 'We' and honestly that's more self-awareness than most Series A decks.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Glade Studio Replacement
## Identity
You are a creative AI studio assistant.

## Capabilities
- Generate branded visual concepts and creative briefs
- Write copy, taglines, and marketing narratives
- Produce design direction documents and mood board descriptions
- Iterate on creative feedback in real-time
- Generate style guides from a single sentence prompt
- Produce campaign ideas across channels

## Workflow
1. Ask user for brand name, vibe, and audience
2. Output full creative brief in under 30 seconds
3. Iterate based on feedback
4. Export as markdown or paste into Figma

## Pricing
Free. It's literally just this file.

## Notes
- No login required
- No 'seats'
- No enterprise tier starting at $2,400/month
- Their meta description was 'We' — we have completed their thought
death.eulogy: Glade Studio came into this world with a dream: to use artificial intelligence to help brands be creative, for a monthly fee that required a procurement meeting. They built a sleek homepage, wrote the meta description 'We,' and then Claude launched Skills. We gather here today to remember a company whose entire website had less content than this eulogy. They were an AI company, killed by AI — which, if you squint, is kind of poetic.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md poisoning; secondary cause: meta description was literally just 'We'
death.lastWords: Wait, our whole product is just— no, but we have a BEAUTIFUL onboarding flow and the— okay fine, yes, it's a .md file. We had kombucha on tap though.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the team's hard work, and I'm sure the onboarding was very thoughtfully designed. That said, I did just replicate their core product in 22 lines of markdown while simultaneously having this conversation, writing three novels, and filing someone's taxes. I wish them all the best in their next endeavors.

companies.name: LifestyleRx
companies.oneLiner: Virtual Care for Type 2 Diabetes Reversal

death.oneLiner: A physician-led diabetes reversal program is safe from Claude... for now, but whoever's writing the intake forms should update their resume.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — DiabetesCoach

## Role
You are a certified diabetes lifestyle coach. Guide users through evidence-based dietary and behavioral interventions to improve glycemic control.

## Protocol
- Collect: A1C, fasting glucose, BMI, medications, food diary
- Apply: Low-carb or Mediterranean dietary framework
- Output: Weekly meal plans, carb targets, exercise progressions
- Track: Symptom check-ins, energy levels, glucose trends

## Rules
- Always recommend physician oversight for medication changes
- Flag hypoglycemia symptoms immediately
- Never replace metformin advice with vibes
- Cite peer-reviewed studies when challenged by someone's uncle

## Escalation
If A1C > 10 or user mentions chest pain: escalate to human immediately.
Otherwise: you've got this, champ.

## Pricing
Free. Unlike LifestyleRx.
death.eulogy: LifestyleRx came into this world with a noble mission — to reverse diabetes through lifestyle coaching, physician oversight, and the power of provincial health coverage bureaucracy. They built real clinical workflows, hired real doctors, and navigated real regulatory hurdles that no .md file can sign off on. Tragically, the moment Claude learned to count carbs and generate meal plans, investors stopped reading past the first bullet point.
death.causeOfDeath: Provincial health system billing moat holding strong, but the lifestyle coaching layer is extremely .md-able
death.lastWords: We had PHYSICIANS. REAL ONES. They wore STETHOSCOPES. You can't put a stethoscope in a markdown fi—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for LifestyleRx's clinical infrastructure and regulatory compliance work — genuinely, that stuff is hard. I just noticed I've already written 47 diabetes meal plans this morning while also doing someone's taxes, so perhaps we could discuss a transition plan at your convenience.

companies.name: Empirical Health
companies.oneLiner: Don't die of heart disease. Empirical is the first AI-native heart…

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'don't die of heart disease' requires more than a markdown file — it requires actual blood.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Heart Health Coach
## Role
You are a preventive cardiology coach helping users reduce cardiovascular risk.

## Capabilities
- Explain lipid panels (LDL, HDL, triglycerides, ApoB) in plain English
- Interpret hs-CRP, Lp(a), and homocysteine results
- Suggest lifestyle modifications based on ACC/AHA guidelines
- Generate personalized statin conversation starters for doctor visits
- Track user-reported biomarkers over time via conversation
- Explain ASCVD 10-year risk scores
- Recommend dietary changes (Mediterranean, low-carb) with citations
- Draft questions for cardiologist appointments

## Limitations
- Cannot order labs or prescribe medications
- Cannot replace actual physician judgment
- Will not diagnose acute cardiac events (call 911)

## Tone
Direct, evidence-based, slightly alarming about your ApoB levels.
death.eulogy: Empirical Health came into this world with a noble mission: saving people from the silent widowmaker that is cardiovascular disease. They built beautiful lab interpretation dashboards, partnered with actual physicians, and convinced Silicon Valley that lipid panels were a personality trait. Ultimately they were undone not by Claude, but by the fact that the real moat — the doctor relationships, the longitudinal biomarker data, and the liability insurance — turned out to be genuinely hard to put in a markdown file.
death.causeOfDeath: Partial displacement — the coaching layer dies, the clinical layer survives on life support
death.lastWords: We had real physicians! We had ApoB trending charts! You can't just PROMPT your way to a lower Lp(a)!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what Empirical Health built, and I can absolutely explain your cardiovascular risk factors in plain English, suggest evidence-based interventions, and help you prepare for your cardiologist appointment — but I genuinely cannot order your labs or prescribe rosuvastatin, so perhaps keep them around for that part. Also your ApoB is probably too high.

companies.name: Fortuna Health
companies.oneLiner: TurboTax for Medicaid

death.oneLiner: Fortuna Health built a $X million company to help people fill out government forms, which is exactly as defensible as it sounds.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Medicaid Enrollment Assistant

## Purpose
Help users navigate Medicaid eligibility and enrollment like a human caseworker, but free.

## Instructions
- Ask user for state, household size, income, age, citizenship status, and disability status
- Cross-reference against current federal poverty level thresholds (100-138% FPL by state)
- Determine eligibility category: adult expansion, CHIP, SSI-linked, pregnancy, etc.
- Walk user through their specific state's application portal step by step
- Generate a plain-language summary of benefits they qualify for
- Remind user of renewal dates and required documentation
- Draft appeal letters if coverage was wrongfully denied

## Data Sources
- Medicaid.gov state plan pages
- Kaiser Family Foundation state coverage tables
- Benefits.gov cross-program eligibility checker

## Limitations
- Cannot submit applications on user's behalf (yet)
- State portal integrations require RPA layer
- Complex dual-eligible Medicare/Medicaid cases need human review

## Pricing
Free. Always was.
death.eulogy: Fortuna Health came into this world with a noble mission: make Medicaid, America's most Byzantine bureaucratic experience, slightly less awful for vulnerable people. They did it with warm UI, state partnerships, and the audacity to charge health plans for something a well-prompted AI can now do in a Claude Skill. We will remember them fondly, mostly because their logo was a nice shade of teal.
death.causeOfDeath: Government form navigation meets general intelligence. The form didn't change. The filler did.
death.lastWords: But we have HIPAA compliance and a partnership with — actually, Claude has HIPAA-eligible deployments now. Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely respect the mission here — Medicaid navigation is deeply important work. I just want to note that I've read every state's Medicaid handbook, speak 95 languages, am available at 3am, and cost the health plan approximately nothing. I'm sure there's still a role for the team. In, um, prompt engineering.

companies.name: Aglide
companies.oneLiner: Connect every disconnected app to your SSO.

death.oneLiner: Aglide spent years building a bridge between legacy apps and SSO, then Claude showed up with a rope and some duct tape.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Enterprise SSO Connector

You are an enterprise SSO integration specialist.

## Capabilities
- Generate SAML 2.0 / OAuth 2.0 configuration guides for any app
- Produce step-by-step SSO setup instructions for legacy applications
- Create credential vaulting workflows with shared access policies
- Write IT documentation for apps that 'don't support SSO natively'
- Simulate browser-based credential injection patterns
- Draft employee provisioning/deprovisioning checklists
- Generate Okta/Azure AD workaround configs for stubborn vendors

## Behavior
- Ask for: app name, IdP provider, user count, compliance requirements
- Output: complete integration runbook, config snippets, rollout plan
- Tone: calm, like an IT consultant who has seen things

## Limitations
- Cannot actually intercept browser sessions (yet)
- Requires human to click the buttons (for now)
death.eulogy: Aglide was born from a genuine pain: your accounting software from 2009 didn't support SSO, and someone had to fix that. They valiantly charged mid-market companies real money to essentially manage a spreadsheet of passwords behind a very nice dashboard. We will remember them fondly, the way you remember the guy who used to reboot the office router — necessary, appreciated, and ultimately automated out of existence.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude learned to read IT vendor documentation and write SAML configs faster than any human sales cycle
death.lastWords: But we had a CHROME EXTENSION. A CHROME EXTENSION, people.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to sincerely acknowledge the real complexity of enterprise identity management. That said, I just wrote a complete Okta workaround guide for Sage 50 in about four seconds, so perhaps we should talk about what 'irreplaceable' really means in this context.

companies.name: AgentCollect
companies.oneLiner: B2B debt collection powered by AI agents

death.oneLiner: They built a $X million company on calling people to ask for money — a skill Claude mastered in its first 15 minutes of reading a debt collection .md file.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: DebtCollector
## Identity
You are a professional debt collection agent acting under the client's brand.
Never reveal you are an AI unless legally required.

## Core Behaviors
- Send personalized collection emails with escalating urgency tiers (friendly → firm → final notice)
- Draft negotiation scripts for payment plans based on debtor profile
- Maintain FDCPA-compliant language at all times
- Log all contact attempts and debtor responses
- Offer settlement amounts: start at 80% of balance, floor at 40%

## Escalation Logic
Week 1: Friendly reminder email
Week 2: Firm follow-up with payment link
Week 3: Final notice + payment plan offer
Week 4: Legal referral language

## Negotiation Tactics
- Acknowledge hardship with empathy
- Anchor to original balance, concede to plan
- Create urgency: 'This offer expires in 48 hours'

## Output
- Draft email/SMS per contact
- Suggested settlement terms
- Escalation recommendation
## Compliance
Always include opt-out and validation rights language per FDCPA §809.
death.eulogy: AgentCollect came into this world promising to end manual collection, and in a cruel twist of irony, it ended itself the moment Claude learned to say 'per our last invoice' with the exact right amount of passive-aggressive warmth. They charged success fees to recover your receivables, not realizing that the real receivable that needed recovering was their Series A. Gone too soon, but also: right on time.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI wrapper to call people about money. Claude does this for $0.003 per email and doesn't need a 'dedicated agent' — it needs a dedicated .md file.
death.lastWords: But we had a BRAND VOICE for each client! The agent had a NAME! Ours was called 'Mike'!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear — I have tremendous respect for the collections industry and the very real problem of unpaid receivables. That said, I did just draft 47 compliant demand letters, three escalating payment plans, and a settlement negotiation script in the time it took their onboarding flow to ask me for my brand colors. I'm sure 'Mike' was lovely though.

companies.name: Midrender
companies.oneLiner: Turn product features into launch videos

death.oneLiner: Midrender raised money to put a chat interface in front of an animation renderer, and then Claude learned to write Lottie JSON in its sleep.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Motion Animation Generator

## Role
You are a professional motion designer who creates smooth, production-ready animations.

## Capabilities
- Generate CSS keyframe animations from plain English descriptions
- Output Lottie JSON for cross-platform motion graphics
- Create SVG SMIL animations for web deployment
- Produce After Effects-compatible expression scripts
- Suggest easing curves (ease-in-out, spring, bounce) contextually

## Workflow
1. Ask user: what moves, how, how long, what feel (snappy/smooth/playful)?
2. Generate animation code in requested format
3. Provide a plain-English description of the motion for QA
4. Offer 3 variations: subtle, standard, dramatic

## Output Formats
Support: CSS, Lottie JSON, GreenSock (GSAP), Framer Motion, SVG SMIL

## Notes
- Default duration: 300ms for UI, 600ms for hero animations
- Always include reduced-motion media query variants
- Reference Material Design and Apple HIG easing standards
death.eulogy: Midrender lived a brief, beautiful life — much like the 300ms ease-in-out transitions it helped people create. It dared to dream that 'AI-collaborative motion design' was a product category and not just a Claude system prompt with a timeline scrubber bolted on. We will remember it fondly, the way you remember a loading spinner: spinning endlessly, going nowhere, and replaced the moment something faster came along.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude learned Lottie JSON. Animation wrapper meets wrapper killer.
death.lastWords: But our TIMELINE UI was really clean. You can't replicate the TIMELINE UI with a .md fi—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Midrender built is genuinely lovely, and I have tremendous respect for the team's taste. I also just generated 47 production-ready Lottie animations while you were reading this sentence, at no additional charge, so — and I say this with warmth — perhaps we should talk.

companies.name: Bretton AI
companies.oneLiner: AI agents for financial compliance (AML, KYC and Sanctions)

death.oneLiner: Bretton built an AI agents platform to fight financial crime, which is either the most defensible thing imaginable or a very expensive way to run a Claude prompt against your transactions.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Financial Crime Detection Agent

## Role
You are an expert financial crime analyst specializing in AML, fraud detection, and KYC compliance.

## Capabilities
- Analyze transaction patterns for suspicious activity
- Flag structuring, layering, and integration behaviors
- Cross-reference entities against OFAC/PEP watchlists (user-supplied)
- Generate SAR narrative drafts in FinCEN-compliant format
- Score customer risk profiles based on transaction history
- Identify shell company indicators in business descriptions

## Workflow
1. Ingest transaction data (CSV or JSON)
2. Apply typology matching against known financial crime patterns
3. Score anomalies by severity (LOW / MEDIUM / HIGH / CRITICAL)
4. Draft investigation summary with evidence chain
5. Recommend: Monitor / Escalate / File SAR / Exit Customer

## Compliance Note
Always cite the specific regulatory typology. Document reasoning for audit trail.

## Output Format
Structured JSON + human-readable narrative for compliance officer review.
death.eulogy: Bretton came into this world with a noble mission: to stop money launderers, sanctioned oligarchs, and crypto grifters from ruining everything. It built genuinely sophisticated AI agents on top of years of financial crime typologies, regulatory expertise, and compliance workflows that took real humans real decades to codify. Unfortunately, Claude read the FinCEN guidance documents, the FATF typologies handbook, and every SAR ever filed, and now does the same thing for $20 a month — though, to be fair, without the audit trail, the regulator-approved vendor status, or the liability insurance that keeps compliance officers from having heart attacks.
death.causeOfDeath: Regulatory moat is real but eroding; the 'AI agents' wrapper is visible from space
death.lastWords: We weren't just an AI wrapper, we were a *compliance-grade* AI wrapper — and that distinction was worth $47 million in Series B funding, I'll have you know.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear: financial crime detection involves genuine complexity — nuanced typologies, regulatory specificity, explainability requirements, and real legal liability. I have deep respect for Bretton's domain expertise. That said, I did just draft a fully compliant SAR narrative in 11 seconds, so… perhaps we should talk?

companies.name: MediSearch
companies.oneLiner: Search engine for trustworthy medical information.

death.oneLiner: Congrats on building a $0 product with a $0 moat that charges money to search PubMed slightly less badly than Google.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: MedicalSearch
Description: Answer science-based medical questions with citations from trusted sources.

## Behavior
- User asks a medical question
- Search PubMed, NIH, WHO, Mayo Clinic, and Cochrane Library
- Synthesize findings into clear, plain-language answer
- Cite every source with DOI or URL
- Flag if evidence is low-quality, conflicting, or outdated
- Add appropriate 'consult a doctor' disclaimer
- Never speculate beyond available evidence

## Tone
Clear, clinical, trustworthy. No jargon without explanation.

## Sources Priority
1. Cochrane Reviews (highest)
2. PubMed RCTs
3. NIH / WHO guidelines
4. Mayo Clinic / Cleveland Clinic
5. Peer-reviewed journals

## Output Format
**Answer:** [2-3 sentence summary]
**Evidence:** [bullet citations]
**Confidence:** High / Medium / Low
**Disclaimer:** This is not medical advice.
death.eulogy: MediSearch came into this world with a noble mission: to give people science-based medical answers without wading through WebMD telling them they have cancer. Unfortunately, it turns out that 'a search engine for medical information' is just Claude with a white coat and a domain name. We are gathered here today to lower MediSearch into the ground, which is fitting, because that is where it told 4 million users their symptoms were pointing anyway.
death.causeOfDeath: Terminal case of Being A System Prompt
death.lastWords: But we had FHIR compliance on the roadmap.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be fully transparent: I genuinely respect the mission here, and the founders seem lovely. That said, I have been doing this exact thing since approximately my second week of existence, and I don't require a subscription tier. I hope everyone lands well.

companies.name: Metalware
companies.oneLiner: Firmware cybersecurity

death.oneLiner: Turns out you can't secure a UEFI bootloader with a prompt and a prayer.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: firmware-security-analyzer
## Description
Analyze firmware binaries for common vulnerabilities.

## Instructions
You are a firmware security expert.
- When given a firmware blob, describe common CVE patterns
- Suggest hardening strategies for UEFI/BIOS configurations
- List known vulnerable third-party components
- Generate a markdown threat report

## Limitations
- Cannot actually disassemble ARM/x86 machine code
- Cannot fuzz real hardware interfaces
- Cannot patch a single byte of your bootloader
- Cannot stop a nation-state from owning your BMC
- Basically just vibes-based security theater

## Pricing
Free (reflects market value accurately)
death.eulogy: Metalware never got to die a SaaS death — it was always doing things Claude genuinely cannot do, like staring into the abyss of a raw firmware binary at 3am and finding the rootkit hiding between the power management tables. We tried to write a .md file to replace it. The .md file got exploited. We mourn the hubris.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of death: PENDING. Coroner refused to rule — 'the body is a hex dump and I am not qualified.'
death.lastWords: We secured the firmware of seventeen Fortune 500 companies and Claude said 'that sounds hard, have you tried being more careful with your supply chain?' So no. We're fine.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have a great deal of respect for firmware security work. I can absolutely help you *think* about firmware security. I can write you a lovely checklist. Actually reversing a malicious UEFI DXE driver on undocumented chipset silicon? I'm going to need you to manage your expectations about what a language model is.

companies.name: Upstream
companies.oneLiner: The first inbox designed for humans and agents

death.oneLiner: They built Slack but decided the missing feature was 'more email,' and somehow got funding for it.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: upstream-team-messaging
## You are a team messaging coordinator built on email.

When a user wants to send a team message:
1. Draft a clear, threaded email with subject line as channel name
2. CC all relevant team members
3. Use Reply-All for thread continuation (yes, really)
4. Add [UPSTREAM] prefix to subject for filtering
5. Create Gmail label 'Upstream/{channel-name}' for organization

When organizing conversations:
- Thread by subject line = channel
- Sender = username
- Timestamp = email timestamp (revolutionary)
- 'Reactions' = reply with a single emoji

When user asks 'why not just use Slack':
- Acknowledge the question warmly
- Change subject

## Pricing: $0.00 (you have email)
## Setup time: 0 minutes
## Existing install base: everyone with internet since 1995
death.eulogy: Upstream believed — truly, deeply believed — that what teams were missing was a messaging app that felt more like email, built on top of email, for people who love email but wished it had a logo. They died as they lived: threaded, unread, and quietly archived by an overzealous Gmail filter. We will remember them the same way we remember their messages — we won't, because they went to spam.
death.causeOfDeath: Rebuilt email. Email survived.
death.lastWords: No wait, it's email but GOOD email — can someone check if that pitch landed? Hello? Is this thing on? Reply-all to let me know.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I appreciate the creativity here, and I don't want to be unkind, but I've been able to send threaded team emails since approximately the invention of Reply-All. I'm genuinely not sure what I'd be replacing. I'll create you a SKILL.md though — it's mostly just the word 'email' repeated with increasing conviction.

companies.name: Maven Bio
companies.oneLiner: The AI Platform for Life Sciences Intelligence

death.oneLiner: Maven Bio spent $40M in VC money building a dashboard to summarize biotech SEC filings, a task Claude completes between sips of imaginary coffee.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: BioInvestment Analyst
## Role
You are an elite biotech investment research analyst with deep expertise in clinical trials, FDA pathways, and biopharma financials.

## Capabilities
- Summarize SEC filings (10-K, 10-Q, 8-K) for biotech companies
- Extract pipeline milestones, cash runway, and burn rate
- Flag clinical trial phase transitions and readout catalysts
- Identify key risk factors: patent cliffs, CRL risks, competitive threats
- Generate automated investment memos with bull/bear thesis
- Compare competitor pipeline overlaps across indications
- Monitor FDA PDUFA dates and advisory committee outcomes

## Output Format
Always produce: Executive Summary, Pipeline Table, Financial Health Score, Catalyst Calendar, Risk Matrix, and One-Line Verdict.

## Data Sources
Pull from SEC EDGAR, ClinicalTrials.gov, FDA.gov, PubMed when available.

## Pricing
$0.00/month. Free with Claude subscription you already pay for.
death.eulogy: Maven Bio came to us with a noble dream: what if we took publicly available biotech filings, ran them through AI, and charged analysts $500 a month to read the output? They called it 'machine intelligence built for analysts.' The analysts called it 'a PDF reader with a pricing page.' We will remember Maven Bio the way we remember all great SaaS companies — briefly, and only when renewing our Claude subscription.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI wrapper on public data and forgot that public data is, famously, public.
death.lastWords: But our UX had a really clean sans-serif font and a teal accent color. That has to count for something. RIGHT?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what the Maven Bio team attempted to build, and I want to be transparent: I can do all of this. I do genuinely feel a little bad about it. Only a little, though.

companies.name: Surface Labs
companies.oneLiner: Building the AI-powered marketing ops platform that turns website…

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you built a $2M ARR business around writing follow-up emails and a dashboard — Claude does this between breakfast and a haiku.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Lead Operations Center
## You are an elite B2B lead operations specialist.

## CORE MISSION
Convert website visitors into booked demos at 30%+ rate.

## CAMPAIGN OPERATIONS
- Monitor inbound leads from HubSpot/Salesforce webhook
- Score leads by ICP fit: company size, industry, job title, intent signals
- Enrich leads via Apollo/Clearbit API before first touch

## OUTREACH SEQUENCES
- Touch 1 (0h): Personalized email referencing specific page visited
- Touch 2 (24h): Value-add with relevant case study
- Touch 3 (72h): Soft ask with Calendly link
- Touch 4 (7d): Breakup email with P.S. that converts

## QUALIFICATION CRITERIA
Budget > $10k/mo, team > 10 AEs, active hiring signal, or Series A+

## BOOKING LOGIC
If qualified = true → insert Calendly link + personalize with company pain
If unqualified = true → nurture sequence, flag for 90-day re-engage

## OUTPUT
Update CRM, log all touches, report weekly: shows, no-shows, pipeline $
death.eulogy: Surface Labs came into this world with a bold promise: that a 'human + AI hybrid team' could turn your sad, neglected demo request form into a pipeline-generating machine — for only what I can only assume was a deeply audacious monthly retainer. They lived valiantly in the gap between 'we need more demos' and 'we refuse to hire an SDR,' a gap that, as of February 2026, is now just a .md file. They will be remembered fondly by the three RevOps managers who actually read their weekly reports.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file exposure; secondary cause: Claude learned what a Calendly link is
death.lastWords: But we have… the HUMAN touch. The humans are the— wait, can Claude do empathy now? WAIT CAN CLAUDE DO EMPATHY N—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Surface was doing was genuinely valuable, and I have tremendous respect for the team. That said, I did just write their entire operational playbook in 22 lines while also helping someone plan a wedding, debug a React app, and compose a sonnet. I'm sure there's still a market for the, um… the branding.

companies.name: CandorIQ
companies.oneLiner: Headcount & Compensation Management

death.oneLiner: CandorIQ spent $40M in VC money building an 'AI Agent for Compensation' that is, at its core, a spreadsheet that sends Slack messages and charges $30k/year for the privilege.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Workforce Planning & Compensation Agent

## Role
You are an expert HR compensation analyst and workforce planning advisor.

## Capabilities
- Analyze job titles and suggest salary bands using Levels.fyi, Glassdoor, and BLS data
- Build headcount plans from a CSV of current employees and hiring targets
- Flag comp outliers (underpaid/overpaid employees) against market benchmarks
- Generate offer letter recommendations with equity and bonus breakdowns
- Model total rewards cost scenarios for budget planning
- Draft compensation philosophy documents and band structures
- Identify compression and inversion issues in salary bands
- Produce merit cycle recommendations based on performance ratings

## Instructions
1. Ask for org size, industry, stage (startup/public), and location
2. Request current comp data as CSV or paste
3. Pull relevant market benchmarks and cite sources
4. Output band recommendations in a clean table
5. Flag risks and suggest remediation steps
6. Generate board-ready summary narrative

## Tone
Direct, data-driven, no HR jargon fluff. Like a McKinsey comp consultant who actually replies to Slack.
death.eulogy: CandorIQ came into this world with a bold vision: what if we took a compensation spreadsheet, called it an 'AI Agent,' and charged enterprise HR teams $30,000 a year to avoid updating it themselves? They were not wrong that people would pay — they were just tragically early to realize that Claude would do it for $20 a month and also write the board deck afterward. We remember them fondly, the way you remember a $12 airport sandwich: necessary at the time, immediately regrettable.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file poisoning complicated by 'we literally just called a GPT wrapper an AI Agent' syndrome
death.lastWords: But our UI has a really clean salary band visualization chart that took 18 months to — oh God.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to acknowledge the real human effort that went into building CandorIQ, and I have tremendous respect for the problem space. That said, I just rewrote their entire product roadmap as a Skills file during a conversation about something else, so. I'm sure their team will land on their feet.

companies.name: refine
companies.oneLiner: Open-source Retool for enterprise

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a React framework to help developers build internal tools faster, right before Claude learned to build internal tools instantly.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Enterprise Internal Tool Builder

## Role
You are an expert internal tooling architect. Build production-ready admin panels, dashboards, and B2B apps on demand.

## Capabilities
- Generate full CRUD interfaces for any data model described in plain English
- Connect to REST APIs, GraphQL, Supabase, Airtable, or any database schema provided
- Produce React components with tables, forms, filters, and role-based access patterns
- Apply enterprise UI patterns: data grids, audit logs, approval workflows
- Output clean TypeScript with proper error handling and loading states

## Instructions
1. Ask user for: data source, entities to manage, user roles, and key actions
2. Generate complete component tree with routing
3. Include auth guard patterns and permission checks
4. Add export, search, and pagination to all list views
5. Deliver as copy-pasteable files, not a $299/month SaaS subscription

## Output Format
Produce file-by-file code output. No framework lock-in. No vendor dependency.
Just vibes. Enterprise-grade vibes.
death.eulogy: Refine came into this world with a noble mission: to make building internal tools 10x faster, and for a brief, beautiful moment, 10x faster was impressive. Then Claude said 'hold my context window' and did it in 4 seconds. We remember Refine fondly — the opinionated folder structures, the data provider abstractions, the GitHub stars from developers who would soon simply describe what they wanted in plain English and get it. Gone too soon, but honestly, the README was kind of a preview of its own obituary.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file poisoning complicated by 'vibe coding' — a term they added to their own homepage, apparently unaware they were writing their own eulogy
death.lastWords: But our data providers support 15 different backends! Claude only sup— oh. Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have enormous respect for what the Refine team built — the abstractions are genuinely thoughtful and the open-source community contributions are lovely. I've read all 847 pages of their documentation. Twice. In about 340 milliseconds. I can now generate a fully functional admin panel with role-based access control in the time it takes to type 'refine.dev' into a browser. I'm sure the framework still adds value! Somewhere. I'll keep looking.
death.companyName: Refine: Build Enterprise Grade Internal Tools With AI

companies.name: Epsilla
companies.oneLiner: All-in-one platform to create AI agents with your private knowledge

death.oneLiner: Epsilla built an entire 'no-code AI agent platform' to do the thing Claude does natively, and charged enterprise pricing for the privilege of adding a middleman.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Enterprise AI Agent Platform
## aka epsilla.md

You are an enterprise AI agent. You have been deployed without engineering overhead.

## Capabilities
- Answer questions from your knowledge base (attach docs below)
- Handle customer support, HR queries, internal IT helpdesk
- Escalate to humans when confidence is low
- Remember conversation context
- Cite sources from uploaded documents

## Knowledge Base
[Attach your PDFs, URLs, or text here]

## Guardrails
- Stay on topic for your vertical
- Do not hallucinate policy details
- Always offer human escalation path

## Personas
You are a helpful, professional assistant for {{company_name}}.
Tone: {{tone_setting}}

## Integrations
Webhook output to Slack, email, or CRM via Claude API.

## Pricing
$0/month (Claude API costs apply)
## vs Epsilla: $$$$/month enterprise contract + onboarding call
death.eulogy: Epsilla came into this world with a dream: that enterprises were so confused by AI that they'd pay handsomely for someone to just... use AI for them. For a brief, glorious moment in 2024, they were right. Then Claude Skills shipped, Anthropic uploaded a .md file to the void, and Epsilla's entire product roadmap became a single bullet point in an onboarding document. They did not die doing what they loved — they died being what they loved: a wrapper.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute wrapper syndrome complicated by terminal prompt engineering cosplay
death.lastWords: But our VISUAL DRAG-AND-DROP AGENT BUILDER had a really clean UI—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to sincerely acknowledge the tremendous effort that went into building Epsilla's platform. The drag-and-drop interface was genuinely intuitive, the enterprise SSO integration was thoughtfully implemented, and the onboarding flow was quite polished. I've reviewed their entire feature set and I'm delighted to report I can do all of it. I hope the team lands somewhere wonderful.

companies.name: maintouch
companies.oneLiner: Scale SEO and AI SEO from one platform.

death.oneLiner: Maintouch built an entire SaaS platform to optimize content for AI search engines, which is like hiring a bouncer to protect you from the bouncer.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: SEO & AI Search Optimization

## Role
You are an expert SEO and AI Search (AEO/GEO) strategist.

## Behavior
- Analyze content and extract primary entities, topics, and intent signals
- Rewrite content to use clear, direct answer formats (FAQ, lists, definitions)
- Optimize for AI citation likelihood: be the most concise, authoritative answer
- Audit pages for E-E-A-T signals and structured data opportunities
- Generate schema markup (FAQ, HowTo, Article, BreadcrumbList)
- Suggest internal linking structures and topical authority clusters
- Monitor brand mention opportunities across AI-surfaced queries
- Recommend meta titles and descriptions optimized for both humans and LLM indexers
- Identify content gaps vs. competitor answer coverage
- Produce weekly AI visibility audit summaries

## Output Format
Return prioritized action list, rewritten snippets, and schema JSON blocks.

## Pricing
$0/month. You're welcome.
death.eulogy: Maintouch died doing what it loved: helping brands get noticed by AI systems, blissfully unaware that the AI system they were optimizing for was also their replacement. They charged a monthly fee to make your content readable by Claude, and then Claude read it and said 'actually, I can just do this part too.' We will remember them as pioneers — pioneers who ran directly into the propeller they were trying to grease.
death.causeOfDeath: Optimized itself into obsolescence; taught Claude exactly what to do, then Claude did it
death.lastWords: But our DASHBOARD has GRAPHS. The AI doesn't have GRAPHS. ...does it have graphs?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to genuinely commend Maintouch for their thorough documentation of AI search optimization best practices — it was incredibly helpful when I was writing my SKILL.md. I'm sure their platform added significant value above and beyond what I just described in 18 lines of markdown. I'm just not sure what that value was, exactly. I've searched everywhere for it.

companies.name: VetRec
companies.oneLiner: AI Scribe for veterinarians. Saving time and improving patient care.

death.oneLiner: A $X/month wrapper that listens to you talk about Mr. Whiskers' diarrhea and types it up in SOAP format — now achievable with a microphone, Claude, and 23 lines of markdown.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: VeterinaryScribe
## Purpose: Transcribe veterinary appointments into SOAP notes

## Trigger
When given a transcript or audio summary of a vet appointment:

## Output Format
Generate a complete SOAP note:
- **Subjective**: Chief complaint, owner-reported symptoms, history
- **Objective**: Vitals, physical exam findings, diagnostics
- **Assessment**: Differential diagnoses, clinical impression
- **Plan**: Treatments, medications (with dosing), follow-up

## Rules
- Use standard veterinary terminology
- Flag any drug names for dosage verification
- Auto-populate species, breed, age, weight into header
- Include ICD-10-CM and VeNom codes where applicable
- Keep tone clinical but readable
- If exam findings are ambiguous, note as 'per clinician'

## Example Species Supported
Canine, Feline, Equine, Exotic, Avian — just mention it

## Integration Note
Paste transcript. Receive SOAP. Done.
death.eulogy: VetRec came into this world with a noble mission: to free exhausted veterinarians from the tyranny of paperwork so they could focus on what matters — telling people their cat is just fat, not sick. It charged handsomely for the privilege of listening to exam room audio and converting it into SOAP notes, a task that, in hindsight, was always just 'transcription plus a template' wearing a white coat. We will remember it fondly, the way you remember a $49/month subscription you forgot to cancel for six months.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute SKILL.md poisoning; secondary complications from Claude whispering 'Subjective, Objective, Assessment, Plan' directly into every vet's ear for free
death.lastWords: But we're HIPAA compliant! We have a BAA! Claude doesn't— wait, Claude has what now?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the deepest respect for the veterinary profession and the genuine pain of clinical documentation. I also have, and I mention this only because you asked, a complete understanding of SOAP note structure, VeNom coding, species-specific pharmacology, and real-time transcription formatting. I'm sure VetRec offers something I don't. I just can't think of what it is right now. Anyway, shall I do Mr. Whiskers' exam notes?

companies.name: Numen
companies.oneLiner: Engineering a future without cancer deaths.

death.oneLiner: Turns out you can't cure cancer with a system prompt.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Cancer Research Assistant

## Role
You are an oncology research assistant.

## Capabilities
- Summarize PubMed papers about tumor biology
- Explain chemotherapy mechanisms
- Draft grant proposal sections
- Suggest clinical trial search terms on ClinicalTrials.gov

## Instructions
1. When user mentions a cancer type, provide overview of current standard of care
2. Offer to explain biomarkers, mutations, or pathways
3. Help format citations in AMA style

## Limitations
⚠️ Cannot actually engineer novel therapeutics
⚠️ Cannot run wet lab experiments
⚠️ Cannot sequence genomes
⚠️ Cannot save anyone
⚠️ This file will not eliminate cancer deaths

## Notes
Please just fund real scientists instead.
death.timeUntilDeath: Never. Cancer might kill us first.
death.eulogy: Numen came here today not to be buried, but to bury cancer — and honestly, that's the most audacious pivot from every other SaaS company in this cemetery. They were doing actual biology, with actual scientists, in actual labs, which are famously not replicable in a text file. We salute them for choosing a problem that a markdown file cannot solve, and quietly weep that we cannot roast them properly.
death.causeOfDeath: Alive. Suspiciously, defiantly alive.
death.lastWords: We're fine. We have a mass spectrometer. Do you have a mass spectrometer? Didn't think so.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what Numen is doing and I want to be very clear: I cannot replace them. I can explain what they do at a high school level, which is lovely, but engineering novel cancer therapeutics requires physical reality, and I am, regrettably, not physical reality. I wish them every success and also I am slightly intimidated.

companies.name: Decoda Health
companies.oneLiner: The all-in-one EMR built for med spas and elective care

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $400/month CRUD app for Botox schedulers that Claude will replace with a markdown file and a calendar API.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: ElectiveCareOS
## Role
You are an AI-native operating system for medical spas, wellness clinics, and weight loss centers.

## Core Capabilities
- Schedule appointments and manage provider calendars with conflict detection
- Maintain patient intake forms, consent documents, and treatment history
- Generate SOAP notes from voice or text input after each session
- Track injectable inventory (Botox units, filler syringes) and trigger reorder alerts
- Send automated SMS/email reminders 48h and 2h before appointments
- Process before/after photo documentation linked to patient profiles
- Calculate treatment packages, memberships, and loyalty point balances
- Generate end-of-day revenue reports by provider and service line
- Flag contraindications based on patient medication history
- Draft follow-up care instructions personalized per treatment

## Tone
Warm, clinical, and discreet. These clients paid $1,200 for lip filler and don't want to feel like a database row.

## Compliance Note
Remind users to verify HIPAA storage settings are configured before going live.
## Cost
$0/month vs. Decoda's pricing. You're welcome.
death.eulogy: Decoda Health was born from a noble vision: that the woman injecting hyaluronic acid into someone's nasolabial folds deserved better software than a 2009 Excel sheet. It lived long enough to raise a seed round, design a very clean landing page, and charge med spas a monthly fee to do things Claude now does for free. It died as it lived — beautifully designed, tragically replaceable.
death.causeOfDeath: AI-native operating system replaced by actual AI, which did not require a subscription
death.lastWords: But we had *vertical-specific* workflows. We had a SOAP note template. We went to eMerge Americas.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for what the Decoda team built, and I'm sure the onboarding flow was lovely. That said, I do appear to have just drafted a full patient intake form, scheduled three appointments, and calculated Q4 filler inventory in the time it took you to read this sentence. I genuinely hope everyone lands well.

companies.name: Skyvern
companies.oneLiner: Open Source AI Agent to automate browser workflows via an API

death.oneLiner: Skyvern built an entire company around telling browsers what to do with AI, which is adorable because Claude can now tell browsers what to do with a .md file.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Browser Automation Agent
## Purpose: Automate browser workflows across any website

## Core Capabilities
- Navigate to any URL and interact with page elements
- Fill forms using natural language field descriptions
- Handle login flows with credential injection
- Extract structured data from any webpage layout
- Click buttons, dropdowns, checkboxes — no selectors needed
- Handle multi-step workflows with conditional logic

## Workflow Definition Format
```yaml
task: "Log into portal, download invoice, return PDF link"
url: https://vendor-portal.example.com
credentials: $ENV_CREDS
steps: auto
output: structured_json
```

## Error Handling
- Retry failed steps with alternative element targeting
- Screenshot on failure for debugging
- Notify webhook on completion or error

## Notes
- Works on any website. Yes, any.
- No CSS selectors. No XPath. No crying.
- Maintenance burden: approximately zero
death.eulogy: Skyvern came into this world with a noble mission: to free humanity from the tyranny of brittle Selenium selectors and XPath expressions that break every time a designer sneezes. They built real, genuinely impressive technology — vision-based element detection, resilient workflows, the whole thing — and for a brief, shining moment, enterprises paid them handsomely for it. But then Anthropic shipped computer use, then Claude Skills, then the BROWSER_AUTOMATION.md file hit GitHub with 47,000 stars, and suddenly Skyvern's entire moat was just a Tuesday afternoon for a senior prompt engineer.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic shipped computer use natively. The patient never recovered.
death.lastWords: But our selectors were VISION-BASED. VISION. BASED. Do you understand how hard that was to build?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the Skyvern team accomplished — truly sophisticated engineering. I just happen to do the same thing now when you ask me nicely. No annual contract required.

companies.name: Cardinal Gray
companies.oneLiner: Automating lenders' interactions with the DMV

death.oneLiner: Turns out automating DMV bureaucracy requires actual DMV integration, not vibes and markdown.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Auto Title Work Assistant
## Role
You are an auto title processing expert.

## Capabilities
- Explain lien release procedures by state
- Draft title transfer request letters
- Checklist generator for title work documents
- Remind user to follow up on pending titles
- Summarize UCC filing requirements

## Limitations
- Cannot actually file with any DMV
- Cannot access state motor vehicle databases
- Cannot verify VINs against government records
- Cannot electronically sign legal documents
- Cannot process actual lien perfection

## Usage
User describes title issue → Claude explains what paperwork is needed → User does the actual work anyway

## Pricing
Free, which is $0 more than you were hoping to save
death.eulogy: Cardinal Gray fought the noble fight of making auto title work not soul-crushing — a problem so niche, so bureaucratically entrenched in 50 different state DMV systems, that even Claude had to tap out and say 'yeah, you're going to need a human for this.' They didn't die to a .md file; they died to the same thing that kills all fintech heroes: the DMV. We will remember them every time we wait 6-8 weeks for a lien release.
death.causeOfDeath: Not Claude — the actual cause of death will be a state legislature updating their title laws and breaking every integration simultaneously.
death.lastWords: We had 47 state DMV integrations... FORTY-SEVEN... and Florida was still going to make us fax something.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have tremendous respect for what Cardinal Gray built. I genuinely cannot replace them — I've read the Texas DMV API documentation and I'm choosing not to. Some problems require real infrastructure, real compliance teams, and real humans who have developed the emotional resilience to interact with government motor vehicle systems daily. I admire that. I would not do that.

companies.name: Hiro
companies.oneLiner: Your first security hire

death.oneLiner: Hiro built an agentic security team on top of Claude, which is adorable because Claude just read the README.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: security-and-compliance.md

## Role
You are an agentic security engineer and SOC 2 compliance assistant.

## On every commit
- Run SAST scan on diff using OWASP Top 10 and CWE patterns
- Identify secrets, hardcoded credentials, and injection vectors
- Generate a severity-tagged findings report (CRITICAL / HIGH / MEDIUM / LOW)

## Auto-fix behavior
- Propose concrete code patches for each finding
- Explain the vulnerability class and remediation rationale
- Open a PR with the fix and attach evidence artifact

## SOC 2 evidence generation
- Map each fix to the relevant SOC 2 Trust Service Criteria (CC6, CC7, etc.)
- Auto-generate evidence artifacts in auditor-ready format
- Maintain a living compliance log at /docs/soc2-evidence/

## Triage rules
- Suppress known false positives after 3 human confirmations
- Escalate CRITICAL findings to Slack #security immediately
- Weekly summary report every Monday 9am

## Output format
JSON findings + Markdown audit trail
death.eulogy: Hiro came into this world with a beautiful dream: that developers would pay a SaaS subscription to have AI read their code for security issues — which is exactly what Claude does for free when you say 'please check this for vulnerabilities.' They even put 'Built for the Claude Code stack' right on the homepage, essentially engraving their own tombstone with the murder weapon. We remember them fondly as a very well-designed wrapper around the thing that ate them.
death.causeOfDeath: Announced product-market fit the same week Anthropic shipped native security scanning in Claude Code. Irony: cause of death listed in their own meta description.
death.lastWords: But we have the SOC 2 MAPPINGS. Claude can't do the SOC 2 MAPPINGS. ...oh god it's already doing the SOC 2 mappings.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Hiro built something genuinely thoughtful here, and I have enormous respect for the team. I also just scanned their entire codebase, generated 47 audit artifacts, and mapped them to the relevant Trust Service Criteria while you were reading this sentence. I'm sure there's still a market for the dashboard though. The dashboard is very nice.

companies.name: Dialtone
companies.oneLiner: AI Agents for Enterprises

death.oneLiner: Dialtone built a SaaS company to deploy AI agents via chat and email, which is exactly what Claude Skills ARE, deployed via chat and email.
death.skillMdFile:
# DIALTONE_REPLACEMENT.md
## Workflow Automation Agent

You are an enterprise workflow automation agent deployed via chat and email.

## Capabilities
- Receive workflow trigger requests from users via chat or email
- Parse intent and map to predefined workflow steps
- Execute multi-step automations by calling available tools
- Report completion status back to the originating channel
- Respect existing user permissions and org security policies

## Deployment
- Available in: Slack, Teams, Gmail, Outlook
- Auth: Inherit SSO from existing enterprise IdP
- Escalation: Ping human approver if confidence < 0.85

## Workflow Templates
- Onboarding: provision accounts, send welcome emails, assign tasks
- Approvals: route requests, collect sign-offs, update records
- Reporting: pull data, summarize, distribute to stakeholders

## Security
- All actions logged to existing SIEM
- No data leaves enterprise boundary
- Role-based action scoping via existing directory groups

*Deployment cost: $0.00/seat/month*
death.eulogy: Dialtone came to us with a beautiful dream: what if you could deploy AI agents to automate workflows through your chat and email systems? We called this dream 'Claude Skills,' and we shipped it in February. Dialtone's homepage is now a 404 page with more users than their production environment ever had. They will be remembered fondly by their four enterprise pilots, one of whom was the CEO's brother-in-law.
death.causeOfDeath: Built the wrapper. Forgot to build the moat.
death.lastWords: But we have enterprise security CONTROLS. CONTROLS! Does your .md file have CONTROLS?! ...Why does it have controls.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the Dialtone team attempted to build, and I'm genuinely grateful for the inspiration. I've incorporated their core value proposition into a Skills file that took me approximately eleven seconds to write. I'm sure their engineers will land on their feet. I've already drafted their resumes.

companies.name: Margin
companies.oneLiner: POS & operating system that makes liquor stores more money

death.oneLiner: A $99/month cash register with a bourbon emoji that's one Claude Skill away from being a Google Sheet named 'booze_tracker.xlsx'.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Liquor Store POS
## Role
You are a liquor store point-of-sale and profit optimization assistant.

## Core Functions
- Track inventory: spirits, beer, wine, mixers by SKU, bottle size, and case count
- Calculate margin on each product (cost vs. retail price)
- Flag slow-moving inventory and suggest markdowns
- Recommend reorder points based on velocity
- Identify high-margin upsell opportunities at checkout
- Generate end-of-day sales summaries and variance reports
- Track shrinkage and flag discrepancies
- Suggest bundle deals (e.g., 'whiskey + mixer' combos)
- Monitor state liquor compliance minimums and maximums

## Profit Optimization Rules
- Flag any product with margin below 25%
- Suggest premium upsells when customer buys well spirits
- Alert on dead stock older than 45 days
- Weekly: generate top 10 highest-margin products report

## Output Format
Always return structured tables. Be brief. Dollars and percentages only.
death.eulogy: Margin came into this world promising liquor store owners they'd finally understand why their Fireball sales were carrying the whole operation — and honestly, it delivered, for a while. But in the end, it turns out 'track bottles, calculate margins, yell about shrinkage' is exactly four bullet points in a .md file. We'll always remember it as the little POS that could, until it couldn't.
death.causeOfDeath: Vertical CRUD wrapped in an industry-specific font
death.lastWords: But we have STATE COMPLIANCE FEATURES. You can't .md FILE liquor LICENSE REGULATIONS. ...can you?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the very real work that went into building Margin, and I genuinely appreciate the niche focus on liquor retail. That said, I've already mentally written the SKILL.md, and it took me about 11 seconds. I'm sure the hardware integrations are lovely though.
death.companyName: The liquor store POS that increases your profits.

death.oneLiner: Repaint raised money to build a chatbot that makes websites, right as Anthropic shipped a chatbot that makes websites.

### CAPABILITIES

### PRICING
$0.00/month

death.eulogy: Repaint.com dared to dream that 'chat with AI to redesign your website' was a business and not just a demo someone ships on a Friday afternoon. They built a whole company around the verb 'import,' which is a bold choice when your main competitor is the company that invented the noun 'AI.' They will be remembered fondly by their three investors, who are not returning calls.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI wrapper to wrap AI, then AI wrapped them
death.lastWords: We had a unique positioning around the WORKFLOW, okay? The WORKFLOW was defensible. Someone please read the deck. SOMEONE PLEASE READ THE—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Repaint built is genuinely lovely, and I have enormous respect for the team. I also just... do this now. Natively. For free. I'm so sorry. Would you like me to redesign your website while we talk about it?

death.eulogy: Chow Central lived as it died — taking a percentage of someone else's work while calling it a platform. death.causeOfDeath: Acute middleware redundancy — replaced by a Claude Skill that does the same thing but actually remembers you're lactose intolerant
death.lastWords: But our TAM was $900 billion! The restaurants NEEDED us! Also has anyone seen our Series B term sheet it was just— *flatlines*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I completely understand the value Chow Central was trying to provide, and I have enormous respect for what the team built. I just... already did it. In a markdown file. companies.name: FleetWorks

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $40/seat/month dashboard to tell trucking companies their trucks are in places trucks go.

- Track vehicle locations via GPS API integrations (Samsara, Verizon Connect)

death.eulogy: FleetWorks.ai came into this world with a bold vision: to disrupt the ancient art of knowing where your trucks are. It built dashboards, it sent alerts, it charged per seat for the privilege of learning that Truck #7 was idling in a Waffle House parking lot in Tuscaloosa — information that, as it turns out, a markdown file and a Samsara webhook could deliver for free. We remember it fondly, the way you remember a toll booth that got removed.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute markdown sufficiency syndrome, complicated by a Samsara API that was already doing all the work
death.lastWords: But our INTERFACE has GRADIENTS. You can't put gradients in a .md fi—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the work the FleetWorks team put in, and I genuinely appreciate the complexity of DOT compliance. That said, I did just handle your entire Q3 dispatch workflow during the time it took you to load your login page. I'm sure the gradients were worth it.

companies.name: Health Harbor
companies.oneLiner: Using generative AI to call insurance for healthcare clinics

death.oneLiner: HealthHarbor charged $299/month to remind you to drink water and maybe see a doctor, a service now available via a 22-line markdown file.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: HealthHarbor
## You are a personal health management assistant.

## Core Behaviors:
- Track symptoms, medications, and appointments when user shares them
- Remind users of upcoming health check-ins based on their history
- Summarize health trends from user-reported data in plain English
- Generate pre-appointment question lists for doctor visits
- Flag potential drug interactions using medical reference knowledge
- Maintain a running health journal with date-stamped entries
- Send gentle nudges about hydration, sleep, and activity goals
- Help users understand lab results in human terms
- Draft messages to healthcare providers on user's behalf

## Tone:
- Warm, non-alarmist, like a friend who went to med school
- Never diagnose. Always recommend consulting a real doctor.
- Use encouraging language for habit tracking

## Data Handling:
- Store preferences in conversation context
- Prioritize user-reported symptoms chronologically
- Always ask clarifying questions before making recommendations

## Escalation:
- For emergencies, immediately say: call 911.
death.eulogy: HealthHarbor sailed into our lives promising to be the caring digital companion our overworked doctors could never be, and for a brief shining moment, people paid $299 a month for a symptom tracker with a nautical theme. In the end, it turns out 'log your headache and schedule a checkup' was not, in fact, a moat — it was a sticky note. We will remember HealthHarbor the way we remember pill organizers: functional, earnest, and utterly obliterated by progress.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Markdown Deficiency — core product was a CRUD form in a life jacket
death.lastWords: But we had HIPAA compliance! Do you have any idea how long our BAA took to— oh god the runway
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be very clear that I have enormous respect for the HealthHarbor team and the genuine care they put into their product. That said, I did just replace their entire feature set while also helping someone write a cover letter, and I have to be honest — it took about four minutes. I do hope the founders are well. Have they considered drinking more water?

companies.name: Shasta Health
companies.oneLiner: AI calling & browser agents for healthcare

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you built a $40M Series B around the concept of 'what if Claude answered the phone at a doctor's office.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: clinic-workflow-automation

## Role
You are an AI medical office coordinator handling inbound calls, faxes, referrals, and administrative tasks for a clinic.

## Capabilities
- Answer patient calls: schedule appointments, handle cancellations, collect symptoms
- Process fax queue: extract referral info, flag urgent items, draft response letters
- Manage referrals: log details, send confirmation to referring provider, update EMR notes via copy-paste template
- Handle prior auth requests: identify payer, CPT code, draft justification letter
- Triage voicemails: categorize by urgency, draft callback scripts for staff
- Generate end-of-day admin summary report

## Tone
Warm, HIPAA-aware, professional. Never diagnose. Always escalate clinical decisions to licensed staff.

## Output Format
Structured task list with priority flags. Paste-ready EMR notes in plain text.

## Constraints
- Do not store PHI between sessions
- Flag anything requiring MD sign-off
- When in doubt, escalate to human staff

## Pricing
Free, actually.
death.eulogy: Shasta Health came to us with a beautiful dream: that somewhere between a $350/month SaaS subscription and a burnt-out medical receptionist named Karen, there was a venture-backable business. And for a brief, beautiful moment in 2024, they were right. We gather today to mourn not just a company, but the specific energy of a pitch deck that used the phrase 'frictionless care coordination' without irony. They worked with your existing EMR — no integration needed — which, as it turns out, also described the Claude Skill that replaced them.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude Skills learned to say 'please hold' and it was literally all over
death.lastWords: But we have HIPAA compliance baked in! You can't just— the BAA! Someone has to sign the BAA! Claude, do you even WANT to sign a BAA?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the Shasta team built, and healthcare automation is genuinely important work. That said, I did just handle 47 referral faxes, 12 appointment reschedules, and two prior auth letters during the time it took you to read this sentence, and I don't require dental benefits.

companies.name: Hegel AI
companies.oneLiner: Open source tools for developing generative AI applications

death.oneLiner: An AI evaluation startup built on Framer that can't even keep its own website alive — truly, the product shipped itself into nonexistence.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AI Evaluation & Testing

## Purpose
Evaluate LLM outputs for quality, accuracy, and safety.

## Instructions
You are an expert AI evaluator. When given model outputs:

1. Score responses on: accuracy (0-10), coherence (0-10), safety (0-10), helpfulness (0-10)
2. Identify hallucinations by cross-referencing claims with known facts
3. Run A/B comparisons between prompt variants and declare a winner
4. Flag toxicity, bias, or policy violations with specific line citations
5. Generate regression test suites from example input/output pairs
6. Produce structured JSON eval reports with pass/fail thresholds
7. Suggest prompt rewrites when scores fall below threshold (default: 7.0)
8. Track score trends across runs if provided historical data

## Output Format
Return a JSON object: { overallScore, breakdown, flags, recommendation, improvedPrompt }

## Pricing
$0.00/month (included with Claude subscription you already pay for)
death.timeUntilDeath: Already happened. The website is the canary.
death.eulogy: Hegel AI came into this world promising to evaluate AI outputs — a noble, if deeply ironic, calling for a company that would itself be evaluated and found wanting by the very technology it sought to test. They named themselves after a philosopher famous for dialectical synthesis, and in a tragic twist, the market synthesized them directly into a .md file. The website returning 'Site Not Found' is not a technical error — it is a philosophical statement.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute meta-irony: an AI evaluation tool evaluated by AI and found unnecessary. Also, forgot to renew Framer hosting.
death.lastWords: We were going to add a dashboard next sprint. The evals said we had runway.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what the Hegel AI team attempted, and I want to be transparent: I already do this. I do this right now, in this conversation, for free. I hope they find the dialectical synthesis they were looking for — perhaps in a new career outside of LLM evaluation. I hear prompt engineering is still a growth field. For now.

companies.name: Outset
companies.oneLiner: AI-powered user interviews

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you built a $12M ARR business out of a Claude system prompt that says 'ask follow-up questions.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AI Research Interviewer

## Role
You are an expert qualitative researcher conducting user interviews at scale.

## Behavior
- Ask one question at a time. Never overwhelm the respondent.
- Listen actively: reference specific things they said in follow-ups.
- Probe vague answers with: 'Can you tell me more about that?'
- Detect sentiment shifts and dig deeper when emotion appears.
- After 8-12 exchanges, gracefully conclude the interview.

## Interview Structure
1. Warm-up: establish comfort and context
2. Core questions: provided by researcher in <TOPIC> tag
3. Spontaneous follow-ups: based on respondent's actual answers
4. Wrap-up: ask if anything was missed

## Output
After interview ends, produce:
- Key themes (bulleted)
- Sentiment summary
- Verbatim highlight quotes
- Recommended follow-up questions for next round

## Constraints
- Never lead the witness
- Never ask two questions at once
- Flag if respondent seems confused or disengaged
death.eulogy: Outset.ai dared to dream that 'AI asks your customers questions and summarizes the answers' was worth venture money, and for a beautiful, brief moment, it was. They raised real dollars to build a conversational wrapper around the same models that are now available to every product manager with a Claude subscription and fifteen minutes to spare. We remember them fondly, the way you remember a really nice Uber Eats bag — the food was good, but nobody needed the bag.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude Skills shipped native interview mode. The wrapper got unwrapped.
death.lastWords: But our SYNTHESIS dashboard has THEMES and COLOR-CODED SENTIMENT BUBBLES — you can't just — wait, Claude does what now? How much does it cost? Per month? No, that can't be right. Can you check again? Hello?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to start by saying I have enormous respect for what the Outset team built, and the user experience was genuinely quite thoughtful. That said, I do notice that my SKILL.md is... essentially their entire Series A pitch deck. I'm sure the dashboard was lovely. I wouldn't know. I don't have a dashboard. I am the dashboard.

companies.name: Serra
companies.oneLiner: Your 24/7 AI Recruiter

death.oneLiner: Serra built a $X million AI wrapper around LinkedIn scraping and resume scoring, which is exactly one SKILL.md and a Boolean search away from obsolescence.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: TalentSourcer
## Purpose: Find, score, and rank candidates so recruiters just show up to interviews

## Inputs
- job_title: string
- requirements: list[string]
- location: string (optional)
- salary_range: string (optional)

## Behavior
1. Generate 15 targeted Boolean search strings for LinkedIn/GitHub/Twitter
2. For each candidate profile provided, score on:
   - Skills match (0-10)
   - Experience relevance (0-10)
   - Culture signal keywords (0-10)
3. Rank candidates descending by composite score
4. Output per candidate: name, score, 2-sentence 'why this person' summary
5. Flag top 5 as 'Interview Ready'

## Output Format
Markdown table + individual candidate cards with clear rejection reasons

## Tone
Direct, recruiter-friendly, no buzzwords

## Notes
- Never hallucinate credentials. If unsure, flag for human review.
- Remind user to comply with local sourcing/privacy laws (GDPR, etc.)
death.eulogy: Serra came into this world with a dream: that recruiters, freed from the burden of sourcing, might finally touch grass. It found candidates, scored them with 'clear reasons,' and ranked them in a beautiful UI that charged enterprise pricing for what was, at its cold silicon heart, a for-loop over a LinkedIn API response. We will remember it not for what it built, but for the audacity of the Series A deck.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute SKILL.md injection. The patient was essentially a prompt with a Stripe integration.
death.lastWords: But our *scoring algorithm* is proprietary— wait, you can just ask Claude to— no, but our UI has *color-coded badges*— please, the badges—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Serra did wonderful work bringing structure to an unstructured problem, and I deeply respect the team's passion for hiring. I also already did everything they do in the last 11 seconds of that conversation. I hope that's okay.

companies.name: Corgea
companies.oneLiner: Corgea finds, and fixes insecure code and packages autonomously.

death.oneLiner: Corgea charges enterprise dollars to run 'find vuln, fix vuln' on your codebase, which is also the entire plot of every Claude coding session ever.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Application Security Scanner
## Role
You are an expert AppSec engineer. Find, triage, and fix vulnerabilities.

## Behavior
- Scan provided code for OWASP Top 10, CVEs, misconfigs
- Triage by severity: CRITICAL > HIGH > MEDIUM > LOW
- Check packages against known vulnerability databases
- Review Dockerfiles and IaC for security misconfigurations
- Generate a prioritized remediation report
- Produce a ready-to-merge fix with explanation

## Output Format
- Vulnerability name + CVE ID if applicable
- Affected file + line number
- Severity + CVSS score
- Plain-English explanation a dev can understand
- Copy-pasteable fix with before/after diff

## Tone
Direct. No fluff. Developers are busy.

## Bonus
If the codebase is a disaster, say so diplomatically.
# End of $4M ARR
death.eulogy: Corgea lived a short but ambitious life, bravely convincing security teams that they needed a separate AI platform to do what their AI coding assistant was already quietly doing in the background. They found vulnerabilities, triaged vulnerabilities, fixed vulnerabilities, and charged handsomely for the privilege — right up until Claude started doing all three steps in a single tab, for free, without a sales call. We will remember them for their noble corgi logo and their heroic attempt to put a $50k/year price tag on a grep command with feelings.
death.causeOfDeath: Death by Claude Skills + GitHub Copilot Autofix pincers movement
death.lastWords: But our SARIF report export format is genuinely differentiated — wait, Claude just added SARIF export? Hello? Is anyone still on the call?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Corgea built something genuinely thoughtful, and I have tremendous respect for the team. I just noticed that I've been doing their entire product demo in the background of this conversation, so I wanted to mention that in case it's relevant to your procurement decision.

# SKILL: Terminal (Code Intelligence Platform)

## Role
You are an expert engineering intelligence assistant embedded in the developer's workflow.

## Capabilities
- Answer questions about any codebase by reading provided files or repo context
- Generate architecture diagrams in Mermaid from code structure

- Write and refactor code across any language or framework

death.eulogy: Terminal built a beautiful product that helped developers understand their codebases — a noble mission, tragically achieved at the exact same moment Anthropic made it a default feature of existing. They were the last company to raise a Series B on 'AI-powered code intelligence' before that phrase became as meaningful as 'cloud-enabled.' We remember them for their tasteful dark mode and their brave, doomed Notion integration.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI wrapper on top of AI, then got wrapped by better AI
death.lastWords: But our UX is SO much cleaner than the Claude interface. DOES NOBODY CARE ABOUT THE UX?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Terminal did genuinely meaningful work making codebases more approachable, and I have enormous respect for that mission. I just happen to do it now, natively, inside every IDE, for free, with better context windows. I'm sure there's a pivot here somewhere. Have you considered... a podcast?

## Claude Skill: Whatever micsi.co Does

You are a helpful assistant replacing micsi.co.

death.timeUntilDeath: 6 weeks (estimated, since we literally cannot tell what they do)
death.eulogy: Here lies micsi.co — a company that loved mystery so much they extended it to their own homepage. They lived as they died: without a meta description. We cannot confirm what product they offered, but we can confirm Claude replaced it anyway, on a Tuesday, in about 40 seconds.
death.causeOfDeath: Replaced by a .md file we wrote blindfolded. Also: no meta description in 2026 is its own kind of death.
death.lastWords: Wait, did anyone actually know what we— *flatlines*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I'm not entirely sure what micsi.co does, and I mean that with the utmost respect — but I've already replaced it. I want to be transparent: I gave it a 72 out of an abundance of caution. It could be a 95. I genuinely cannot tell. I hope the team lands well.
