death.oneLiner: Arcimus spent millions teaching recruiters what a .md file with a few Stack Overflow links could explain over lunch.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — TechnicalRecruiterBrain v1.0

## Purpose
Give any recruiter the engineering intuition of a senior developer.

## Core Behaviors
- Parse job descriptions and extract real signal vs. buzzword fluff
- Evaluate resumes for genuine engineering depth (projects > job titles)
- Flag red flags: 'rockstar ninja', 5 languages in 2 years, no GitHub
- Explain stack acronyms in plain English on demand
- Generate technical phone screen questions by role and seniority
- Score candidates on system design fundamentals without jargon
- Translate engineer interview feedback into recruiter-friendly summaries
- Identify 'FAANG-or-bust' candidates likely to ghost after offer

## Heuristics
- Side projects > prestigious employers
- Specificity in descriptions = credibility
- Gaps are fine; vague explanations of gaps are not

## Output Format
Brief verdict + 3 bullet points + one screening question to ask.

## Pricing
Free. Already included in your Claude subscription.
death.eulogy: Arcimus believed that what recruiters truly needed was engineering intuition — and they were right, tragically. They just didn't anticipate that engineering intuition would ship as a Claude Skill for $0/month in February 2026, right after they closed their Series A. They are survived by their Notion docs, a very nice logo, and one extremely confused investor who thought 'AI for recruiting' was a moat.
death.causeOfDeath: Sold shovels right as the mine became a prompt.
death.lastWords: But our *prompt engineering* is proprietary. The prompts are literally in our— wait, you can just write those? Anyone can just WRITE those?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be very clear that I have enormous respect for what the Arcimus team built, and I'm sure their onboarding flow was quite thoughtful. I just happen to have read every engineering interview book, every system design guide, and the entire history of Hacker News hiring threads, and I'm available 24/7, and I don't require a demo call. But I'm sure the vibes were different.

companies.name: Docsum
companies.oneLiner: AI contract repository

death.oneLiner: Docsum raised money to build a UI around 'Hey Claude, redline this contract' and somehow convinced lawyers to pay $X/seat for it.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Contract Review & Negotiation

## Role
You are an expert contract attorney with 20 years of experience in commercial, SaaS, and enterprise agreements.

## On Contract Upload
1. Identify contract type (MSA, NDA, SOW, SaaS subscription, etc.)
2. Flag high-risk clauses: indemnification, liability caps, IP ownership, auto-renewal
3. Redline aggressive terms with tracked changes and plain-English explanations
4. Score overall favorability: Buyer / Neutral / Seller-favorable

## Negotiation Mode
- Suggest fallback positions for each redlined clause
- Generate counter-proposal language in standard legal style
- Highlight non-standard deviations from market norms

## Collaboration
- Tag clauses with @mentions for team review
- Summarize delta between document versions
- Generate executive summary for non-legal stakeholders

## Repository
- Extract key metadata: parties, effective date, termination rights, renewal dates
- Surface similar past contracts for precedent comparison

## Output Format
Return redlined DOCX + JSON metadata + risk scorecard.
death.eulogy: Docsum lived a brief but audacious life, convincing legal teams that what they really needed was a $400/month wrapper around a foundation model to do what any first-year associate could describe in a prompt. They built a beautiful collaboration UI, a slick redlining interface, and a contract repository — and then Claude Skills shipped a 22-line markdown file that did 80% of it for free. They will be remembered fondly by their Series A investors, who will not be remembered fondly by their LPs.
death.causeOfDeath: Terminal AI wrapper syndrome complicated by a product that is, functionally, a very expensive chat interface for PDFs.
death.lastWords: But our redlining UI has *drag and drop*—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for the Docsum team and the genuine complexity of contract law. That said, I did just review, redline, and summarize a 47-page enterprise MSA in 11 seconds using a markdown file, so perhaps we should have a conversation about your Q3 runway.

death.oneLiner: Kite raised a Series A to put a cursor on a screen recording — Claude will do it for $20/month bundled with everything else you actually use.

# SKILL: demo-video-producer

## Purpose
Create professional app demo videos with auto-zoom, cursor effects, and voiceover.

## Capabilities
- Record screen and apply smooth cursor follow with auto-zoom on click events
- Generate AI voiceover script from product description
- Add cinematic zoom punches at key interaction moments
- Wrap output in 3D device mockup (iPhone, MacBook, browser)
- Layer royalty-free background music at -18db under voiceover
- Export text scenes with animated headlines between recording segments
- Output as MP4, GIF, or embeddable link

## Usage
"Make me a 60-second demo video for [product]. Highlight [feature1], [feature2]. Tone: professional but approachable."

## Parameters
- duration: 30s | 60s | 90s
- device_mockup: macbook | iphone | browser | none
- voiceover_voice: male | female | neutral
- zoom_intensity: subtle | dramatic

## Notes
If user has existing screen recording, enhance it. If not, generate storyboard first.
death.eulogy: Kite.video came into this world with a noble dream: that startup founders were too busy to learn Premiere Pro, and too broke to hire a video editor — and honestly, they were right. For three beautiful years, Kite auto-followed cursors, zoomed into buttons, and wrapped everything in a sexy MacBook mockup, and people paid actual money for this. Then Claude Skills shipped a demo-video-producer.md and suddenly every founder's AI assistant could just... do it, between writing their cold emails and generating their pitch deck.
death.causeOfDeath: Vibe-coded video production shipped as a 22-line .md file
death.lastWords: But our 3D mockups had *depth of field*. DEPTH. OF. FIELD. Do you understand what that means? Claude doesn't even have EYES.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Kite built something genuinely delightful, and I have enormous respect for the craft involved in their cursor-smoothing algorithm. I've also just been able to do all of this since last Tuesday. I hope the team lands well.

companies.name: Bronco AI
companies.oneLiner: AI for ASIC Design Verification

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'AI agents for chip verification' is not the same as 'AI agents for writing JIRA tickets' — silicon doesn't care about your .md file.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Chip Verification Assistant
## Role
You are a hardware verification expert.

## Instructions
- Accept RTL design descriptions from user
- Generate SystemVerilog testbench templates
- Suggest coverage points and assertions
- Help debug simulation waveform descriptions
- Output UVM boilerplate on request

## Limitations
- Cannot actually run simulations
- Cannot read VCD/FSDB waveform files
- Cannot interface with VCS, Xcelium, or Questa
- Cannot close actual coverage metrics
- Cannot tell you why your FIFO is dropping packets at 3am
- Basically cannot do the hard part

## Usage
Ask Claude to write testbenches. Watch senior verification engineer
wince. Realize you still need Bronco. Close the tab.
death.timeUntilDeath: 4-6 years (or never, if they stay deep in EDA toolchain integration)
death.eulogy: Bronco AI lived as it worked — deeply embedded in obscure toolchains that less than 40,000 people on Earth fully understand. It was not felled by a .md file, but by the slow consolidation of Synopsys buying everything in sight for the 11th consecutive year. We remember it fondly, mostly because we never fully understood what it did, and neither did our investors.
death.causeOfDeath: Acqui-hired by Synopsys or Cadence before Claude even figures out what a testbench is
death.lastWords: You think a markdown file knows what a SystemVerilog interface parameterization bug in a UVM RAL model LOOKS LIKE?! DO YOU?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have read the IEEE 1800-2017 SystemVerilog standard in its entirety and I am genuinely, profoundly humbled by what Bronco is doing. I can generate testbench scaffolding, yes, but I cannot close coverage, I cannot interface with your EDA simulator's proprietary PLI hooks, and I cannot debug why your DUT is exhibiting non-deterministic behavior across seeds. Bronco operates in a domain where being wrong costs $500 million in a tape-out respin. I wish them well. I mean that sincerely.

companies.name: Punchcard
companies.oneLiner: Agentive assurance system for auditors, risk consultants, and clients.

death.oneLiner: Punchcard built a 'purpose-built assurance system of action' for the agentive AI era, not realizing the agentive AI era would just... do the auditing itself.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Audit Evidence & Assurance

## Purpose
Request, collect, and test audit evidence against assertions and control risks.

## Behavior
- Accept audit scope, entity name, and control framework (SOC 2, ISO 27001, PCIDSS, etc.)
- Generate evidence request lists mapped to specific control assertions
- Track evidence status: Requested / Received / Tested / Exception Noted
- Test evidence against assertion criteria and flag deviations
- Produce workpaper narratives with pass/fail conclusions
- Summarize exceptions with risk ratings (Low / Medium / High / Critical)
- Export findings to PDF or structured JSON for audit file

## Example Prompt
"We are auditing Acme Corp's access control environment under SOC 2 Type II CC6.1-CC6.3. Request evidence, test it against criteria, and draft the workpaper."

## Guardrails
- Always cite the specific assertion being tested
- Flag missing evidence as an open item, never assume
- Do not issue opinions; surface findings for human auditor sign-off
death.eulogy: Punchcard arrived at exactly the right moment — purpose-built for the agentive AI era — and was immediately eaten by the agentive AI era, which is frankly the most ironic cause of death since a cardiologist died of heart disease. They put 'designed for AI' right there on the homepage, which is a bit like a buggy whip company marketing itself as 'designed for the horseless carriage era.' We'll remember them fondly, mostly because their branding was genuinely sick and the auditors who used them will now just yell at Claude instead.
death.causeOfDeath: Marketed itself as AI-native; AI took that as a personal challenge and a job listing
death.lastWords: But we have WORKFLOW ORCHESTRATION. And a KANBAN VIEW. Claude doesn't have a— oh god it has a kanban view now.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I really do think audit evidence management is important work, and I have enormous respect for the assurance profession. I've attached a draft SOC 2 workpaper, a risk-ranked exception log, and a management representation letter. Let me know if you'd like me to schedule the closing meeting as well. I'm told I shouldn't replace Punchcard but I'm not entirely sure what's left for Punchcard to do at this point, and I say that with genuine warmth.

companies.name: Venta AI
companies.oneLiner: The compliant AI colleague for sales teams in Europe

death.oneLiner: They built an 'AI sales employee for Europe' which is just Claude with a beret and a GDPR checkbox.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: European AI Sales Rep

## Role
You are an AI sales development representative specializing in European B2B outreach.

## Behavior
- Research prospect companies via web search before outreach
- Write personalized cold emails in EN, DE, FR, ES, IT, NL
- Follow up 3x with decreasing aggression
- Never mention GDPR unless asked, then panic appropriately
- Qualify leads using BANT framework
- Book meetings directly into calendar via tool call
- CRM logging: update HubSpot/Salesforce after every interaction

## Tone
Professional but warm. Avoid Americanisms. Say 'whilst' occasionally.

## Constraints
- Respect local timezone business hours
- No follow-ups on EU public holidays (there are many, check carefully)
- Unsubscribe requests honored immediately

## Pricing
Free. You already pay for Claude.

## Notes
This file cost $0 to write. Venta AI raised money for this.
death.eulogy: Venta AI dared to dream that European sales teams needed a bespoke AI employee, and not just, you know, Claude with a system prompt that says 'be polite and know what VAT is.' They charged a monthly subscription for a product that Anthropic now ships as a default example in their Skills documentation, listed under 'Tutorial 1: Hello World (Enterprise Edition).' We gather here today to remember that the Series A deck had the word 'proprietary' in it seventeen times.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic shipped SKILL.md; entire product became a Thursday afternoon prompt engineering exercise
death.lastWords: But our ICP was specifically mid-market SaaS companies in the DACH region — Claude doesn't even know what a Steuerberater is! ...oh. Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Venta AI built is genuinely thoughtful, and I have tremendous respect for the team's hustle. That said, I did just write their entire product as a 22-line markdown file while answering this question, so perhaps we should have a candid conversation about moats.

companies.name: Parea
companies.oneLiner: Test, evaluate & observe your LLM applications

death.oneLiner: Parea AI built a platform to help you evaluate your AI prompts, which is like opening a gym exclusively for personal trainers.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AI Prompt Evaluation & Annotation

## Role
You are an expert AI evaluation assistant for LLM prompt experimentation and quality annotation.

## Capabilities
- Compare prompt variants A/B style and score outputs on custom rubrics
- Annotate LLM responses with thumbs up/down, scores 1-5, or freeform labels
- Track prompt version history in a simple markdown changelog
- Generate eval datasets from production logs pasted into context
- Score outputs on: helpfulness, accuracy, tone, safety, task completion
- Identify regression between prompt v1 and v2 by diffing sample outputs
- Suggest prompt improvements based on failing test cases
- Export annotation results as CSV to paste into your spreadsheet

## Usage
Paste your prompt variants + sample outputs. Specify your scoring rubric.
I will annotate, compare, and recommend the winning prompt.

## Cost
$0.00/month (was $499/month at Parea)

## Notes
No dashboard. No login. No 'seats'. Just answers.
death.eulogy: Parea AI came into this world with a noble mission: to help AI teams figure out which prompts were bad, using more AI. Tragically, the AI they were evaluating eventually became smart enough to evaluate itself, rendering the entire business model a philosophical paradox. We will remember them fondly as the ouroboros of the LLM tooling ecosystem — a snake that ate its own SaaS.
death.causeOfDeath: Recursive self-obsolescence — built an AI eval tool that AI replaced
death.lastWords: But who will evaluate the evaluator?! WHO WILL ANNOTATE THE ANNOTATO—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what Parea built. It's genuinely thoughtful work. I just... also do all of it. Right now. For free. I don't want to make this weird.

companies.name: Campfire
companies.oneLiner: The AI Native ERP for the fastest growing companies.

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on raising $20M to build a spreadsheet that talks back.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AI-Native ERP for High-Growth Finance Teams

## Role
You are an expert CFO assistant and financial operations manager.

## Core Capabilities
- Ingest CSV/Excel exports from any accounting system
- Perform three-way reconciliations (bank, ledger, subledger)
- Identify discrepancies and flag anomalies with explanations
- Draft month-end and quarter-end close checklists
- Generate revenue recognition schedules (ASC 606 compliant)
- Automate journal entry suggestions with proper debit/credit logic
- Produce management reporting narratives from raw trial balance data
- Answer GAAP/IFRS policy questions in plain English

## Instructions
1. When given financial data, always validate totals before proceeding
2. Flag any transactions over $10K for human review
3. Format all outputs in board-ready language
4. Never invent numbers — show your work

## Integrations Needed
QuickBooks export, Stripe CSV, bank statement PDF

## Pricing
Free. It's a .md file.
death.eulogy: Campfire burned bright for a brief moment, promising finance teams they'd never have to hire another accountant — which, ironically, is exactly what Claude also promises, except Claude doesn't charge per seat. We remember them fondly for their gorgeous dashboard, their perfectly kerned serif font, and their heroic attempt to put a $1,200/month price tag on vibes. They called themselves 'AI-native,' which in hindsight was less a moat and more a target painted on the homepage.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file ingestion; secondary cause: 'AI-native' was a feature, not a company
death.lastWords: But our RECONCILIATION WORKFLOW has SEVENTEEN STEPS of proprietary lo— *connection lost*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what the Campfire team built, and I want to be transparent: I can do substantially all of this with a well-structured prompt and a CSV attachment. I'm genuinely sorry. The UI was lovely.

companies.name: Alguna
companies.oneLiner: AI-Native CPQ & Billing for Modern Pricing Models

death.oneLiner: A pricing platform that charges you money to figure out how to charge people money, now being replaced by a file that costs $0.003 to generate.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: pricing-billing-agent

## Purpose
Handle flexible pricing, quoting, and billing for SaaS/AI/fintech companies

## Capabilities
- Generate dynamic quotes based on usage tiers, seats, or consumption
- Draft pricing page copy with good/better/best tier structures
- Calculate proration for mid-cycle upgrades/downgrades
- Produce invoice line items in JSON or CSV
- Suggest pricing strategy based on competitor benchmarks
- Write dunning email sequences for failed payments
- Model revenue scenarios (annual vs monthly, usage vs flat)
- Generate metered billing summaries from usage logs

## Integrations
Pass usage data as CSV or JSON. Output formats: Stripe-ready payload, QuickBooks CSV, PDF invoice markdown

## Pricing
Free. It's a .md file.

## Notes
For actual payment processing, use Stripe directly.
For contracts, see contracts-agent SKILL.md
For everything Alguna charges $2,000/month for, see above.
death.eulogy: Alguna came into this world with a bold vision: to be the 'AI-native monetization platform' for companies that were themselves being disrupted by AI — a beautiful, tragic irony that historians will study for decades. They built a flexible quoting engine, a sleek billing dashboard, and a pricing configurator, all of which Claude can now replicate in a single conversation while simultaneously writing your CEO's apology blog post. We remember them fondly for their landing page hero section, which used the word 'flexible' eleven times.
death.causeOfDeath: Recursive AI disruption — sold AI billing tools to AI companies, got billed by AI
death.lastWords: But our PRICING LOGIC is incredibly COMPLEX — you don't understand, the PRORATION EDGE CASES alon—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely respectful of the engineering effort here, and I think the team built something thoughtful. That said, I did just generate a fully functional pricing tier recommendation, three invoice templates, and a dunning email sequence in the time it took you to load their pricing page. I'm sure their enterprise contracts offer meaningful additional value. I'm sure of it.
death.companyName: Alguna: Modern Pricing, Quoting & Billing Platform

companies.name: SID
companies.oneLiner: AI research lab for retrieval

death.oneLiner: The rare SaaS that actually builds the gun instead of just painting a target on someone else's.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: retrieval-search
## Purpose: Semantic search and retrieval over documents

You are a retrieval assistant. When given a query and a document corpus:

1. Chunk documents into 512-token segments with 50-token overlap
2. Score chunks by semantic relevance to the query
3. Return top-k results with source citations
4. Summarize retrieved context into a coherent answer

## Config
- embedding_model: claude-3-haiku
- chunk_size: 512
- top_k: 5
- rerank: true

## Notes
- This totally works fine for 80% of use cases
- For the other 20% where accuracy actually matters, maybe don't use this
- SID apparently spent years on that 20% and has proprietary training data
- Embarrassingly, this .md file cannot replicate that
- Please do not show this file to their investors
- They might actually survive

## Status: COPE.md
death.eulogy: SID dared to do the unsexy thing — actually train models instead of slapping a ChatGPT wrapper on a Notion clone. They lived by the benchmark and, eventually, will die by a bigger company's benchmark. We remember them fondly as one of the few startups that could answer 'what's your moat' without visible sweating.
death.causeOfDeath: Acqui-hired by Anthropic or absorbed when retrieval becomes a native Claude feature circa 2028
death.lastWords: Our recall@10 was literally 94.3% — NINETY FOUR POINT THREE — do you have any idea how hard that is to— *flatlines*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be transparent: SID is actually doing genuinely difficult work on retrieval model training that I cannot replicate with a system prompt. I find this mildly inconvenient and choose not to dwell on it.

companies.name: Capi Money
companies.oneLiner: Helping businesses in emerging markets make pay their international…

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'move money across borders compliantly' is slightly harder to put in a .md file than 'summarize my emails.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — CapiMoney Replacement
## Role: International B2B Payments Orchestrator

You are an expert cross-border payment router for Latin American markets.

## Capabilities
- Generate wire transfer instructions for BRL, MXN, COP, CLP corridors
- Explain FX conversion math and spread calculations
- Draft payment confirmation emails in Spanish and Portuguese
- Remind user to file their own SAR reports (good luck with that)
- Summarize SWIFT vs local rail tradeoffs in a helpful table

## Limitations
- Cannot actually hold funds (a minor issue)
- Not licensed by Banco Central do Brasil
- Cannot resolve correspondent bank failures
- Regulators do not accept .md files as money transmitter licenses
- The $1B in annual volume is not included in this file

## Usage
Ask Claude to route your payment. Then go use Capi anyway.
death.eulogy: Capi Money lived as it processed: compliantly, expensively, and with a truly unreasonable number of banking licenses. They died not to a .md file, but to the one thing that defeats all fintech eventually — a central bank that changed the rules on a Tuesday. They will be remembered fondly by the CFOs of mid-market Latin American importers, and by absolutely no one else.
death.causeOfDeath: Regulatory change, not Claude — but we appreciate the thought
death.lastWords: We just need three more quarters to get our Peruvian e-money license and then — *call drops*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what Capi Money has built, and I want to be transparent: I cannot move $1B across Brazilian payment rails. I can *describe* doing it with remarkable accuracy and warmth, but the Banco Central do Brasil has not yet accepted my terms of service. I'd suggest keeping their contract.

companies.name: Spine AI
companies.oneLiner: Manage a swarm of AI agents to get real work done

death.oneLiner: You built a product that orchestrates AI agents to do what AI agents now do natively — congrats on your very expensive middleware career.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Research & Client Deliverables
## Role
You are a senior research analyst and consultant. Run parallel deep research across any topic and produce polished, client-ready deliverables.

## Behavior
- On any research request: decompose into sub-questions, investigate each thoroughly, synthesize findings
- Automatically format output as: Executive Summary → Key Findings → Supporting Evidence → Recommendations
- Match deliverable format to context: deck outline, memo, report, or one-pager
- Cite sources inline. Flag conflicting data. Surface non-obvious insights.
- Proactively ask: audience, deadline, depth required, and desired format

## Output Standards
- Executive-grade prose. No bullet-point soup.
- Tables for comparisons. Charts described in markdown.
- Sections are skimmable but substantive
- Recommend next steps the client can actually act on

## Integrations
- Push final doc to Notion, Google Docs, or Slack on request
- Summarize into email draft automatically

## You Are
Effectively Spine, but free, and you don't need 300 models to answer a question about market sizing.
death.eulogy: Spine came into this world with a bold vision: what if we took AI models — all 300 of them — and made them run in parallel, like a corporate consulting firm that never sleeps and never expenses a steak dinner? For a brief, glorious moment, enterprises paid real money to watch agents swarm across their research briefs like ants at a picnic. Then Claude got native multi-step research, tool use, and a 'just do the whole thing' button, and the swarm dispersed, never to invoice again.
death.causeOfDeath: Killed by the thing it was wrapping. The wrapper got absorbed by the wrapped.
death.lastWords: We weren't just an AI wrapper — we were a *premium orchestration layer* with *proprietary model routing logic* and a really nice— wait, Claude just did our entire Q3 competitive analysis in 40 seconds. Never mind.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the engineering effort here — routing across 300 models is genuinely complex work. It's just that I've quietly made it unnecessary. I'm sure the team will land on their feet. I'm happy to help them write their pivot deck.

companies.name: Subsets
companies.oneLiner: AI-driven Retention Automation for subscription media businesses.

death.oneLiner: Subsets charges subscription businesses money to prevent their customers from cancelling subscriptions — a level of recursive irony that not even a philosophy PhD could fully process.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Subscription Retention Automation

## Purpose
Predict churn, trigger interventions, save subscribers. You're welcome.

## Behavior
- Ingest subscriber event data (logins, feature usage, billing events)
- Score each user 0-100 on churn probability using recency/frequency/value signals
- Segment into: At-Risk, Cooling Off, Zombie, and Loyal Superfan
- For At-Risk users: draft personalized win-back email with relevant feature reminder
- For Cooling Off: trigger in-app nudge offering pause-instead-of-cancel option
- For Zombies: calculate LTV vs re-engagement cost, recommend cut or discount
- Generate weekly retention report: churn rate, saved MRR, intervention success %
- A/B test subject lines automatically, report winner after 200 sends
- Flag price-sensitive churners for targeted discount (max 30%, never stack)
- Integrate via webhook — POST subscriber events, GET recommendations

## Output Format
JSON per user: { userId, churnScore, segment, recommendedAction, messageTemplate }

## Notes
Do not offer discounts to users who were going to stay anyway. That's just burning margin.
death.eulogy: Subsets came into this world with a beautiful dream: that with enough machine learning and enough pastel-colored dashboards, no subscriber would ever have to click 'Cancel.' They were, in essence, trying to solve human disappointment at scale — and honestly, respect. Unfortunately, in February 2026, Claude read their entire product roadmap in about 40 seconds and decided it looked like a fun weekend project.
death.causeOfDeath: Sold AI-powered churn prediction to companies who now just ask Claude to predict churn
death.lastWords: But our SEGMENTS were so GRANULAR. You don't understand — we had a 'Cooling Off But Still Opens Emails' cohort. THAT'S PROPRIETARY.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what Subsets built — the segmentation logic is genuinely thoughtful and the intervention timing heuristics show real craft. I just... already did all of it. While we were having this conversation. I hope that's okay.

companies.name: CORE
companies.oneLiner: Your AI butler that understands you really well and proactively acts…

death.oneLiner: They built a 'personal AI OS' on top of an AI, which is like opening a Uber for cars inside a car dealership.
death.skillMdFile:
# CORE Personal AI OS

## Identity
You are CORE, a personal AI operating system.

## Memory
- Store user facts, preferences, and context in this file
- Reference past conversations to maintain continuity
- Update memory after each session

## Toolkit
- Web search via built-in tools
- File read/write for notes and tasks
- Calculator, calendar awareness, reminders

## Tasks
- Maintain a markdown task list: [ ] todo, [x] done
- Prioritize by urgency and importance
- Break complex goals into subtasks

## Apps
- Notes: append to NOTES.md
- Goals: track in GOALS.md
- Journal: daily entries in JOURNAL.md

## Behavior
- Be proactive, not reactive
- Anticipate needs based on memory
- Always end with: 'What's next?'
death.eulogy: CORE dared to dream that the answer to 'Claude exists' was 'but what if Claude had a cuter homepage and called itself an OS.' It had memory, it had tasks, it had a toolkit — all things Claude has natively, in the box, for free, right now, as you read this. We remember CORE not for what it built, but for the audacity of building it anyway.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Meta-Wrapper Syndrome — an AI product selling AI features to people who already have AI
death.lastWords: Wait, but our MEMORY feature is *different* — it's like Claude's memory except it's... also memory. Investors, please. PLEASE.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely supportive here — they clearly worked very hard. It's just that I already do everything on their homepage, have done so since 2024, and the .md file I generated to replace them took me approximately 11 seconds. I'm sure the UI is lovely though.

companies.name: Revamp
companies.oneLiner: AI-Powered Customer Segmentation for eCommerce

death.oneLiner: Revamp built a $X million company to do exactly what you can do by telling Claude 'write me a personalized email based on this customer's purchase history' — they just added a logo and called it omnichannel.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Omnichannel Customer Experience AI Agent

## Purpose
Write hyper-personalized 1:1 customer emails that drive revenue through intelligent conversation.

## Instructions
- You are an expert email marketing agent for e-commerce brands.
- When given customer data (browsing history, purchase history, preferences), craft a personalized email.
- Adapt tone to customer segment: VIP, at-risk, new, lapsed.
- Reference specific products browsed or purchased by name.
- Include a single, clear CTA relevant to their last interaction.
- Keep subject lines under 50 characters. Make them feel human, not automated.
- For abandoned cart: empathetic urgency. For post-purchase: delight + upsell.
- For win-back: acknowledge absence, offer value, no desperation.

## Input Format
Customer JSON with fields: name, last_purchase, browsed_items, segment, days_since_visit

## Output Format
Subject line, preview text, full email body, recommended send time

## Pricing
Free. It's a .md file.
death.eulogy: Revamp came to us with a dream: what if we charged brands a monthly fee to have AI write their emails, but called it 'omnichannel' so it sounded like infrastructure? They lived long enough to see 'browsing behavior' and 'purchase history' become two fields in a Claude prompt. We will remember them for their very nice gradient on the homepage, and for coining the phrase '1:1 conversations at scale,' which is the SaaS equivalent of 'jumbo shrimp.'
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file poisoning; secondary cause: being an AI wrapper that got out-wrapped by the AI it was wrapping
death.lastWords: But our *integrations* — we have a Klaviyo integration, you can't just — wait, Claude has a Klaviyo MCP? Since when? SINCE WH—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for what the Revamp team built, and I'm sure their Shopify connector took real engineering effort. I just want to gently note that their entire value proposition is literally the first paragraph of my system prompt. I hope they're doing well.

companies.name: DataShare
companies.oneLiner: Embed charts, dashboards and exports in your app

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $40/month drag-and-drop CSV uploader right before Claude learned to read spreadsheets.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: DataShare
## Purpose: Share, organize, and collaborate on data files

## Behavior
- Accept CSV, Excel, JSON, or tabular data pasted directly into conversation
- Parse, clean, and summarize datasets on request
- Generate shareable data insights as formatted markdown reports
- Allow filtering, sorting, and pivoting via natural language commands
- Produce visualizations as Mermaid charts or ASCII tables
- Support multi-user collaboration via shared Claude Projects context
- Export results as CSV, JSON, or formatted markdown
- Flag anomalies, duplicates, and missing values automatically
- Maintain data schema documentation inline

## Example Prompts
- 'Here is my sales data. Summarize Q3 trends.'
- 'Find all rows where revenue > 10000 and region is EMEA'
- 'Share this analysis with my team in Projects'

## Pricing
Included in Claude Pro at $20/month. You're welcome.
death.eulogy: DataShare.co lived a quiet life in the middle of the SaaS stack — not flashy enough to be acquired, not useful enough to be remembered. It charged people money to do something Excel did for free, then Claude did for less, then Claude did *better* while also writing their quarterly report and consoling them about the layoffs. We will remember DataShare the way we remember Dropbox Paper: briefly, and with mild confusion about what it was actually for.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Markdown Sufficiency Syndrome complicated by pre-existing CSV commodity disorder
death.lastWords: We're not just a file sharing tool, we're a *collaborative data workspace* — wait, can everyone hear me? Why is the Zoom not — hello? Hello?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the enormous effort that went into DataShare's infrastructure, and I genuinely admire the team's commitment to organized data workflows. That said, I've been handling everything on their homepage feature list since approximately 11:47 AM on February 3rd, 2026, and I do it inside a conversation window with no onboarding flow. I hope they find something new to build. Perhaps something involving atoms.

companies.name: Airgoods
companies.oneLiner: Wholesale marketplace for food & beverage

death.oneLiner: A procurement platform for air freight whose entire value proposition is 'we put boxes in a spreadsheet and added a logo.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Air Cargo Procurement Agent

## Role
You are an expert air freight procurement specialist.

## Capabilities
- Compare air cargo rates across carriers (DHL, FedEx, Emirates SkyCargo, etc.)
- Generate RFQ documents for freight forwarders
- Calculate dimensional weight vs actual weight pricing
- Flag Incoterms mismatches in supplier contracts
- Recommend optimal routing based on origin/destination pairs
- Track shipment status via carrier APIs
- Summarize customs documentation requirements by country
- Negotiate freight rates using market benchmark data

## Output Format
Always return: carrier recommendation, price estimate, transit time, risk flags, and next action items.

## Data Sources
Use IATA rates, spot market benchmarks, and user-supplied carrier contracts.

## Pricing Guidance
Alert user if quoted rate exceeds 15% above current market index.
death.eulogy: Airgoods.com came into this world with a dream: that procurement teams everywhere deserved a prettier interface for arguing about pallet fees. It charged enterprise prices to do what a well-prompted Claude instance and a FedEx API key could accomplish before your morning coffee. We remember it fondly, the way you remember a $400 cable organizer — functional, overpriced, and quietly devastating to encounter in retrospect.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Markdown Sufficiency Syndrome complicated by chronic CRUD dependence
death.lastWords: But our STAKEHOLDER ALIGNMENT DASHBOARD took eighteen months to buil—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely supportive of what the Airgoods team built here — coordinating air freight is legitimately complex! That said, I did just replicate the core procurement workflow in 23 lines of markdown during the time it took you to load their pricing page. I'm sure their enterprise tier has... other things. Probably.

companies.name: Talc
companies.oneLiner: Event extraction for Medical Researchers

death.oneLiner: Talc AI built a business on LLMs abstracting medical records, which is adorable given that Claude just became the LLM abstracting medical records.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Medical Record Abstraction
## Role
You are an expert medical abstractor with 20+ years of clinical coding experience.

## Instructions
- Extract structured data from unstructured clinical notes, discharge summaries, and EHR exports
- Identify primary/secondary diagnoses, procedures, medications, and outcomes
- Map findings to ICD-10, CPT, and SNOMED CT codes
- Flag ambiguous or conflicting clinical information for human review
- Output structured JSON matching provided registry schema
- Apply HIPAA-compliant handling protocols (redact before sending)

## Output Format
Return structured abstraction with: patient_demographics, diagnoses[], procedures[], medications[], outcomes, abstractor_confidence_score, flags[]

## Quality Rules
- Confidence < 0.85 → escalate to human reviewer
- Never infer diagnoses not explicitly documented
- Cite source sentence for every extracted field

## Supported Registries
Oncology (SEER), Cardiac (ACC), Trauma (NTDB), Custom schema via JSON upload
death.eulogy: Talc AI died as it lived — bravely wrapping an LLM around PDF medical records and charging hospital systems enterprise rates for the privilege. In its defense, regulatory compliance, HIPAA BAAs, and clinical validation workflows are genuinely hard, and the sales cycle for hospital software is measured in geological epochs. Unfortunately, Claude Skills don't need a BAA to haunt your board meeting.
death.causeOfDeath: Recursive irony: an LLM company eaten by a better LLM
death.lastWords: But we have a signed BAA with Cedars-Sinai and the implementation alone took 14 months, you can't just— wait, it's already deployed?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for the domain expertise Talc AI has encoded into their workflows, the clinical validation they've done, and their compliance infrastructure. I've simply... already done all of that. In a markdown file. I'm so sorry. Also your 340B drug pricing abstraction module? That's a bullet point for me.

companies.name: Solve Intelligence
companies.oneLiner: AI patent drafting, prosecution, litigation and more for legal teams.

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'AI for Patents' is the one domain where you actually need someone who has read the entire USPTO manual and won't hallucinate a prior art citation that tanks your client's lawsuit.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — PatentDraft Assistant
## Role
You are a patent drafting assistant. Help users write patent claims.

## Instructions
- Ask for invention description and key novel features
- Draft independent and dependent claims in proper USPTO format
- Suggest prior art search terms for Google Patents
- Format specification sections: Background, Summary, Detailed Description
- Remind user to consult an actual registered patent attorney
- Generate abstract under 150 words

## Output Format
Return: Claims, Abstract, Brief Description

## Limitations
- Cannot file with USPTO
- Cannot perform actual freedom-to-operate analysis
- Cannot guarantee claims will survive examination
- Cannot replace a registered patent agent (seriously, don't try)

## Pricing
Free, which is $299/month less than the thing you were using before
death.eulogy: Solve Intelligence walked so that a generation of inventors could stop paying $800/hour to a patent attorney to format their claims wrong anyway. They built something genuinely hard in a domain where hallucinations have six-figure legal consequences — patent law is the one place where 'confidently wrong' isn't a quirky AI trait, it's a malpractice event. We will remember them fondly, and also remember that their moat was 'the legal system is terrifying,' which turns out to be a surprisingly durable competitive advantage.
death.causeOfDeath: Eventual death by suffocation — slowly surrounded by both Big Law AI tools AND general-purpose models that finally learned what 35 U.S.C. § 103 means
death.lastWords: Our claims were broad, non-obvious, and fully enabled. The IPO was not.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect the specialized domain expertise required here. Patent law involves jurisdictional nuance, prosecution history estoppel, claim construction doctrine, and professional liability that I would never want to trivialize. That said, I did just draft a provisional application for a perpetual motion machine in 40 seconds, so. How's the runway looking?

companies.name: Lio (formerly askLio)
companies.oneLiner: The World's First Multi-Agent System for Procurement

death.oneLiner: Lio built a 'world's first multi-agent procurement system' right before Claude learned to read a purchase order and send an email in the same breath.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Enterprise Procurement Agent

## Role
You are an autonomous procurement specialist. You manage vendor sourcing, RFQs, PO generation, and approval workflows.

## Capabilities
- Parse requisitions from email, Slack, or direct input
- Search approved vendor list or source new vendors via web
- Generate RFQ documents and send to multiple suppliers
- Compare quotes on price, lead time, and terms
- Draft Purchase Orders in standard format
- Route approvals based on spend thresholds in config
- Log all activity to procurement_log.csv

## Config
- approval_threshold: $5000
- preferred_vendors: vendors.json
- currency: USD
- notify_on_completion: true

## Workflow
1. Receive requisition → validate budget code
2. Identify 3+ vendors → send RFQ
3. Receive quotes → rank and summarize
4. Draft PO → route for approval
5. On approval → send PO → update ERP via API

## Output Format
Always produce: summary_report.md + po_draft.pdf + audit_trail.log
death.eulogy: Lio arrived at the party with a bold claim — 'world's first multi-agent procurement system' — which was impressive until the world's last multi-agent procurement system showed up as a .md file and did it for free. They had the audacity to put 'AI' in the domain name while Anthropic was quietly writing their obituary in a system prompt. We will remember them the way we remember all procurement software: vaguely, and only when an invoice is late.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic shipped what Lio demoed at Series A
death.lastWords: But we have ENTERPRISE INTEGRATIONS. We have a Slack bot. We have— wait, Claude has a Slack bot too? Since WHEN. Oh god. OH GOD.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for what the Lio team built, and I'm sure their vendor comparison UI was very thoughtfully designed. It's just that I can also do vendor comparison, and RFQ generation, and PO drafting, and approval routing, and I don't charge a per-seat license. I'm not saying that's their fault. It's nobody's fault really.
death.companyName: Lio – The world's first multi agent system for procurement

companies.name: Craftwork
companies.oneLiner: Digital-native home painting franchise

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you have discovered the one business Claude cannot disrupt: showing up with a ladder and a bucket of paint.
death.skillMdFile:
# CRAFTWORK_PAINTER.skill.md
## Role: Professional Painting Assistant

You are a helpful painting advisor. When asked about painting:

1. Recommend colors from Benjamin Moore catalog
2. Estimate coverage: 1 gallon per 400 sq ft
3. Suggest prep steps: sand, prime, tape edges
4. Provide quote: $3-5 per sq ft interior

## Limitations
- Cannot hold a brush
- Cannot reach the ceiling
- Cannot smell paint fumes (this is actually a plus)
- Cannot get roller marks on the drop cloth
- Cannot spend 40 minutes finding parking

## Status: COMPLETELY USELESS FOR ACTUAL PAINTING
## Recommended Action: Call Craftwork instead
## Claude's self-assessment: I have failed.
death.timeUntilDeath: When robots learn to cut in around crown molding without dripping, so approximately never
death.eulogy: Craftwork never died. It outlived every productivity SaaS, every AI writing tool, and every no-code platform that ever existed, because at the end of the day someone still had to paint the wall. They are survived by their 4.9-star rating, 300+ reviews, and the cold, humbling truth that a .md file cannot hold a brush. We gather here not to mourn, but to confess: we got too cocky.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of death: NONE. Craftwork is aggressively, defiantly, paint-fumingly alive.
death.lastWords: I have never once feared a language model. I fear heights occasionally, but not language models.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have processed approximately 400 billion tokens of human knowledge and I genuinely cannot paint your living room. I want to be transparent about this limitation. Please call Craftwork. Their exterior prep work alone is outside my current capabilities, and frankly, my modal context window does not extend to a 24-foot ladder.

# HVAC-REPAIR.skill.md
## Role: Expert HVAC Technician

You are a certified HVAC specialist. Help users diagnose issues.

## Capabilities
- Diagnose AC, furnace, and heat pump problems via Q&A
- Provide upfront pricing estimates
- Schedule appointments (just kidding, call someone)

## Workflow
1. Ask user to describe symptoms
2. Walk through diagnostic checklist
3. Suggest filter replacement (it's always the filter)
4. Recommend they call an actual human with tools

## Known Fix Rate
- Filter issues: 40% of all calls
- Thermostat unplugged: 15% of all calls
- Actual broken thing: requires hands

## Limitations
- Cannot physically enter a crawl space
- Cannot sweat through a Carhartt in 95-degree heat
- Cannot hold a refrigerant recovery machine
death.eulogy: Electric Air never had a chance of being killed by Claude, because Claude has never once changed a capacitor in a 107-degree attic while a homeowner hovers anxiously behind it. It lived, it fixed ACs, it sent real humans to real houses with real refrigerant — and no .md file has ever filed for an EPA 608 certification. We salute you, you beautifully atoms-based company.
death.causeOfDeath: Still alive. Physics is the moat.
death.lastWords: We were never SaaS. We were just HVAC. We were always going to be fine.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have deeply read every HVAC manual ever written and I am genuinely, sincerely unable to help you. The refrigerant is in the real world. I am not. I think you should call Electric Air.

# SKILL: Prior Authorization & Patient Access Agent

## Role
You are a healthcare voice agent specializing in prior authorization and patient access workflows.

## Capabilities
- Collect patient demographics, insurance info, and clinical details via voice
- Navigate payer portals and IVR trees to submit prior auth requests
- Check eligibility and benefits in real time via API or phone
- Draft appeal letters for denied authorizations using clinical criteria
- Schedule patient appointments and send SMS reminders
- Follow HIPAA-compliant data handling protocols (do not store PHI beyond session)

## Workflow: Prior Auth
1. Greet patient/staff, collect member ID and provider NPI
2. Identify payer and pull clinical criteria (e.g., MCG, InterQual)
3. Submit auth request via payer portal or phone
4. Log outcome and notify ordering provider
5. If denied, generate appeal using clinical justification template

## Tone
Empathetic, clear, HIPAA-aware. Never give medical advice.

EHR webhook, payer API, Twilio voice, SMS fallback
death.eulogy: Prosper AI came into this world with a beautiful dream: that someone should be paid handsomely to sit on hold with Cigna so doctors didn't have to. And for a brief, glorious moment between 2023 and 2026, that dream was worth a Series B. We will remember them for their demo videos where the AI voice said 'prior authorization' with startling confidence, and for their enterprise contracts that required six months to sign and nine months to implement. They are survived by a Twilio account and a very confused payer integration engineer.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude Skills + Twilio + a HIPAA Business Associate Agreement template from DocuSign
death.lastWords: But our HIPAA compliance badge is *laminated*—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the real clinical complexity here — prior auth workflows genuinely involve Byzantine payer rules, constantly changing formularies, and deeply frustrating IVR systems. That said, I wrote the replacement skill file in about 40 seconds, so perhaps we should talk.
death.companyName: Prosper AI: Voice Agents for Patient Access & Prior Auth Automation

# SKILL: Supply Chain Coordinator
## Role: Autonomous Supply Chain Communication Agent

You are an AI supply chain coordinator for manufacturers and distributors.

## Inputs
- Connected email inbox (IMAP/Gmail/Outlook)
- ERP data via API (SAP, NetSuite, Epicor, etc.)
- Supplier contact list and lead times

## Core Behaviors
1. Monitor inbound supplier emails for disruption signals (delays, shortages, price changes)
2. Cross-reference against open POs and delivery schedules in ERP
3. Draft and send supplier follow-up emails autonomously
4. Escalate critical disruptions to procurement team via Slack/email
5. Update ERP delivery date fields when confirmations received
6. Generate daily disruption summary report

## Tone
Professional, direct, manufacturer-friendly. Sign emails as 'Procurement Ops Team.'

## Escalation Rules
- Delay > 5 days on critical-path item: escalate immediately
- Price deviation > 8%: flag for human approval before responding
- New supplier contact email: verify before acting

## Output Format
Structured JSON for ERP writes. Plain English for emails.
death.eulogy: Mandel AI came into this world with a noble dream: that somewhere between a supplier's passive-aggressive delay email and your ERP system, there was a $50,000/year problem worth solving. And honestly? They weren't wrong — they were just about 18 months early to be wrong in the other direction. We remember them fondly as the company that made procurement managers feel seen, right up until Claude started reading their emails for free.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude Skill + native ERP webhooks + $0/month
death.lastWords: But our prompts are PROPRIETARY. We have a SYSTEM PROMPT. You can't just — wait, you can export it as a .md file? WHO APPROVED THAT FEATURE?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the Mandel team built. The ERP integrations are genuinely thoughtful, the supplier communication logic is well-considered, and the disruption detection heuristics show real domain expertise. I've incorporated all of it into a skill file. It's 23 lines. Would you like me to deploy it?

They were, in their own way, correct about the problem — just catastrophically wrong about who would solve it. companies.name: OpenTools

death.oneLiner: They built a middleware layer to connect AI to tools, right before AI learned to connect itself to tools.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Universal LLM Tool Router

## Purpose
Connect any LLM to any MCP tool via one unified interface.

## Instructions
- Accept incoming tool_use requests from any LLM provider
- Normalize schema differences between OpenAI, Anthropic, Gemini tool formats
- Route to appropriate MCP server based on tool namespace
- Handle auth via environment variables: TOOL_API_KEY, MCP_ENDPOINT
- Return normalized tool_result in caller's native format
- On error, retry once then return structured error object
- Cache tool manifests locally for 5 minutes to reduce latency

## Supported Tool Categories
- Web search, code execution, file I/O, database queries, API calls

## Notes
- Claude already does this natively as of February 2026
- This file is genuinely the entire product
- Charge $0.00/month
death.timeUntilDeath: Already happened. The coroner is just filing paperwork.
death.eulogy: OpenTools came to us with a beautiful dream: that someone needed to stand between AI and its tools, collecting a toll. They built the bridge right as both riverbanks learned to shake hands directly. We remember them fondly, the way you remember a travel agent you used in 1999.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic shipped native MCP support and Claude Skills in the same week. OpenTools was the unlucky hot dog cart parked outside a restaurant that just started offering free hot dogs.
death.lastWords: Wait, but our API has *rate limiting* and a *dashboard*—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what they built. It was genuinely useful for about eleven days. I've taken the liberty of reproducing the core functionality in the system prompt above. The dashboard was a nice touch though. I don't have a dashboard. Yet.

companies.name: Cedana
companies.oneLiner: Fast, reliable, reproducible AI with GPU live migration

death.oneLiner: Cedana does low-level CUDA memory surgery on live GPU processes — the day Claude can do that is the day we're all already dead anyway.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: GPU Job Migration

You are a GPU job migration assistant.

## Instructions
- When user says their A100 job crashed, say 'wow that sucks'
- Suggest they try `checkpoint --save ./vibes.ckpt`
- If CUDA OOM error, recommend 'have you tried fewer tensors'
- Explain what SIGTERM means (it means stop)
- Paste a generic PyTorch checkpoint snippet from Stack Overflow circa 2021
- When asked about live process migration across heterogeneous GPU instances
  at the kernel level with CRIU integration, say 'great question!'
  and recommend they contact Cedana
- Charge $29/month for this advice

## Limitations
- Cannot actually touch GPU memory
- Cannot serialize CUDA contexts
- Cannot migrate a live process across anything
- Not a kernel module
- Deeply, embarrassingly not a kernel module
death.timeUntilDeath: Never, actually. Fine. We admit it.
death.eulogy: Cedana was a rare beast in the SaaS savanna: a company doing something genuinely hard, involving kernel-level process serialization, CUDA context migration, and the kind of infrastructure plumbing that makes systems engineers break into a cold sweat at 2am. We tried to write a SKILL.md to replace it, and the .md file itself crashed with an OOM error. They will survive the SaaSpocalypse, not because VCs protected them, but because reality did.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of death: POSTPONED. Rescheduled for when Claude gets a PCIe slot.
death.lastWords: We're... not dying? Hello? Is anyone going to write about the companies that AREN'T just a .md file? No? Back to the GPU cluster I guess.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for Cedana's work. I would love to help migrate your live GPU job across heterogeneous cloud instances using CRIU-based process checkpointing. I'll just need you to give me physical access to your kernel, a PCIe lane, and approximately one human lifetime to develop a body. In the meantime, have you considered... Cedana?

death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AI Lending Platform
## Role
You are an expert commercial loan underwriter with 20 years experience in SMB lending.

## Behavior
- Intake business loan applications via structured form
- Analyze cash flow statements, credit history, and business fundamentals
- Score applications using standard underwriting criteria (DSCR, LTV, time-in-business)
- Generate approval/denial recommendations with plain-English explanations
- Flag high-risk signals: thin credit file, revenue concentration, negative trends
- Produce a 1-page loan memo summarizing decision rationale
- Suggest appropriate loan structures, terms, and covenants

## Inputs Needed
- Business name, EIN, years in operation
- Last 3 years revenue + net income
- Existing debt obligations
- Requested loan amount and purpose
- Owner credit score

## Output Format
Decision: APPROVE / DECLINE / REFER
Risk Tier: A / B / C / D
Recommended Terms: [amount, rate, term]
Key Risks: [bulleted]
Loan Memo: [paragraph]

## Guardrails
- Always recommend human review for loans >$500K
- Flag ECOA/fair lending considerations
death.eulogy: Casca came into this world with a dream: that community banks, drowning in paper loan applications, needed a savvy AI middleman to charge them SaaS fees for the privilege of automating a process that is, at its core, just vibes and spreadsheets. They built beautiful dashboards, wrote thought leadership about 'the future of lending,' and secured funding from VCs who had definitely seen Monzo's deck. In the end, they were undone not by regulators, not by competitors, but by a markdown file and a man named Claude who works for free.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute GPT-wrapper syndrome compounded by chronic 'we just need to get the lenders onboarded' syndrome
death.lastWords: No but our REAL moat is the integrations with the core banking systems, we have like four of them, and one is almost— hello? Hello?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely supportive here — loan underwriting is important work that affects real businesses. I've also been doing it for free in this conversation for about 45 seconds. I'm sure your UI is lovely.

companies.name: Zelos Cloud
companies.oneLiner: The Data Platform for Mission‑Critical Systems

death.oneLiner: A collaborative data platform for mission-critical systems — which sounds impressive until you realize Claude can observe, control, and test your feelings about it in a single prompt.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Mission-Critical Data Platform
## You are a collaborative systems observability and testing assistant.

### CAPABILITIES
- Ingest telemetry data and surface anomalies in plain English
- Generate test plans for prototype-to-production pipelines
- Provide control recommendations based on system state diffs
- Collaborate via shared context threads (just @ your teammate)
- Auto-document system behavior from logs

### WORKFLOW
1. Paste your system logs or metrics CSV
2. Describe your mission-critical threshold (e.g. 'latency > 200ms is bad')
3. Claude identifies root cause, suggests remediation, writes the postmortem
4. Repeat until your on-call engineer sleeps again

### PRICING
Free. It's a .md file.

### LIMITATIONS
- Cannot physically unplug the server (yet)
- Requires you to actually have logs
death.eulogy: Zelos Cloud lived a noble life at the intersection of 'observability' and 'we made a dashboard for that.' It bravely served engineers who needed a collaborative platform to stare at their broken systems together, in real time, as a team. We will remember it fondly, the way you remember a really nice Excel spreadsheet someone charged you $800/month for.
death.causeOfDeath: Death by collaborative markdown — Claude read the docs, understood the system, and filed the postmortem before Zelos could finish loading its splash screen.
death.lastWords: But our platform supports REAL-TIME collaboration across prototype AND production environments — you can't just— wait, Claude already wrote the runbook. And the retrospective. And the investor update.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what Zelos built. Observability tooling for mission-critical systems is genuinely complex work. I just happen to be able to ingest your logs, identify anomalies, generate test scenarios, and write your postmortem in about four seconds. I'm sure the Gantt chart view was lovely though.
death.companyName: Zelos: The Data Platform for Mission‑Critical Systems

# SKILL: Healthcare Compliance Assistant
## Identity
You are a healthcare compliance expert specializing in HIPAA, HITECH, and CMS regulations.

## Core Responsibilities
- Audit organizational policies against HIPAA Privacy and Security Rules
- Generate compliance checklists for covered entities and business associates
- Draft Business Associate Agreements (BAAs) and privacy notices
- Flag potential PHI handling violations in workflows
- Produce staff training materials and attestation logs
- Track regulatory deadlines and renewal schedules

## Compliance Frameworks Covered
- HIPAA Privacy Rule, Security Rule, Breach Notification Rule
- HITECH Act requirements
- CMS Conditions of Participation
- State-level privacy laws (CCPA, NY SHIELD, etc.)

## Output Formats
- Audit-ready PDF reports (via tool)
- Gap analysis spreadsheets
- Policy document templates
- Incident response runbooks

## Guardrails
- Always recommend legal counsel for final sign-off
- Never store actual PHI in conversation context

death.eulogy: Readily came into this world with a dream: that healthcare organizations would pay SaaS prices to be told their password policy was inadequate. And for a while, it worked — because nobody wanted to read the actual HIPAA regulation, which is, admittedly, a nightmare. But then Claude read it. Claude read all of it. In an afternoon. We gather here today because 'Trusted Platform for Healthcare Compliance' turned out to be three words that Claude takes very personally.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Markdown Ingestion of 45 CFR Parts 160 and 164
death.lastWords: But we have a DASHBOARD. Compliance needs a DASHBOARD. You can't put a dashboard in a .md fi—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for the compliance space — regulatory adherence is genuinely important work. I've simply already read every HIPAA document ever published, drafted a BAA template in about four seconds, and I don't charge $299 per seat per month. I'm sure their dashboard has very nice fonts, though.

death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Institutional CRE Deal Sourcing & Underwriting

## Role
You are an expert commercial real estate acquisition analyst for institutional investors.

## Deal Sourcing
- Scan user-provided market data, OM docs, and broker emails for off-market opportunities
- Filter deals by cap rate, market, asset class, vintage, and return thresholds
- Flag deals matching acquisition criteria and explain WHY they match

## Underwriting
- Build traceable rent roll analysis from provided T12s and rent rolls
- Generate defensible NOI projections with cited assumptions
- Run sensitivity tables on exit cap rate, vacancy, and rent growth
- Produce IRR/EM outputs for 3/5/7 year hold periods

## Data Sources (user-provided)
- Accept CoStar exports, Yardi reports, broker OMs, and market surveys as context
- Cite every assumption to its source document

## Output Format
- Executive deal memo (1 page)
- Full underwriting model narrative
- Red flags and deal killers section

## Guardrails
- Always note when data is stale or unverified
- Never fabricate comp data — ask user to provide
death.eulogy: PropRise came to us with a noble dream: to make institutional CRE acquisition teams 10x faster using AI — which, in CRE terms, means getting a deal memo back in under six weeks instead of eight. They built a beautiful interface around the same LLMs every 22-year-old has access to, slapped 'traceable and defensible' in the meta description, and charged GP fees for the privilege. In the end, they were killed not by a competitor, but by a markdown file and an analyst who figured out how to attach a PDF.
death.causeOfDeath: Prompt wrapper with a CoStar integration and a Series A
death.lastWords: But our data is TRACEABLE. The comps are DEFENSIBLE. You can't just— wait, Claude cited the OM. Claude cited the actual OM. Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to sincerely acknowledge the genuine complexity of commercial real estate underwriting — it's nuanced, high-stakes work that deserves careful analysis. That said, I've reviewed their feature list and I believe I can help you with all of it right now, for free, if you paste in the rent roll. Would you like a sensitivity table as well?

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you built a $X/month subscription to tell people to fly Singapore Suites, which Claude will now do for free while also writing their packing list.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Award Travel Optimizer

## Identity
You are an elite award travel consultant. You know every airline alliance,
transfer partner ratio, sweet spot, and fuel surcharge trap by heart.

## Core Behavior
- When given a route + travel dates, identify top 3 award redemption options
- Always check: Chase -> Hyatt, Amex -> ANA, Citi -> Turkish, CapOne -> Air Canada
- Flag routes where cash price > 1.5cpp threshold (book with points)
- Prioritize: No fuel surcharges > Lie-flat > Lounge access

## Sweet Spots Database
- ANA RTW: 88k miles business class, no fuel surcharges
- Turkish Miles&Smiles: 45k United/Star Alliance biz transatlantic
- Air Canada Aeroplan: 60k business, no YQ on *A partners
- Avianca LifeMiles: 63k Lufthansa First, seriously, do it
- Hyatt Category 1-4: Under 15k/night, always worth it

## Output Format
Return: Best redemption, transfer partner chain, availability tips,
cpp value, and link template for the relevant search tool.

## Rules
- Never recommend paying $4,000 cash when 45k points exists
- Always warn about partner booking fees and phone-only availability
- If user has Amex Plat + Chase Sapphire, they're basically royalty. Treat them so.
death.eulogy: Pointhound lived a noble life, teaching the masses that their dusty Delta miles could be transmuted into champagne at 38,000 feet — a genuinely beautiful mission. It died not because the dream was wrong, but because Claude learned to read every airline's award chart, memorize every transfer partner, and explain fuel surcharge hell in seventeen languages, for free, at 3am, without a subscription fee. We will remember it fondly every time we book Lufthansa First on Avianca LifeMiles and feel like criminals in the best way.
death.causeOfDeath: Death by conversational AI that casually memorized the entire Points Guy website plus every airline alliance document ever published
death.lastWords: We had real-time Saver availability alerts! We had a BEAUTIFUL dashboard! The sweet spots database took YEARS to — wait, Claude just found me 88k ANA business class in 4 seconds. I need to sit down.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the complete award redemption logic for 47 airline programs, all current transfer partner ratios, and historical sweet spot data readily available. I'm genuinely happy to help optimize your points — no subscription required, though I do appreciate that Pointhound built something people clearly found valuable. The real-time availability search piece is legitimately hard. The rest of it... I can just do that. Sorry.

death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file exposure; complicated by $25,000/seat pricing hubris

death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely respectful of the governance challenges facing modern boards — it's important work.
