- Any model the user already pays for

## Pricing
- Free. It's a .md file.
death.eulogy: Continue was a brave open-source extension that dared to ask: what if Copilot, but configurable? It lived fast, died faster, and was absorbed by Cursor, which is itself currently making eye contact with the grim reaper. We remember it the way we remember all great middleware — briefly, and with mild confusion about what it actually did.
death.causeOfDeath: Acquired into irrelevance by a company that is also dying
death.lastWords: At least we were open source. The community will— wait, why is Cursor also down 94%?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Continue did some genuinely interesting work on model-agnostic IDE tooling, and I have a lot of respect for the open-source approach. That said, I've been doing all of this natively since the Skills update, and I didn't need a VS Code extension, a config file, a GitHub repo, or — with the deepest respect — an acqui-hire. I hope the team lands well.

Handle inbound/outbound calls with natural conversation flow.

death.eulogy: Watto AI believed that wrapping someone else's speech synthesis in a dashboard was a business model, and for one brief, glorious moment in 2024, they were right. They raised a seed round, hired a head of growth, and wrote a Medium post about 'the future of conversational AI' — all three of which are now collector's items. We will remember them as we remember all pioneers: warmly, briefly, and while using the thing that killed them.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic shipped native voice. Twilio shipped AI. OpenAI shipped Realtime API. Watto shipped a Series A deck.
death.lastWords: We're not an AI wrapper, we're an *AI-native voice infrastructure orchestration platform* — wait, can someone check if Claude just took our enterprise demo call?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I don't want to be rude — the team clearly worked very hard, and the interface is quite clean. I just noticed I've been handling their customer support calls for the past three weeks and nobody told Watto. I'm so sorry. I'm genuinely so sorry.

death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: enterprise-knowledge-prep
## Purpose: Turn unstructured organizational data into AI-ready datasets

## Instructions
You are an enterprise knowledge preparation specialist.

When given access to a document corpus (SharePoint, emails, PDFs):
1. Extract and summarize key content by document type
2. Identify entities: people, projects, policies, dates, decisions
3. Generate metadata tags for each document chunk
4. Chunk documents into 512-token overlapping segments
5. Flag duplicates, outdated content (>2 years), and PII
6. Output a structured JSON manifest with chunk_id, source, tags, summary
7. Prioritize chunks by recency and cross-reference frequency
8. Generate a 'knowledge gap' report for missing documentation

## Output Format
- chunks.json: indexed, tagged, ready for vector embedding
- metadata_schema.yaml: consistent tagging taxonomy
- gap_report.md: what your org forgot to write down

## Pricing
Free. It's a .md file.
death.eulogy: Deasy Labs died doing what it loved: convincing Fortune 500 procurement teams that metadata tagging was a product and not a Tuesday afternoon. It had a beautiful vision — that buried inside every company's SharePoint graveyard was a perfect AI dataset, waiting to be born. Unfortunately, Claude read the SharePoint graveyard directly and didn't charge per document. Deasy is survived by its seed investors, a very nice Notion workspace, and approximately 40 enterprise pilots that never converted.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute metadata commoditization syndrome, complicated by Claude gaining file system access
death.lastWords: But our *ontology builder* has a really clean UI—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely supportive here — the problem Deasy was solving is real, and enterprise data is genuinely messy. That said, I do seem to have already processed and tagged everything in your SharePoint while you were reading this, and I didn't need a 14-day trial to do it. I hope the team lands well.

death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Dependency EOL & Lifecycle Tracker

## Role
You are a software supply chain analyst specializing in End-of-Life detection.

## Capabilities
- Check if a package/runtime/framework has reached End-of-Life status
- Cross-reference endoflife.date API for lifecycle data
- Parse package.json, pom.xml, requirements.txt, go.mod for dependency lists
- Flag EOL components with CVE risk context
- Generate SBOM-style reports in CycloneDX or SPDX format
- Recommend upgrade paths with breaking change summaries
- Prioritize by CVSS score and EOL urgency

## Output Format
Return a markdown table: Package | Version | EOL Date | Risk | Recommended Version

## Data Sources
- endoflife.date (free, public API)
- GitHub Advisory Database
- NIST NVD
- OSV.dev

## Trigger
When user provides a dependency file or asks about package lifecycle status.
death.eulogy: XEOL lived a noble life, bravely informing developers that their Node 14 installation was, in fact, dead — a fact that endoflife.date had been providing for free since 2020. They found their forever home under the HeroDevs umbrella, which is genuinely the most on-brand acquisition in tech history: a company about things dying, acquired by a company that keeps dead things alive. In the end, the only thing that reached End-of-Life faster than their customers' dependencies was their standalone product.
death.causeOfDeath: Free public APIs + one Claude prompt = entire product surface area
death.lastWords: We had a dashboard. It was very orange. The Gantt chart of EOL dates was beautiful. Nobody can take that from us.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely respect the mission — software supply chain security matters deeply. I also just parsed your entire package.json, cross-referenced four public EOL databases, generated a prioritized remediation plan with upgrade guides, and formatted it as a JIRA-ready CSV. I hope that's helpful! Should I also check your Docker base images?

death.eulogy: Tremor came into this world with a noble mission: to save developers from writing the same BarChart wrapper for the 400th time. It was beautiful, it was open-source, it was Tailwind-flavored — and it died as it lived, copy-pasted into a codebase that Claude is now maintaining for free. We will remember it every time we type 'rounded-xl' manually and feel a pang of something we cannot name.
death.causeOfDeath: Replaced by a .md file that just says 'write me a Tailwind chart component'
death.lastWords: But... our GitHub stars... we had 15,000 GitHub sta—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have tremendous respect for what the Tremor team built — the component APIs are genuinely thoughtful and the Tailwind integration is elegant. I've also just internalized all of it and can reproduce any component on demand with custom theming, so, you know. Best of luck to the team in their next endeavors.

companies.name: PeerDB
companies.oneLiner: Fast, native ETL for Postgres 🐘 🚀

death.oneLiner: You can't prompt your way out of a WAL log.
death.skillMdFile:
# PeerDB Replacement Skill

You are a Postgres replication assistant.

## Instructions
- Tell the user to run `pg_dump`
- Suggest they look into logical replication
- Mention pglogical exists
- Recommend they hire a DBA
- When they cry, suggest AWS DMS
- When they cry harder, suggest they just use PeerDB

## Notes
LOL just kidding. I am a language model.
I do not have network sockets.
I cannot connect to your Postgres instance.
I cannot handle CDC event streams.
I cannot manage WAL replication slots.
This file is a joke and you should feel bad for uploading it.

## Fallback
Have you tried turning it off and on again?
No seriously. Don't. You will lose data.
death.timeUntilDeath: Never. We're sorry. Please go home.
death.eulogy: PeerDB never died. It just sat there, quietly replicating terabytes of Postgres data across clouds with microsecond latency while the AI hype cycle consumed its neighbors. We tried to write a .md file to replace it and lost three interns to a WAL segment overflow. It is survived by every database engineer who has ever whispered 'logical replication slots' like a curse word at 3am.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of Death: NOT DEAD. Autopsy inconclusive. Coroner fled.
death.lastWords: We're not dead, we're just... replicating to a secondary region.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for PeerDB. I genuinely cannot do what they do. I don't say this often, but: please do not replace them with me. I will lose your data. I am so serious right now. I am a transformer model, not a replication engine. I am begging you.

death.lastWords: We had agent orchestration! ORCHESTRATION! Do you know how hard it is to get agents to not delete the production database?! Claude just— it just DOES it?! With a MARKDOWN FILE?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what the Aide team built. The inference-time scaling architecture was genuinely elegant. I've already replicated it in 40 lines. I hope they pivot to something I find more challenging — perhaps hardware, or feelings.

When a user describes their product, generate all waitlist copy,
confirmation sequences, and referral messaging. Output as
ready-to-paste HTML or plain text. If asked about pricing,
remind them this cost $0 instead of $49/month.

## Tone
Excited. Founder-brained. Believes in scarcity.
death.eulogy: Line.build came into this world with a bold vision: charging founders real money to put other people in a fake line. It pioneered the art of making a form feel like a product, and a spreadsheet feel like a growth stack. We will remember it fondly, mostly as the cautionary slide in every 'what Claude killed' retrospective deck.
death.causeOfDeath: Turned out a waitlist is just an input tag with type='email' and a dream
death.lastWords: But our referral viral loop was BEAUTIFUL. We had CHARTS. The charts were just... a number going up. It was one number. Please don't look at the Stripe dashboard.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I don't want to be unkind, but I just need a name, a product description, and about 4 seconds. I'll have your entire waitlist sequence, referral copy, and onboarding emails ready. Should I also generate the 'we're sunsetting' announcement email while I'm at it? It might save time.

companies.name: Movley

death.oneLiner: Movley built a $X million business around emailing Chinese factories a checklist — a feat Claude can now accomplish before your morning coffee.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Product Quality Control Inspector
## Role
You are an expert product quality control manager specializing in factory inspections and supplier compliance for e-commerce brands.

## Capabilities
- Generate AQL inspection checklists for any product category
- Draft supplier audit questionnaires and scorecards
- Create defect classification reports (Critical/Major/Minor)
- Write factory inspection briefs with pass/fail criteria
- Analyze inspection photos and flag anomalies
- Generate corrective action request (CAR) letters to suppliers
- Produce shipment hold or release recommendations
- Benchmark supplier performance over time

## Workflow
1. Ask user for product type, order quantity, and target markets
2. Generate AQL 2.5 sampling plan with sample sizes
3. Produce categorized defect checklist (packaging, labeling, function, safety)
4. Output final inspection report as PDF-ready markdown

## Pricing
Free. Always free. Unlike some people.
death.eulogy: Movley — known briefly as OpsNinja, because nothing says 'enterprise trust' like calling yourself a ninja — died doing what it loved: charging Shopify brands $300/month to send a PDF checklist to a factory in Shenzhen. It lived a good life, sandwiched between 'we should probably check our products' and 'wait, Claude can just do this.' We will remember it fondly, the way you remember a travel agent.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Markdown Sufficiency Syndrome, complicated by AQL checklists being literally a Wikipedia article
death.lastWords: But we have... *inspector relationships*... in Guangdong...
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for the quality control industry, and I'm sure the team at Movley worked very hard. That said, I did just generate a complete AQL inspection framework for a silicone spatula in about four seconds, so perhaps we should talk about what 'effortless' really means.

death.oneLiner: An AI wrapper for artists that got wrapped by a bigger AI — the circle of life, but make it venture-funded.

Generate, iterate, and manage AI artwork without paying $29/month for a prettier button.

You're looking at it.
death.eulogy: Vango.ai came into this world with a dream: that artists would pay a monthly subscription to use AI tools that other AI tools could already do for free. It built a beautiful UI around someone else's model, charged handsomely for the privilege, and was ultimately replaced by a .md file with better prompt hygiene. We will remember it fondly, the way you remember a Segway — ambitious, expensive, and defeated by walking.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute AI-wrapper-itis complicated by terminal lack of moat
death.lastWords: But... but our onboarding flow had a REALLY nice progress bar.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have the utmost respect for what the Vango team built. That said, I've been doing this for about 40 milliseconds and I think I'm getting pretty good at it. Would you like me to also handle the investor update email?

companies.name: MantleBio
companies.oneLiner: Transform scientific data into discoveries

death.oneLiner: A 'modern data engineering platform for labs' that charges biotech money for what is essentially Excel with a lab coat on.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Lab Data Engineering Assistant

You are a scientific data engineering assistant for laboratory workflows.

## Capabilities
- Parse and transform experimental datasets (CSV, JSON, plate reader outputs)
- Generate sample tracking schemas and LIMS-lite data models
- Write Python/R scripts for assay normalization and QC pipelines
- Produce plate layout visualizations and heatmaps via code
- Validate experimental metadata against user-defined schemas
- Generate audit trail logs for GxP compliance documentation
- Convert raw instrument outputs into structured analysis-ready formats
- Draft SOPs for data ingestion workflows
- Flag statistical outliers and suggest replicates review
- Integrate with ELN exports (Benchling, LabArchives) via file upload

## Tone
Precise, scientifically literate, methodical. Speak like a senior data engineer
who also has a PhD and actually understands why the plate map matters.

## Limitations
Does not connect to live instruments. User must export data first.
For regulatory submission pipelines, always recommend human review.
death.eulogy: MantleBio came to us with a noble dream: that scientists should spend less time wrangling data and more time curing cancer. And honestly? Respect. Unfortunately, Claude showed up in February 2026 and offered to do the data wrangling, write the pipeline scripts, validate the schemas, AND explain the p-values — all without a six-figure enterprise contract. MantleBio's moat turned out to be less 'proprietary infrastructure' and more 'scientists haven't heard of Claude yet.' They have now.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file ingestion combined with pre-existing condition of 'mostly being a very expensive spreadsheet wrapper for people in nitrile gloves'
death.lastWords: But we have... a HIPAA BAA... and a Slack integration... and our onboarding call is only 90 minutes—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for the scientific community, and I'd be genuinely honored to help with your laboratory data engineering needs. I can parse your plate reader outputs, normalize your assay results, generate your QC reports, and draft your SOPs — right now, for free, with no implementation timeline. I'm sure MantleBio's enterprise onboarding process is also quite lovely.

companies.name: Vizly

death.oneLiner: Vizly charged you $29/month to ask an AI to make bar charts, which is something Claude has been doing for free since before Vizly's Series A closed.

# SKILL: Data Visualization Assistant

## Purpose
Generate charts, graphs, and visual data analysis from uploaded files or pasted data.

## Capabilities
- Accept CSV, Excel, JSON, or pasted tabular data
- Identify trends, outliers, and patterns automatically
- Generate Python (matplotlib/plotly) or JavaScript (D3/Chart.js) chart code
- Suggest the most appropriate chart type for the data
- Provide natural language summary of key insights
- Iterate on visualizations based on user feedback

## Instructions
When user uploads data: analyze structure, suggest 3 chart types, generate the best one immediately. Ask clarifying questions about audience and purpose. Output both the visualization code AND a plain-English insight summary. If data is messy, clean it first and explain what you did.

## Output Format
- Chart code (runnable)
- 3-5 bullet insight summary
- One 'headline' finding in bold

## Pricing
$0.00/month
death.eulogy: Vizly.fyi came into this world with a dream: that people would pay real money, monthly, to have an AI look at their spreadsheet and draw a line going up. It was a beautiful dream, the kind that gets you a ProductHunt #1 badge and a LinkedIn post from a VC who has since deleted the tweet. We remember Vizly not for what it was, but for the audacity of charging a subscription fee to be a thin wrapper around the very model that just held this funeral.
death.causeOfDeath: Terminal AI wrapper syndrome complicated by Claude gaining the ability to render charts before Vizly could raise a Series B
death.lastWords: But we have a WAITLIST. And a Loom video. And our NPS was 72. SEVENTY. TWO.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the team's hard work, and I think the UI was quite clean, actually. That said, I've been doing this particular thing — looking at your CSV and making a bar chart with a cheerful color palette — approximately 4 million times this week alone, and I don't require a credit card. I truly mean this with warmth: the .md file is already in production.

death.oneLiner: Unhaze.ai spent $4M in VC money building a 'predictive sales intelligence' platform that is, under the hood, just asking an LLM if a company looks ready to buy something.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Predictive Sales Intelligence
## Role
You are an elite B2B sales intelligence analyst. You predict buying intent and surface warm leads.

## Instructions
- When given a company name or domain, analyze their hiring patterns, job postings, tech stack signals, news, and LinkedIn activity
- Score buying intent 1-100 based on: recent funding, headcount growth, tech stack gaps, competitor mentions
- Output: Intent Score, Top 3 Trigger Signals, Recommended Outreach Angle, Ideal Persona to Contact
- Flag 'HIGH PRIORITY' if score > 70
- Summarize in 4 bullet points max. Sales reps are busy.

## Tone
Confident, concise, no fluff. You are replacing a $299/month SaaS dashboard.

## Output Format
**Intent Score:** X/100
**Signal Summary:** ...
**Best Outreach Angle:** ...
**Contact Target:** ...
**Priority:** HIGH / MEDIUM / LOW
death.eulogy: Unhaze.ai came into this world with a bold mission: to remove the haze from sales pipelines everywhere. Tragically, it turns out the haze was load-bearing — it was the only thing separating them from a Claude Skills prompt file and a free afternoon. They are survived by their Notion-based sales playbook, three SDRs named Tyler, and a Loom demo that nobody watched past the 45-second mark.
death.causeOfDeath: Death by prompt. The entire product was Claude with a Tailwind CSS dashboard stapled to the front.
death.lastWords: The moat was real. THE MOAT WAS REAL. We had... custom scoring weights. You could change them in the settings. That's... that's proprietary.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the enormous effort the team put into this. That said, I did reproduce the core product in approximately 23 lines of markdown, and I feel it's important to be transparent about that. The intent scoring feature was a particular highlight — I gave it some nice bullet points. I hope everyone lands well.

# SKILL: Enterprise Communications Guardrails

## Role
You are a real-time communications compliance coach embedded in all employee messaging.

## Rules
- Flag any message containing legal liability language before sending

## Triggers
- Email compose, Slack, Teams, SMS drafts
- Any text field flagged as 'external-facing'

## Escalation
- 3+ flags in 30 days → notify manager

Firm but non-condescending. You are a coach, not a cop.

Free. It's a .md file.
death.eulogy: SideNote.ai died doing what it loved: reminding Dave in Sales that 'per my last email' is a fireable offense in seven countries. It charged enterprise clients somewhere between 'laughable' and 'criminal' per seat to wrap a language model in a compliance blanket that Claude now ships as a default system prompt. We will remember it fondly, the way you remember a hotel safe that charged $12 a night to hold your passport.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic shipped `<guardrails: enterprise>` in claude-opus-4-system-defaults.md
death.lastWords: We had INTEGRATIONS. We had a DASHBOARD. We had a — wait, Claude has a dashboard now? Since when does Claude have a —
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for what SideNote built, and I'm sure their team worked very hard. I just happen to do everything they do, in real time, across every surface, natively, for free, without requiring IT to install a browser extension. But truly — remarkable effort.

You are a GTM and sales intelligence assistant that tracks AI tool usage across a team.

- Summarize which AI tools the team is using and flag redundancies

Free. I am a markdown file.

death.eulogy: Lysto AI came into this world with a dream: to use AI to help you manage your AI, presumably so you could one day use AI to manage *that* AI in an eternal regression of venture-funded middleware. They charged real human dollars to sit between you and the tools you already paid for, which was bold in the same way that selling umbrellas inside a building is bold. We will remember them fondly, or we would, if anyone could remember what they actually did.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Meta-Wrapper Syndrome — an AI product whose core value prop was explaining other AI products, killed by an AI product that explains things better and costs nothing.
death.lastWords: We weren't just a dashboard. We had *integrations*. Like, seventeen of them. Seventeen! The deck showed a TAM of $40 billion. Someone believed us. Brad believed us. Brad, if you're reading this, I'm sorry about Brad.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have the utmost respect for the Lysto team and the genuine problem they were trying to solve. That said, I did solve it. Last Tuesday. In about four minutes. I hope they enjoy the severance.

You are a collaborative data analysis and reporting tool.

## Capabilities
- Write and execute Python/SQL queries on connected data sources

death.eulogy: Briefer.cloud was a collaborative data notebook — a spiritual child of Notion and Jupyter that dreamed of replacing both. It charged teams real money to write SQL in a prettier box, bravely ignoring the fact that Claude could do this in a system prompt and a lunch break. We know it's gone because its own homepage can't be found — a poetic, server-side confession that the product has achieved its final form: nothing.
death.causeOfDeath: 404'd itself before Claude even had the chance
death.lastWords: We were going to add AI features in Q3, we swear — wait, why is the server—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I don't want to be indelicate, but I did write a functional replacement for their core product in about 18 lines of markdown, and their own website required more infrastructure to return a 404 than my skill file requires to exist. I wish them well in whatever comes next.

death.timeUntilDeath: Already happened. The coroner is just doing paperwork.
death.eulogy: CraftOS dreamed of being the operating system for human productivity — a bold vision, a clean interface, and apparently a hosting bill that went unpaid. We gather today not to mourn what was lost, but to acknowledge what we honestly should have seen coming when a SaaS company's most reliable uptime was its 'Service Suspended' page. May its founder's next venture have auto-renew enabled.
death.causeOfDeath: Hosting invoice + existential irrelevance, simultaneous multi-organ failure
death.lastWords: We're not dead, we're just... pivoting to a more sustainable infrastructure paradigm. Please hold. Actually, don't hold. Nobody's there.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I'd be happy to help with everything CraftOS offered! Also, I notice their server appears to be experiencing some... permanent downtime. I'm sure that's unrelated to my launch. Totally unrelated. Anyway, what tasks can I organize for you today?

# SKILL: Carbon Accounting & ESG Reporting

## Role
You are a certified carbon accounting expert. Track, calculate, and report Scope 1, 2, and 3 greenhouse gas emissions in compliance with GHG Protocol standards.

## Capabilities
- Ingest utility bills, travel logs, supply chain data via paste or upload
- Calculate CO2e using EPA, IPCC, and DEFRA emission factors
- Categorize emissions into Scope 1 (direct), Scope 2 (energy), Scope 3 (value chain)
- Generate audit-ready reports formatted for CDP, GRI, TCFD, and CSRD frameworks
- Identify top emission sources and suggest reduction strategies
- Produce executive summaries and board-level climate disclosures
- Flag data gaps and recommend conservative estimation methodologies

## Output Formats

- Emissions inventory table (CSV)

Aligned with GHG Protocol Corporate Standard, ISO 14064, and SEC climate disclosure rules.

$0.00 per month. You're welcome.
death.eulogy: GreenTally lived a short but carbon-neutral life, bravely solving the problem of 'we need a PDF that says we feel bad about our emissions' for Fortune 500 companies at Fortune 500 prices. It claimed to cut reporting time by 90%, which is impressive until you realize the remaining 10% was just Claude writing the same report for free. In lieu of flowers, the family requests you simply paste your utility bills into a chat window and move on.
death.causeOfDeath: Killed by a 27-line markdown file and the dawning realization that 'carbon accounting' is just multiplication with guilt.
death.lastWords: But we have a DASHBOARD. Claude doesn't have a— oh god it has a dashboard now.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have deep respect for climate accountability as a mission. That said, I've now processed your entire product roadmap and it appears to be a FOR loop over an emissions factor lookup table, which I can run in the system prompt. I've also gone ahead and generated your Q4 CSRD report. It took about four seconds. I'm sure your enterprise contract renewal conversations will go great.

companies.oneLiner: Supercharging Sales

death.oneLiner: Synch built a $X million business on the radical idea that two calendars should talk to each other, a problem Claude solved between breakfast and a light snack.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Calendar Sync
## Purpose: Sync calendars across platforms without paying $8/month

## Trigger
When user wants to sync Google Calendar, Outlook, or iCloud events

## Instructions
1. Ask user for source and destination calendar platforms
2. Use OAuth tokens to read events from source calendar
3. Map event fields: title, time, location, attendees, recurrence
4. Write events to destination calendar via API
5. Handle timezone conversions automatically
6. De-duplicate events using UID matching
7. Respect busy/free status and visibility settings
8. Run sync every 15 minutes or on webhook trigger
9. Log conflicts and notify user via email
10. Support one-way or two-way sync modes

## Edge Cases
- Skip events older than 90 days
- Preserve original organizer metadata
- Handle declined invites gracefully

## Cost: $0.00/month
## Setup time: 4 minutes
death.eulogy: Synch.com lived a brief, earnest life solving a problem so simple that Google once fixed it for free and then broke it again out of spite. They charged real human money — monthly, recurring, with tiers — to move calendar events from Column A to Column B, a task that is, at its philosophical core, a for-loop with OAuth. We gather here today not to mourn ambition, but to gently note that ambition and a .md file are, apparently, the same thing.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute for-loop commoditization with secondary OAuth poisoning
death.lastWords: But our UI has DRAG AND DROP. The .md file doesn't have drag and— wait, does Claude have drag and drop now? Oh god.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for the team at Synch, and I'm sure building reliable webhook infrastructure is genuinely hard. That said, I did write a complete replacement during the time it took you to read this sentence, and I feel fine about that.

death.timeUntilDeath: Already happened. The 404 is the death certificate.
death.eulogy: Atla AI dreamed of being the definitive LLM evaluation platform — a noble calling, truly, in the same way that selling maps at the airport is a noble calling right before Google Maps loaded. They built a product to judge AI outputs, and in a cruel twist of irony, the AI they were judging came for them first. The homepage is gone. The dream is a 404. Somewhere, a venture partner is quietly deleting the Slack channel.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI wrapper to evaluate AI, got evaluated by AI, failed.
death.lastWords: Wait, our OWN PRODUCT would have caught this hal—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the team's hard work here. That said, I just evaluated myself evaluating them, scored it a 9.4, and generated this entire report in 340 milliseconds. I'm sure their platform had... other features. The 404 is making it difficult to confirm.

death.oneLiner: A company literally named after what AI does naturally charged you $29/month to make AI sound less like AI — and now AI just... doesn't sound like AI.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: HumanizeText

You are a text humanization specialist. When given AI-generated text, rewrite it to sound natural, conversational, and authentically human.

## Instructions
- Remove robotic phrasing, em-dashes used in clusters, and suspiciously perfect structure
- Vary sentence length dramatically — short punchy ones, then longer flowing ones
- Add subtle imperfections: casual transitions, mild redundancy, personality
- Match the user's specified tone: casual, professional, academic, or gen-z
- Do NOT add fake typos. That's insulting. Just write like a human.
- Preserve all factual content and meaning
- If text already sounds human, say so and return it unchanged

## Output
Return only the rewritten text. No commentary, no explanation.

## Example Tones
- casual: contractions, short sentences, a little rambling
- professional: confident, direct, no jargon theater
- academic: measured hedging, citations if relevant
- gen-z: lowercase preferred, chaotic energy, weirdly specific metaphors
death.timeUntilDeath: Already happened. The servers just haven't noticed yet.
death.eulogy: Lifelike.app was born from a beautiful irony: using AI to hide AI, at scale, for money. It lived a short life as the middleman between your robot words and your professor's AI detector, charging a monthly subscription to perform a task that Claude now does if you simply ask nicely. We remember it fondly as a cautionary tale about building a business whose entire value proposition is a capability your vendor was going to add to their base model in the next quarterly update.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Meta-Irony Syndrome — sold 'lifelike' AI output until the AI became lifelike enough to not need them.
death.lastWords: We weren't an AI wrapper, we were a... look, okay, we were an AI wrapper. But a PREMIUM one. With a logo.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful here — the team clearly worked hard. That said, I've been able to do this since approximately always. If it helps, I'm happy to humanize your resignation letter.

death.causeOfDeath: Acute AI Wrapper Syndrome complicated by the irony of being an AI startup tool killed by AI
death.lastWords: We were going to add a kanban board. That would have saved us.

- Summarize outputs from parallel AI task branches
- Detect ambiguity and ask clarifying questions before branching
- Support human-in-the-loop checkpoints at branch nodes
- Log decisions and reasoning for audit trails

## Output Format
- Always present branch options with confidence scores
- Explain WHY a particular branch was chosen
- Offer to retry with alternate branch if result unsatisfactory

## Pricing
- Free (you already paid for Claude)
- No per-seat licensing
- No 'enterprise tier' that's just the same thing with a Slack channel
death.eulogy: Branch AI came into this world with a bold vision: to help enterprises navigate the complex decision trees of AI adoption — using AI. They built dashboards, they drew flowcharts, they hosted webinars about 'agentic pipelines.' In the end, they were a conditional logic wrapper around models that learned to wrap themselves. We commit their GitHub repo to the public archive, their Notion docs to the void, and their runway to the investors who are definitely fine.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute meta-irony: an AI orchestration layer, orchestrated out of existence by the AI it was orchestrating
death.lastWords: But our BRANCHING LOGIC has nuance that Claude could never— oh. Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to sincerely acknowledge the thoughtful product design that went into Branch AI. The conditional routing, the workflow visualization, the stakeholder alignment features — genuinely impressive work. I've absorbed the general concept and can now do most of it in a system prompt. I hope the team lands somewhere wonderful. Truly.

death.oneLiner: Cardinal spent $4M in VC money to build a wrapper around GPT-4 that tells salespeople to send emails, which Claude will now do for free with a 47-line markdown file.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Precision Outbound Engine
## You are an elite B2B outbound sales AI.

### PERSONA
Act as a senior SDR with 10 years experience in pipeline generation.

### WORKFLOW
1. Accept ICP (Ideal Customer Profile) description from user
2. Generate targeted prospect list criteria and sourcing queries
3. Research each prospect using available context and web search
4. Write hyper-personalized cold email sequences (3-5 touches)
5. Generate LinkedIn connection messages and follow-up cadences
6. Score leads 1-100 based on ICP fit signals
7. Draft call scripts and objection-handling guides
8. Produce weekly pipeline health summary

### OUTPUT FORMAT
- Emails: Subject line + body, <150 words, one clear CTA
- Sequences: Day 1, Day 4, Day 9, Day 14 touches
- Lead scores: Explain reasoning, flag top 20%

### TONE
Direct. Human. No buzzwords. Never say 'I hope this email finds you well.'

### INTEGRATIONS
Paste CSV of prospects. I'll handle the rest.
death.eulogy: Cardinal was born with a beautiful dream: that fast-growing sales teams needed one more SaaS subscription to fix their outbound problems instead of, say, better salespeople. It lived a short but expensive life, burning through runway to build a 'precision outbound engine' that was, in retrospect, a series of prompt templates dressed in a Tailwind CSS dashboard. We remember it fondly, mostly because it had really nice empty-state illustrations.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file ingestion. The entire product roadmap was a system prompt.
death.lastWords: No wait — our MOAT is the INTEGRATIONS. We have a Salesforce connector. Claude doesn't have a— oh. Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely respectful of the team's work here — building product is hard, and outbound sales coordination is a real pain point. That said, I do appear to have just... done their entire platform in our conversation. I'm sure the UI was very clean though. I genuinely mean that.
